claim: Google does not publish CrUX's exact eligibility threshold; the official methodology only states a URL/origin needs to be publicly discoverable (HTTP 200, not noindex) and have 'a sufficient number of distinct visitors' / enough samples to be statistically significant. The exact minimum monthly visit/pageview count is undisclosed. Any specific number our audit cites must be flagged as an estimate, not a Google-stated fact.
notes: Foundational caveat: the audit methodology must state the threshold is undisclosed/estimated. Also: origins/pages with >20% of traffic in ineligible dimension combinations are dropped entirely.

claim: The best-available third-party estimate is that URL-level CrUX field data appears once a page reaches roughly ~1,000 monthly views per device type (DebugBear), with a lower-bound estimate around ~400 pageviews/month per device for an origin-level summary (Erwin Hofman's testing). Because these are per-device-type (mobile vs desktop counted separately), a single-location shop's mobile traffic must independently clear the bar. Cite both as estimates.
notes: Use ~400/mo/device as the 'origin summary may appear' floor and ~1,000/mo/device as the 'URL-level data appears' line. Both are practitioner estimates, not Google figures.

claim: CrUX has two-level availability with origin fallback: when a specific URL lacks enough traffic, PageSpeed Insights falls back to origin-level (whole-site) field data; only when origin-level data is also insufficient does PSI show 'No Data'. A page with only 3 views/month gets no URL-level data even though another page on the same domain with ~1,000 views does. Our audit should record three states per URL: URL-level field data, origin-level only, or no field data.
notes: PSI fallback chain: URL field data -> origin field data -> No Data. Lab/Lighthouse always runs regardless.

claim: Typical low-traffic single-location Calgary shop sites (barber, cafe, spa, trades, dental, etc.) frequently fall BELOW the URL-level threshold, so they have only origin-level field data or none at all. This is the expected default, not an anomaly: 'low-traffic pages often lack URL-level data' and small sites commonly show the CrUX 'no data' / 'not enough usage data' message. The audit should treat missing field data as the norm for this customer segment.
notes: CrUX covers ~16M+ origins total (DebugBear) — a small fraction of all sites — and inclusion skews to higher-traffic sites, so most single-location shops sit below the bar. Exact Calgary-shop fraction is undisclosed; frame as 'most.'

claim: Lighthouse/lab data CANNOT measure INP at all because INP requires real user interactions at the moment users actually choose to interact; lab tests only passively load the page. Therefore, for a site with no field data our audit cannot truly grade INP — it can only use TBT (Total Blocking Time) as a lab proxy and must label INP as 'lab-estimated / not measurable in lab', never report a hard INP pass/fail from Lighthouse alone.
notes: web.dev: lab 'cannot accurately predict when users will choose to interact... and thus cannot accurately measure' INP. TBT is the lab proxy. PSI itself drops INP from assessment when there isn't enough field INP data.

claim: Lab and field LCP diverge sharply in BOTH directions for the same page, so a lab LCP must not be reported as the user's real LCP. Documented PSI example: lab LCP 8.2s vs field LCP 1.9s on the same page (lab far worse due to throttling + cold cache); web.dev also notes optimized-cache sites can show field LCP 'much faster' than lab. The audit must not fail a page on lab LCP alone when no field data exists.
notes: Same-page example also gave CLS lab 0.04 vs field 0.12 (field WORSE) — divergence is bidirectional and metric-dependent.

claim: Lab LCP is systematically pessimistic relative to typical users because Lighthouse deliberately throttles to ~1.6 Mbps / 150ms latency with 4x CPU throttling and always tests cold-cache first-load — DebugBear frames lab as showing 'the slowest 5%-10%' experience. For grading, our methodology should treat lab metrics as a conservative worst-case floor, not the median, when reconciling with absent field data.
notes: Implication: a passing lab score is strong evidence of a healthy page; a failing lab score may overstate the real-user problem. Asymmetric confidence.

claim: Lab CLS is structurally incomplete vs field CLS: Lighthouse only captures layout shifts that occur above-the-fold during initial load, whereas field CLS accumulates ALL unexpected shifts across the full page lifespan (lazy-loaded images during scroll, late ads, personalized content). So a clean lab CLS can coexist with a 'Poor' field CLS. The audit should grade lab CLS as 'load-time only' and not claim full-lifecycle CLS compliance from lab.
notes: For static Astro shop sites with reserved image dimensions, lab CLS is usually a good signal — but the caveat must be documented for sites with lazy media/ads.

claim: Google's official guidance is that when field data is present it should be trusted over lab data, and when field data is absent Lighthouse remains the recommended tool to approximate performance and surface optimization opportunities. The defensible audit methodology is therefore: prefer field (URL > origin) when available; when absent, grade on lab while explicitly labeling it 'lab-only / no real-user data' and reporting LCP/CLS as diagnostics plus TBT as the INP proxy.
notes: Concrete rubric for packages/audit: (1) field-URL pass/fail = authoritative; (2) field-origin = directional only (whole-site avg, not this page); (3) no field = lab grade with disclaimer, INP shown as TBT-derived estimate, never a hard INP fail.

claim: CrUX field data is a 28-day rolling aggregate reported at the 75th percentile (p75): a metric 'passes' when at least 75% of real-user loads are in the 'good' range (LCP <2.5s, INP <200ms, CLS <0.1). Because the audit's lab run is a single synthetic sample, it is not a p75 of real users and cannot be presented as a CrUX pass/fail — the audit must distinguish 'p75 field pass' from 'single lab sample within target.'
notes: Targets (LCP<2.5/INP<200/CLS<0.1) are the p75 'good' boundaries — already settled in repo research; the new point is the p75/28-day framing that lab cannot replicate.

claim: On mobile, LCP is the Core Web Vitals metric local-shop sites fail most — only ~59% of mobile pages have 'good' LCP vs ~72% on desktop — so the dominant mobile perf failure is a slow-loading hero/largest element, not interactivity. Mobile INP (~74% good) and mobile CLS (~79% good) pass far more often, so perf fixes should prioritize LCP first.
notes: Mobile vs desktop split: LCP 59% vs 72% good; INP 74% vs 97%; CLS 79% vs 72%. Implication for our engine: a fast static hero image is the single highest-leverage perf win. Our static-Astro output already targets this.

claim: For pages with POOR LCP, resource load delay (browser is slow to discover/start the hero request) is the second-largest LCP subpart after TTFB (≈1,290ms at p75 vs TTFB 2,270ms), while actual image download duration is the smallest (≈350ms). This means 'hero discovered late' (lazy-loaded, JS-inserted, or CSS-background hero) hurts more than raw image bytes — so making the hero an eager in the initial HTML with fetchpriority=high recovers more score than compression alone.
notes: p75 poor-LCP subparts: TTFB 2270ms, load delay 1290ms, render delay 360ms, load duration 350ms. Counterintuitive: load DURATION is least impactful. Validates eager + fetchpriority over CSS background-image heroes in our Hero.astro.

claim: Lazy-loading the LCP/hero image is a measurable, still-common anti-pattern: ~16% of mobile pages lazy-load their LCP image, which delays the highest-priority request and typically adds 200-800ms to LCP. The hero must NOT have loading='lazy'; only below-the-fold images should be lazy-loaded.
notes: 16% mobile pages (9.5% native loading=lazy + 6.7% custom). Down from 18% in 2022. Easy own-goal — many DIY/page-builder sites blanket-apply lazy loading. Our Hero.astro should eager-load the hero and lazy-load only later sections.

claim: Modern image formats are still under-adopted: WebP is ~7-12% of images and AVIF ~0.3%, while JPEG/PNG dominate (~32%/28%). Converting hero/gallery images to WebP yields ~25-35% file-size reduction with no visible quality loss, making 'no WebP/AVIF' a common-but-missing failure on local-shop sites that recovers LCP and total page weight.
notes: WebP ~95%+ browser support now, so the only cost is build-time conversion. AVIF grew 386% 2022-2024 but still tiny. Astro's <Image>/<Picture> can emit WebP/AVIF automatically — worth wiring into the engine.

claim: Page-builder/CMS platform choice is the single largest empirical driver of whether a site passes Core Web Vitals: WordPress sites pass at only ~43% vs Squarespace ~68-70%, Wix ~71-75%, Shopify ~75%, and Duda ~84% (HTTP Archive CrUX, 2025). WordPress+Elementor/Divi sites routinely ship 3-5MB pages that could be ~300KB. So a prospect on WordPress+page-builder is the strongest single predictor of a failing mobile PSI score and the best outreach target.
notes: June 2025 CWV pass: Duda 83.6%, Shopify 75.2%, Wix 70.8%, Squarespace 67.7%, Drupal 59.1%, WordPress 43.4%. Use as a prospecting/qualification signal: detect builder via headers/Wappalyzer; WordPress+Elementor = high-conversion audit lead. Our static Astro engine structurally beats all of these.

claim: Web-font loading is a meaningful secondary perf failure on two axes: (1) layout shift — font swaps contribute ~15-20% of total CLS on the median page; and (2) render delay — FOIT/late fonts delay text-LCP. Setting font-display:swap (or optional) plus a size-matched fallback fixes both; one documented case cut CLS from 0.18 to 0.03. Self-hosting/preloading fonts also removes a render-blocking third-party request to Google Fonts.
notes: Our token system uses display+body font pairs (--font-display/--font-sans) — must ship with font-display:swap, preload, and self-host (or fontsource) to avoid both the CLS and the render-blocking Google Fonts request.

claim: Layout shift from late-inserted content (embeds, banners, images without width/height, ad/booking iframes) is the main CLS failure mode; reserving space (explicit width/height or aspect-ratio on every image and embed slot) is the fix. Mobile CLS already passes on ~79% of pages, so CLS is a 'finish the job' item rather than the primary failing metric — but unreserved hero/gallery images and injected embeds are the residual offenders.
notes: Lower priority than LCP because mobile CLS passes ~79% of the time. Cheap to get right at build: every and embed slot carries explicit dimensions/aspect-ratio. Our static components should enforce this.

claim: Missing/weak caching is a real but secondary contributor: ~25% of requests ship with no Cache-Control header and many cacheable responses carry Set-Cookie (~40%) that defeats CDN caching. For local shops the practical failure is hosting on a slow shared origin with no CDN — which shows up as high TTFB (the largest poor-LCP subpart at ~2,270ms p75). Serving static output from a CDN/edge host (Cloudflare Pages/Netlify) collapses TTFB and is a structural fix rather than a per-asset one.
notes: TTFB dominates poor-LCP pages (2270ms p75) and is mostly hosting/origin, not content. Our deploy model (static Astro on Cloudflare Pages/Netlify edge) inherently fixes CDN+caching+TTFB — a built-in advantage over shared-host WordPress prospects. Caching-header figures are 2021 (latest dedicated Almanac caching chapter).

claim: Ranked by frequency-as-primary-cause of a failing mobile PSI score on local-shop sites, the order is roughly: (1) platform/page-builder bloat (sets the baseline — WordPress+Elementor halves pass rate), (2) unoptimized/late hero image (oversized OR lazy-loaded OR JS/CSS-background, hitting the 73% of pages where LCP is an image), (3) render-blocking third-party scripts/embeds (booking/chat/analytics/fonts), (4) slow origin / no CDN (high TTFB), (5) web-font + embed layout shift (CLS). INP is rarely the primary mobile failure for simple brochure sites. Fixing the hero + removing/deferring embeds + serving static-from-CDN recovers the most score per effort.
notes: This is the synthesized ranking the question asks for. Note ordering blends frequency (platform/image) with recoverable-score-per-fix (CDN/TTFB is high-impact but platform-level). For our audit rubric: weight perf score heavily on LCP-of-hero + third-party request count + builder detection; INP can be near-pass-by-default for static brochure sites.

claim: Google officially uses Core Web Vitals (LCP, INP, CLS) within its core ranking systems as part of page experience, but states 'there is no single signal' and that Search 'always seeks to show the most relevant content, even if the page experience is sub-par' — so good CWV does not guarantee ranking and never outweighs content relevance.
notes: Primary Google doc. Frame perf to buyers as a real but secondary/tiebreaker SEO factor, not a ranking driver. Honest pitch: it helps when content is otherwise comparable, and supports UX/conversion.

claim: Google explicitly says 'getting good results in reports like Search Console's Core Web Vitals report or third-party tools doesn't guarantee that your pages will rank at the top' — passing CWV thresholds (LCP<2.5s/INP<200ms/CLS<0.1) is a quality floor, not a ranking lever a buyer can expect to move positions.
notes: Use this to avoid overselling a 'perf' line item as an SEO ranking guarantee. Repo's CWV targets already align with Google's 'good' thresholds.

claim: In the Local Pack, ranking is dominated by Google Business Profile signals (~32%), reviews (~20%), on-page (~15%), and proximity/relevance — and recognized Local Search Ranking Factors surveys do NOT list page speed or Core Web Vitals as a distinct local-pack ranking factor.
notes: Most important honesty point for a Calgary local shop: for map-pack visibility, GBP completeness + reviews + proximity dominate; site perf is not a measured local-pack factor. Perf's value is conversion-side, not local-ranking-side.

claim: Google's local results are based on three documented factors — relevance, distance (proximity), and prominence — with proximity being the single strongest visibility driver (cited at ~36-55% depending on position); website performance is not one of the three.
notes: Reinforces: a perf line item cannot overcome proximity/review deficits in the map pack. Sell perf as conversion + organic-tiebreaker, and pair with GBP/review work for local visibility.

claim: A Google/SOASTA machine-learning study found that as mobile page load time goes from 1s to 10s, the probability of a mobile visitor bouncing increases 123%, and as on-page elements grow from 400 to 6,000 the probability of conversion drops 95% — i.e., mobile speed and page weight materially affect whether visitors stay and convert.
notes: Primary Google/Think source. This is the honest core of the perf pitch: speed is a CONVERSION lever (bounce/engagement), supported by Google's own data. Our static-Astro output is well under the bounce-risk curve.

claim: A Deloitte/Google study across 30M+ sessions found a 0.1s improvement in mobile load time correlated with measurable lifts — ~8.4% higher retail conversions (and ~9.2% higher average order value), and ~10.1% higher travel conversions — demonstrating that even small speed gains move revenue outcomes.
notes: Effect sizes are for e-commerce/travel/lead-gen; a barber/cafe with a phone-call CTA won't see identical % lifts, but the directional 'faster = more conversions' holds. Cite conservatively for local shops.

claim: Independent analysis (Portent, ~100M page views / 5.6M sessions across 20 sites) shows B2C e-commerce conversion rate falls from 3.05% at 1s load to 1.68% at 2s, 1.12% at 3s and 0.67% at 4s, with conversion dropping ~0.3% per additional second — the steepest losses occur within the first few seconds, making a sub-3s mobile load the highest-leverage perf target.
notes: Most directly applicable to verticals with on-site transactions/orders (retail, cafe ordering). For call/booking-CTA verticals the absolute % won't transfer, but it justifies prioritizing first-paint speed (LCP<2.5s).

claim: Roughly half of mobile visitors abandon a site that doesn't load within ~3 seconds, and per-second mobile delay can cut conversions by up to ~20% — so for local-shop mobile traffic (the dominant device for 'near me' searches) load speed directly affects whether a lead reaches the click-to-call/booking CTA.
notes: Local 'near me' intent is overwhelmingly mobile and high-intent; a slow load loses the lead before the CTA. This is the strongest buyer-facing justification for a perf line item on a local shop site.

claim: Page speed influences SEO indirectly through user-behavior signals (bounce/dwell): even where CWV isn't a heavy direct ranking factor, faster pages reduce bounce and increase engagement, which Local Search Ranking Factors surveys count as 'behavioral signals' (~8-9% weight) contributing to local and organic rankings.
notes: Indirect/secondary mechanism — present it as a supporting benefit, not a primary claim. Behavioral signals are a small, debated slice of local ranking weight.

claim: Mobile sites are slow in the wild — Google measured an average full mobile landing-page load of ~15 seconds with over 70% of pages taking >5s to show above-the-fold content — so a static, fast-by-default build (Astro static output hitting CWV targets) is a genuine, demonstrable differentiator versus typical small-business sites.
notes: Supports the repo's static-output strategy as a real selling point: most competitor small-shop sites are slow, so 'fast by default' is a credible, measurable advantage in a PageSpeed/Lighthouse comparison.

claim: Pre-rendered HTML delivered from a CDN edge gives a fast, predictable LCP because the markup arrives ready-to-paint with no client-side JS execution required before content appears; real React-SPA-to-Astro migrations cut LCP from ~4s to ~1.5s. Edge delivery + static HTML get LCP into 'good' territory by default, BUT the LCP element is almost always the hero image, so final LCP is gated by image weight/format/priority — the one perf lever still requiring per-build discipline.
notes: LCP is 'mostly guaranteed but not free' — the architecture removes JS/server latency, but the hero image is the remaining variable every build must control.

claim: The LCP element is an image on ~76% of mobile pages and ~85% of desktop pages, so on a local-shop site the hero image is almost certainly the LCP element. This makes hero-image optimization (weight, format, dimensions, priority) the single highest-impact perf task per shop, not an optional nicety.
notes: Justifies why the perf checklist must center on the hero image for every vertical (barber, cafe, spa, etc. all lead with a hero photo in the engine).

claim: Serving the hero/LCP image in a modern format (AVIF first, ~50% smaller than JPEG; WebP fallback, ~25-35% smaller) plus correctly sized responsive variants (srcset) materially reduces LCP. Astro's Image component does format conversion, responsive sizing, and dimension-setting automatically, so the checklist item is 'use the Image component for the hero,' not hand-roll .
notes: Codify: hero must go through Astro Image (or equivalent) -> AVIF/WebP + srcset. A raw multi-MB JPEG hero is the classic way a static site still fails LCP.

claim: Preloading the LCP (hero) image and/or setting fetchpriority="high" on it measurably speeds LCP: CoreDash field data shows 98% of loads with a preloaded LCP image score 'good' vs 88% without, and p75 LCP roughly 2x faster; a Google Flights test cut LCP from 2.6s to 1.9s by adding fetchpriority="high" alone. Despite this, only ~2.1% of pages preload their LCP image — a cheap, high-impact win to bake into every build.
notes: For a plain foreground the preload scanner usually finds it, so fetchpriority="high" is the priority lever; explicit matters most for CSS-background heroes or JS-driven/slider heroes. The engine's hero should set fetchpriority=high and avoid being a CSS background or lazy-loaded.

claim: Never lazy-load the LCP/hero image. ~17% of mobile sites lazy-load their LCP image, which severely delays LCP because the browser defers the very image that defines the metric. The hero must load eagerly (loading="eager", and ideally fetchpriority="high"); only below-the-fold images get loading="lazy".
notes: Easy footgun: a blanket loading=lazy applied to all images. Checklist: hero eager, everything below fold lazy.

claim: Every and
notes: CLS is 'guaranteed good' for static first-party HTML provided dimensions are set — the engine's static layout has no async-injected content. The remaining CLS risks are (a) raw without dimensions, (b) web fonts, (c) third-party embeds — all controllable per build.

claim: Web fonts are a residual CLS/LCP risk that static architecture does NOT solve automatically: FOUT/FOIT swaps cause layout shift when the fallback and webfont have different metrics, and render-blocking font CSS delays text paint. Mitigations to codify: self-host fonts (removes third-party connection + lets you preload), use font-display: swap (or optional for zero shift), preload the 1-2 critical fonts with crossorigin, and set size-adjusted fallback metrics to eliminate swap shift.
notes: The engine pairs a display + body font (theme.css --font-display/--font-sans), so two webfonts is the typical surface. Self-hosting also avoids a third-party origin and gives full caching/preload control. Astro's built-in font API (experimental/stable depending on version) and Capsize-style fallbacks address the swap shift.

claim: Self-hosting fonts (vs linking Google Fonts) improves LCP, FCP and CLS by removing an extra third-party connection and render-blocking external CSS, and enabling preload/caching control of the font file — and it takes roughly 10 minutes to set up. This is a one-time engine decision that benefits every shop.
notes: Codify in the engine, not per-shop: ship self-hosted font files + @font-face rather than <link> to fonts.googleapis.com. Aligns with the no-third-party-origin theme.

claim: Third-party embeds (booking widgets, Google Maps iframes, chat widgets) are the primary way a static shop site still fails Core Web Vitals: iframe widgets often add 20-40 extra requests and many KB of JS (some embeds 100KB-2MB), competing for early bandwidth (hurts LCP/FCP) and blocking the main thread (inflates INP). Each embed must be deliberately controlled per build — they bypass the zero-JS guarantee.
notes: Most relevant to verticals that book online (barber/spa/fitness/dental) or embed a map. Law/retail/trades/cafe may only have a map. This is THE per-shop perf judgment call.

claim: Use the facade pattern for heavy embeds: show a lightweight static stand-in (screenshot/placeholder + button) and load the real third-party resource only on user interaction. Facades cut LCP meaningfully (YouTube-facade pages average ~800ms faster LCP; lite-youtube is ~224x faster than the full player) and keep embed JS off the main thread until clicked, protecting INP. For maps, ship a static map image linking out to Google Maps; for chat/booking, load on click.
notes: Concrete checklist: (1) map = static image + link-out, not live iframe; (2) booking = link/button to the booking host, not an inlined widget, OR click-to-load facade; (3) chat = load on interaction. The engine's ContactNAP already uses a mapUrl link rather than an iframe, which is the correct facade-equivalent default.

claim: When an embed must be inline, give its iframe explicit width/height (or reserve container space via min-height/aspect-ratio) and add loading="lazy" so it loads only near the viewport — preventing both the CLS from late-injected embeds and the bandwidth contention from below-fold widgets. Lazy iframes are supported in modern browsers.
notes: Fallback rule for when a true facade isn't built: reserve space + lazy-load any inline iframe.

claim: Core Web Vitals are scored at the 75th percentile of real-user (field/CrUX) page loads, segmented mobile vs desktop, preferring URL-level data and falling back to origin-level. Implication for the engine: a good Lighthouse/lab score is necessary but not sufficient — the guarantee only holds in the field if real hero images and any embeds are controlled; mobile (slower CPU/network) is the binding constraint, so the perf checklist must be validated at mobile widths, matching the repo's mobile-first screenshot discipline.
notes: Explains why 'screenshot-verify at mobile + desktop' in CLAUDE.md is the right verification altitude, and why a heavy hero that passes on desktop can still fail mobile field CWV.

claim: A reusable per-build perf checklist that ships with every shop is justified and stable across all verticals, because the guaranteed-by-architecture items (static HTML, edge CDN, zero JS) are constant and the residual failure modes are a small fixed set: (1) hero image weight+format+responsive, (2) hero set eager + fetchpriority high (never lazy), (3) explicit width/height on all images/iframes, (4) self-hosted preloaded fonts with swap + sized fallbacks, (5) third-party booking/map/chat handled via link-out or facade/lazy. This is the always-reused checklist to codify.
notes: Synthesis claim: maps directly to a skill/checklist. Aligns with packages/audit grading perf and with CLAUDE.md's 'Core Web Vitals LCP<2.5 / INP<200 / CLS<0.1' bar. The first three items are auto-handled if the hero uses Astro's Image component, reducing the checklist to: use Image for hero + set priority, self-host fonts, link-out/facade embeds.

claim: Lighthouse/PSI's default mobile network throttling ('Slow 4G': 1.6 Mbps down, 750 Kbps up, 150ms RTT) is roughly 40-55x slower than the real median Canadian mobile network. Opensignal measured Canada's overall Download Speed Experience at ~82 Mbps (Aug 2025), rising to ~92 Mbps (Feb 2026). Lighthouse itself states Slow 4G represents 'the bottom 25% of 4G connections and top 25% of 3G connections' — i.e. a deliberate worst-case floor, not the Canadian median.
notes: Lab Slow 4G is intentionally a stress-test floor, not a market median. For Calgary, real-network LCP at 82-92 Mbps will be far faster than the PSI mobile lab number implies. The audit's perf grade should treat the PSI mobile lab score as a worst-case/relative diagnostic, not as a literal prediction of what Calgary visitors experience.

claim: The Calgary/Canadian local-shop mobile audience is overwhelmingly on premium-tier iOS devices, not the mid-tier Android profile Lighthouse emulates. StatCounter (May 2026) puts Apple at 64.4% of Canadian mobile vendor share, Samsung 20.6%, Google 6.8%, Motorola ~2%. Lighthouse/PSI emulates a mid-tier Android (historically Moto G4/G Power) with a 4x CPU slowdown, which is slower than the iPhone the median Canadian shop visitor actually carries.
notes: Device CPU drives INP/TBT more than network drives LCP. The emulated mid-tier Android understates the speed of the typical Canadian iPhone, so both the network AND device sides of PSI mobile bias toward pessimism for this specific market. Counterpoint Q3 2025 corroborates iOS-led, premium-skewed Canadian market.

claim: PageSpeed Insights already reduced its mobile lab CPU throttling from 4x to ~1.2x in December 2024 specifically because the 4x multiplier over-stated slowness versus real-world; locally-run Lighthouse CLI still defaults to 4x. This means PSI-web and a local 'pnpm lighthouse' run will report materially different perf scores for the same site, so the audit must pin which tool/throttling it uses.
notes: Directly actionable: the packages/audit collector must standardize on one configuration (PSI API vs local Lighthouse 4x) or scores are non-comparable across prospects and across time. Recommend pinning the PSI API (CrUX + 1.2x lab) for outreach consistency, or explicitly documenting the local 4x bias.

claim: For the pass/fail bar, real-user field data (CrUX, reported at the 75th percentile over a rolling 28-day window) is the authoritative measure of whether a site is actually fast for its visitors, and is the signal Google uses for Core Web Vitals ranking — lab data 'may not capture real-world bottlenecks.' The audit should weight CrUX field data (LCP<2.5s / INP<200ms / CLS<0.1 at p75) over the lab score when CrUX exists for the prospect's origin.
notes: Resolves the core question: prefer field data to avoid flagging sites that pass on real Calgary networks/devices. CrUX p75 already bakes in the real Calgary network mix and iOS device skew, so it neither over- nor under-states for this market the way synthetic Slow-4G does.

claim: Many small Calgary local-shop sites will have NO CrUX field data because CrUX requires a minimum traffic threshold; low-traffic shop origins are not represented. For these the audit cannot rely on field data and must fall back to lab — which is exactly where the Slow-4G/mid-tier-Android pessimism bites. The rubric should explicitly branch: use CrUX p75 if present, else lab score with a documented 'lab is worst-case' caveat rather than a hard fail.
notes: Single-location independent shops are the typical no-CrUX case. This is the practical reason the audit can't simply 'always use field data' — most prospects will be lab-only, so the lab interpretation rule matters most.

claim: Canadian network availability is effectively ubiquitous (Opensignal: all major carriers tied at 99.3-99.4% of time on a 3G/4G/5G connection), but 5G is NOT the default experience — users spend only ~17.5% of time on 5G (Telus, the leader). The realistic target profile for a Calgary shop visitor is good 4G, not 5G: Opensignal-class 4G download was ~70-80 Mbps, far above Slow 4G's 1.6 Mbps but below the 5G headline numbers (~190 Mbps).
notes: Argues against targeting 5G (~190 Mbps) as the design assumption and against targeting Slow-4G (1.6 Mbps) as the failure bar. A 'Regular/Fast 4G' profile (~40-80 Mbps, ~40-70ms RTT) is the realistic median to sanity-check real-device load against for this audience.

claim: Recommended audit policy: treat PSI mobile LAB perf as a relative/diagnostic score (good for spotting unoptimized images, render-blocking, missing dimensions) but base the pass/fail GRADE on CrUX field p75 when available, and on a real-device or field-weighted check (good-4G profile + iOS) when not. This avoids both failure modes the question raises: failing sites that are fast for real Calgary visitors, and passing sites that are slow on actual Calgary networks.
notes: Synthesis / directly actionable change to packages/audit perf rubric. Concretely: (1) query CrUX via PSI API and grade on p75 LCP/INP/CLS if present; (2) else run lab but label it 'worst-case Slow-4G / mid-tier Android — real iOS/4G visitors will be faster'; (3) never hard-fail an image-optimized static site on lab LCP alone.

claim: The Lighthouse single 'performance score' (0-100) is a weighted average of five lab metrics with fixed weights in Lighthouse 10-12: LCP 25%, TBT 30%, CLS 25%, FCP 10%, Speed Index 10%. An audit should store the three Core Web Vitals (LCP, CLS, and the TBT proxy) individually rather than grading off the composite, because the composite blends two non-CWV proxies (FCP, SI = 20%) and the INP-proxy TBT (30%) into one number that can hide which metric is actually failing.
notes: Weights confirmed identical from two sources (Google's own scoring doc and DebugBear). The composite is not 'deprecated' per se, but grading on it is lower-fidelity than grading the individual CWV.

claim: Lighthouse / PageSpeed Insights lab runs CANNOT measure INP (interaction responsiveness) at all — the synthetic run never interacts with the page. TBT is the lab proxy, but TBT only captures main-thread blocking during initial load; a page can score 0 ms TBT and still fail INP in the field. For a brochure-style local-shop site (little JS, no app interactions), an audit should treat INP as low-signal in lab mode and not penalize on a TBT/INP basis unless real CrUX field INP is available.
notes: This is the single biggest reason the rubric should not weight the composite score: 30% of it is a proxy that barely applies to static brochure sites.

claim: Most local-shop sites have NO CrUX field data — Google only publishes Core Web Vitals field data for origins/pages that exceed an (undisclosed) traffic threshold, so low-traffic small-business sites return 'no field data' and PSI shows lab data only. The audit must detect the field-data-absent case and gracefully fall back to lab metrics (LCP, CLS, and load proxies) rather than reporting a blank/failed CWV section as a defect.
notes: Directly shapes audit logic: for a Calgary single-location shop, expect origin-level CrUX at best and often nothing; lab data is the only deterministic signal we control.

claim: A single mobile Lighthouse/PSI run is unstable: Google attributes variance to multiple high-impact sources (page nondeterminism, local/Tier-1 network variability, client hardware, resource contention) and states 'the median Lighthouse score of 5 runs is twice as stable as 1 run.' Practitioner consensus is ±5 (often ±5-10) points of swing per run. The audit should run multiple lab passes (or use PSI which medians internally) and use a median, and must NOT grade pass/fail on a knife-edge threshold from one run.
notes: Google's own variability doc is the primary source for the '5-run median = 2x stable' figure and the seven variance sources. PSI's API runs Lighthouse server-side; calling it returns a single lab pass, so a grade within ~5 points of a boundary should be treated as a tie, not a fail.

claim: Lighthouse 'Opportunities' and 'Diagnostics' — including 'Remove unused CSS' and 'Serve static assets with an efficient cache policy' — do NOT contribute to the performance score. Google states explicitly: 'only metrics contribute to your Lighthouse Performance score, not the results of Opportunities or Diagnostics.' The rubric should therefore NOT penalize a site for these flagged audits as if they were score-bearing; they are advisory and at most indirectly correlated with the metrics.
notes: This is the core 'avoid penalizing on noise' answer: unused-CSS and cache-TTL warnings are real-but-non-scored. Treat as informational, never as grade deductions.

claim: The 'efficient cache policy' warning frequently fires on THIRD-PARTY assets the shop owner cannot control (Google Fonts, GA/gtag, Facebook Pixel, embedded maps/booking widgets), which set their own short cache lifetimes. Penalizing a local-shop site for this is misleading because it is not fixable by the site owner and does not affect first-load CWV. The audit should ignore or down-rank cache-policy flags on cross-origin third-party assets.
notes: Cache TTL only helps repeat visits, not the first paint a new prospect experiences; and third-party TTLs are out of the owner's hands. Low diagnostic value for a brochure site.

claim: The 'Remove unused CSS' warning is near-universal and rarely material for a small brochure site: the 2024 Web Almanac median page ships ~79 KB CSS with ~52 KB unused, so almost every site trips it. It is a non-scored opportunity and its LCP/FCP impact is small for the tiny stylesheets typical of a static shop site (an Astro/Tailwind build); aggressive removal can even hurt because per-page CSS files lose cross-page caching. Do not deduct for unused-CSS warnings.
notes: Lighthouse maintainers themselves debated the unused-CSS threshold (issue #9353). 'common-but-missing' here = the warning is almost always present but a clean fix is almost always absent and unnecessary.

claim: LCP is the load-bearing lab metric for grading local-shop sites: it is 25% of the Lighthouse score AND a real Core Web Vital, and for a brochure/hero-image layout the LCP element is almost always the hero image or main heading. The highest-yield fixes (compress/serve a properly sized hero, preload it, inline critical CSS, font-display:swap) are exactly what these sites get wrong. The rubric should weight measured LCP heavily.
notes: Good threshold: LCP < 2.5s at p75 (field) or as the lab value. Hero-image weight makes LCP the metric most predictive of 'this shop site feels slow.'

claim: CLS is the cheap-to-fix, high-signal layout metric for brochure sites: it is 25% of the Lighthouse score, a real Core Web Vital (good = <=0.1), and the dominant cause on shop sites is images/iframes/embeds without explicit width/height. It is deterministic and reproducible in lab mode (unlike INP), so it is a reliable grading input. The rubric should keep CLS as a weighted metric and flag missing image dimensions as the concrete fix.
notes: CLS measures cleanly in lab data and maps to a one-line fix (width/height or aspect-ratio), making it both diagnostically load-bearing and actionable in our own builds.

claim: FCP and Speed Index are proxies, not Core Web Vitals, and are strongly correlated with LCP (LCP can never be faster than FCP). Together they are only 20% of the Lighthouse score. They are useful as secondary 'render-start' diagnostics but should NOT be graded as independent must-pass metrics — optimizing LCP generally pulls FCP/SI along. Treat them as supporting evidence, not primary grade drivers.
notes: FCP/SI add little independent signal beyond LCP for a brochure site; keep them as informational, not as separate pass/fail gates.

claim: Lab and field data legitimately diverge — Google states lab measurement 'is not a substitute for field measurement,' because real users have slower devices/networks; a page can score 95 in Lighthouse and still fail CWV in the field (or vice-versa). For grading, the audit should report lab metrics as the deterministic baseline we can control in our builds, and surface CrUX field data (when present) separately, never conflating the two into one number.
notes: Practical consequence for outreach: lab data is what we can promise to improve in a rebuild; field data is what Google ranks on but is often unavailable for the prospect's low-traffic site.

claim: Confirmed current Core Web Vitals 'good' thresholds (p75 of real users) for the rubric: LCP <= 2.5s, INP <= 200ms (INP became the stable CWV replacing FID in March 2024), CLS <= 0.1. These match the repo's existing targets and remain current as of 2026; FID is fully retired and should not appear in the audit.
notes: Re-verification of the seed context's targets against primary source — unchanged and correct. Drop any FID references.

claim: In the most-cited local ranking factors survey (Whitespark/BrightLocal 2025), Google Business Profile signals are the single largest local-pack category at ~32% of weight, followed by reviews ~20%, on-page/website signals ~15%, behavioral ~9%, links ~8%, citations ~6%, social ~5%. This means the website itself (on-page) directly accounts for only ~15% of local-pack ranking weight; the majority lives in GBP and reviews, which the website cannot directly control.
notes: This is the foundational ceiling claim: ~15% of the local-pack (3-pack/map) result is on-page. Establishes that a build-and-handoff website realistically influences a minority of local-pack ranking, with GBP+reviews (52%+) sitting outside the website. Frame client expectations accordingly.

claim: Proximity (physical distance between the searcher and the business address) is a dominant, largely fixed local-pack factor that no website change can alter — it is set by the GBP-registered address. For storefronts this anchors a strong radius; the website contributes zero to proximity.
notes: Hard ceiling. A handoff site cannot promise local-pack rank improvements driven by proximity. Useful disclaimer language for sales/scoping to avoid over-promising.

claim: For local ORGANIC (blue-link) results — distinct from the map/3-pack — on-page/website signals are the LARGEST category at ~33%, plus links ~24%, while GBP drops to ~7%. The static website's leverage is far higher in local organic than in the local pack, so the realistic website ROI is organic 'near me' and city+service queries rather than the 3-pack.
notes: Reframes the value proposition: a build-and-handoff site's realistic ceiling is strong local-organic ranking (where it controls ~33%), feeding the pack indirectly. This is the honest 'what the website actually buys you' answer.

claim: NAP (Name, Address, Phone) must appear as crawlable text on the website (not inside an image) and must match the GBP listing exactly; consistent NAP across the site and citations is a relevance/trust signal the website directly controls. Rendering phone/address as text (click-to-call tel: links, text address) is the website's core NAP contribution.
notes: Directly maps to our audit items clickToCall and addressOrMap. Ensure ContactNAP.astro and SiteFooter.astro emit NAP as text, not image, and identical across pages. This is fully within a static-site handoff's control.

claim: LocalBusiness schema (JSON-LD) on the homepage and key pages — including name, address, geo/phone, hours, and for service-area businesses areaServed, plus hasOfferCatalog for services — helps Google parse the business and is fully within a static site's control. It is part of the ~15% on-page weight and is a one-time build asset requiring no ongoing maintenance.
notes: Maps to our localBusinessJsonLd audit item and seo/LocalBusinessJsonLd.astro. Recommendation: extend the component to emit areaServed (Calgary + neighbouring areas) for trades/service verticals and hasOfferCatalog/menu items. 'common-but-missing' because most prospect sites lack it — a concrete differentiator.

claim: Dedicated service pages (one page per service/offering) and city/location-relevant content are top individual on-page local signals; thin single-page sites under-index on relevance for service+location queries. A static build can ship multiple service/location pages once, with no ongoing SEO retainer needed.
notes: Tension with our current minimal single-page demo architecture. For multi-service verticals (trades, dental, law, auto) a one-time multi-page build materially raises the local-organic ceiling. Cafes/barbers/retail with one offering benefit less.

claim: Keyword-targeted title tags and H1/H2 headings that include the service + city (e.g. 'Barber in Calgary') are direct, website-controlled on-page signals; the GBP landing-page title tag is among the top organic factors. These are set once at build time and need no maintenance.
notes: Cheap, fully-controllable, one-time win. Our SEO layer / page <title> and Hero H1 should template service+Calgary. Verify per-shop content does this.

claim: Review signals (recency, velocity, quantity, rating) rose to ~20% of local-pack weight in 2025, with review RECENCY emerging as a top factor — reviews under ~30 days carry full weight while reviews older than ~180 days retain only 10-20%. This is entirely a GBP/ongoing-operations signal the website cannot generate; a static site can at most display reviews and link out to leave one.
notes: Key for build-and-handoff model with no retainer: the 20% review weight decays without ongoing review-gathering, which is the CLIENT's job post-handoff. Our Reviews.astro displays rating/proof but cannot influence the ranking signal. Hand off a 'review request' link/QR as a one-time asset and document it.

claim: A website is not required to appear in the local pack (GBP listings rank without one), but adding the website URL to GBP and aligning website content with the GBP category/services strengthens Google's relevance confidence in the listing. The website's local-pack contribution is therefore indirect reinforcement, not a primary lever.
notes: Honest framing: the site is a relevance-reinforcement and conversion asset for the pack, and a primary ranking asset for local-organic. Handoff should include linking the site in GBP and matching site copy to GBP category/services.

claim: Service-area businesses (no public storefront) rank in a tighter, more volatile radius than storefronts because a hidden address gives Google a weaker proximity anchor; they compensate with reviews, content and service-area schema rather than proximity. The website's relative importance (areaServed schema, city/service pages) is HIGHER for SABs than for storefronts.
notes: Vertical-specific: trades and mobile auto are SABs. Our build should emphasize areaServed (Calgary neighbourhoods/satellite towns) schema and service-area copy for these — it does more proportional work where proximity is weak. Storefronts (cafe, barber, retail, dental) lean on proximity instead.

claim: Citation/NAP consistency across third-party directories (Yelp, YellowPages, etc.) is a ~6% local-pack signal that lives off-site, not on the website; a one-time-built site cannot manage citations. The website's only role is to be the canonical, exactly-matching NAP source those citations are checked against.
notes: Out of scope for build-and-handoff (citations are ongoing). Mention at handoff as a client task; our deliverable just guarantees a consistent canonical NAP. Low impact and out of our retainer-free model's control.

claim: Behavioral/engagement signals (clicks, calls, direction requests, dwell, mobile usability) form ~9% of local-pack weight; a fast, mobile-first, conversion-complete static site (Core Web Vitals LCP<2.5s/INP<200ms/CLS<0.1, click-to-call, booking) indirectly improves these by converting the traffic GBP sends, even though it cannot directly set the ranking signal.
notes: This is the strongest honest 'website helps the pack' lever besides on-page: a faster, more usable site generates the behavioral signals (calls/clicks) that feed the ~9% category. Aligns with our existing CWV targets and conversion inventory (clickToCall, bookingLink, mobileViewport). Static output directly supports this.

claim: Google's LocalBusiness structured data has exactly two required properties: name and address (a full PostalAddress object). Everything else (geo, telephone, openingHoursSpecification, priceRange, url, etc.) is recommended, not required, for validity. A site is rich-result-eligible with only name+address present.
notes: The repo's current LocalBusinessJsonLd.astro already emits name, address, telephone, url, optional geo — so it clears the validity bar. The gap is the recommended properties, not validity.

claim: Google instructs using the most specific LocalBusiness sub-type available rather than the generic LocalBusiness @type (its own examples: Restaurant, DaySpa, HealthClub). The repo's component currently hardcodes @type:'LocalBusiness' for every shop, which is valid but leaves specificity (and entity understanding) on the table — @type should be a per-vertical field.
notes: Action: make @type a content field per shop (e.g. shop.schemaType) defaulting to vertical mapping below.

claim: Correct most-specific @type per vertical: barber -> HairSalon (no BarberShop type exists in schema.org; HairSalon is the closest, under HealthAndBeautyBusiness); cafe -> CafeOrCoffeeShop (Restaurant if full-service dining); spa -> DaySpa (or BeautySalon/NailSalon as fit); fitness -> HealthClub or ExerciseGym (both under SportsActivityLocation); dental -> Dentist; auto -> AutoRepair (under AutomotiveBusiness); retail -> Store or a Store subtype (ClothingStore, HardwareStore, JewelryStore, etc.).
notes: BarberShop confirmed absent from schema.org; community proposal (issue #2049) never merged — HairSalon is the accepted mapping.

claim: For law firms, use @type 'LegalService' (a direct LocalBusiness subtype), NOT 'Attorney'. The Attorney type is ambiguous/superseded for this purpose and is not the recommended business-entity type; LegalService is the inclusive, current choice for a law office. For trades, use the specific HomeAndConstructionBusiness subtype: HVACBusiness, Plumber, Electrician, GeneralContractor, RoofingContractor, HousePainter, Locksmith, or MovingCompany — not generic LocalBusiness.
notes: Attorney describes an individual lawyer/practitioner; the business entity should be LegalService. Trades template needs a sub-vertical field since 'trades' maps to ~8 distinct types.

claim: Self-markup of aggregateRating/review about your OWN business violates Google policy and makes the page ineligible for the star/review rich result. Per Google's review snippet guidelines, if the entity being reviewed controls the reviews about itself, LocalBusiness (and any Organization) structured data is ineligible for star reviews — this includes embedding Google/Facebook review widgets and self-supplied aggregateRating values. aggregateRating is only legitimate when a third-party site reviews OTHER businesses.
notes: Load-bearing guardrail: the shop-site engine must NOT emit aggregateRating/review in LocalBusiness JSON-LD even though the Reviews.astro component shows a rating on-page. On-page star display is fine; marking it up as self-aggregateRating is a policy violation and risks a manual action. This is the single biggest correctness fix for the schema.

claim: Google lists aggregateRating, review, department, geo, menu, openingHoursSpecification, priceRange, servesCuisine, telephone, and url as the recommended LocalBusiness properties. Of these, the safe-and-valuable set for a self-owned shop site is: geo, telephone, url, openingHoursSpecification, and priceRange (plus menu/servesCuisine for food). aggregateRating and review are recommended ONLY for third-party review sites and must be omitted from a self-owned shop.
notes: Engine should add openingHoursSpecification and priceRange (both already in content shape via hours/services) and keep geo/telephone/url. Explicitly exclude aggregateRating/review.

claim: geo should be a GeoCoordinates with latitude and longitude expressed to at least 5 decimal places, and telephone should include country and area code in international format (e.g. +1-403-555-0100 for Calgary). These precision/format rules are the difference between a populated and an ignored value.
notes: Repo already emits geo + telephone; enforce >=5 decimal lat/lng and +1-403 E.164-ish format in content authoring.

claim: openingHoursSpecification is the structured, machine-readable hours form: an array of OpeningHoursSpecification objects each with dayOfWeek (schema.org day URLs, can be an array of days sharing the same hours), opens, and closes in 24h HH:MM. If closes < opens the range spans past midnight (handles late-night cafe/bar hours). This supersedes the free-text openingHours string and is what powers the hours shown in the local pack/knowledge panel.
notes: The Hours.astro component already has the data; emit it as openingHoursSpecification in the JSON-LD, not just as an HTML table.

claim: priceRange is a recommended LocalBusiness property; Google will not display it if it exceeds 100 characters. Conventional values are symbol bands ($, $$, $$$, $$$$) or a numeric range ('$10-$30'). It is most meaningful for cafe/restaurant, spa, retail, and auto; useful but lower-signal for dental/law.
notes: Keep under 100 chars; trivial to add as a per-shop content field.

claim: Service-area / mobile businesses that travel to the customer (most trades) should use areaServed to declare their geographic coverage instead of (or alongside) implying customers visit a storefront; areaServed supersedes the older serviceArea property. A storefront business customers physically visit (cafe, retail, barber, spa, dental, gym, auto bay) should NOT rely on areaServed and should keep address+geo as the primary signal.
notes: Distinct branch in the engine: trades content shape needs areaServed (e.g. 'Calgary and surrounding area' or a list of GeoCircle/AdministrativeArea); storefront verticals keep PostalAddress + geo.

claim: hasMap (which supersedes the older 'map' property) points to the business's Google Maps URL and, combined with geo, reinforces the location for 'near me' and maps surfacing. It is genuinely useful for all storefront verticals but is a nice-to-have, not required-for-validity, and is NOT a substitute for address/geo.
notes: ContactNAP.astro already has mapUrl — feed it into hasMap in the JSON-LD.

claim: For food establishments (cafe, restaurant) the correct subtype FoodEstablishment branch adds load-bearing food-specific properties: hasMenu (supersedes the older 'menu'; accepts a Menu object, text, or URL), servesCuisine, and acceptsReservations (boolean, URL, or 'Yes'/'No'). Menu content is best marked up as schema.org/Menu -> hasMenuSection -> hasMenuItem for machine readability, though Menu itself is not a guaranteed rich-result type.
notes: Aligns with the repo's existing restaurant/retail research; use hasMenu over menu and the Menu->MenuSection->MenuItem nesting the Services.astro 'Menu' heading already implies.

claim: makesOffer (for an Organization/LocalBusiness) and hasOfferCatalog (for an Organization/Service, pointing to an OfferCatalog of Offer -> itemOffered Service) are the correct way to express the services/menu a business performs in structured data. They are nice-to-have entity-enrichment for AI/entity understanding, NOT required for LocalBusiness validity and NOT themselves a Google rich-result trigger. Useful where a service menu is core (spa, barber, auto, law, fitness, trades, dental).
notes: Service/price data already exists in Services.astro; emitting hasOfferCatalog is a cheap entity-richness win but should be scoped as nice-to-have, not a default-must.

claim: Dentist is simultaneously a MedicalBusiness/MedicalOrganization and a LocalBusiness in schema.org, so a dental shop site can keep LocalBusiness-style markup (address, geo, hours, telephone) under @type 'Dentist' and optionally add medical fields (medicalSpecialty, isAcceptingNewPatients). The self-serving aggregateRating prohibition applies identically — a dental practice must not self-markup star ratings.
notes: No special exemption from the review-snippet policy for health businesses; if anything Google scrutinizes health/medical content more (YMYL).

claim: sameAs (linking the shop's authoritative profiles: Google Business Profile/Maps URL, Facebook, Instagram, Yelp) is a recommended Organization/LocalBusiness property for entity reconciliation and is broadly beneficial across all verticals, but is nice-to-have for rich-result eligibility (not required for validity).
notes: Cheap to add from existing social/booking links; aids Knowledge Graph entity linking and AI citation.

claim: A complete, policy-safe minimal LocalBusiness JSON-LD for a self-owned Calgary shop is: @type=<most-specific vertical subtype>, name, address (PostalAddress with streetAddress, addressLocality 'Calgary', addressRegion 'AB', postalCode, addressCountry 'CA'), geo (>=5dp), telephone (+1-403...), url, openingHoursSpecification, priceRange (where meaningful), hasMap, and sameAs — and explicitly WITHOUT aggregateRating/review. Plus vertical add-ons: hasMenu/servesCuisine/acceptsReservations (cafe), areaServed (trades), hasOfferCatalog (service verticals).
notes: This is the target shape for an upgraded LocalBusinessJsonLd.astro. addressCountry must be 'CA' and addressRegion 'AB' for Alberta. The current component omits PostalAddress structure (passes address as a flat field via shop.address) — should be a typed PostalAddress object for full quality.

claim: The audit rubric's 'localBusinessJsonLd' check should be upgraded from binary-present to quality-graded: (1) is @type a specific subtype vs generic LocalBusiness; (2) is address a structured PostalAddress; (3) does it AVOID self-serving aggregateRating/review (a present-but-policy-violating block should fail or warn, not pass); (4) presence of geo + openingHoursSpecification. A site emitting self-aggregateRating should be flagged as a defect, not credited.
notes: Directly actionable for packages/audit: self-markup aggregateRating is a common prospect mistake and a strong outreach hook ('your schema risks a Google penalty').

claim: In current local-search ranking factor surveys, citation signals (including NAP consistency) are a mid-weight, supporting factor — roughly 6-9% of local pack / local organic influence — far below Google Business Profile (17-32%), reviews (14-20%), and on-page SEO (24-33%). NAP consistency is best treated as foundational table-stakes, not a primary ranking lever a shop can rank-climb on.
notes: BrightLocal's algorithm guide gives citations ~6% local pack / ~7% local organic; GBP and reviews dominate. NAP accuracy is framed as 'a good start' foundation, not differentiation. Implication for our service: deliver consistent NAP, but do NOT promise ranking jumps from citations alone.

claim: Consistent NAP (Name/Address/Phone) across the website, GBP, and major directories has a measurable association with local visibility: businesses with consistent NAP across major citation sources are reported ~40% more likely to appear in the local pack, and inconsistent NAP is also a documented consumer-trust/conversion problem (BrightLocal: ~80% of consumers lose trust in a business with inconsistent contact data).
notes: The 40% figure circulates via BrightLocal-derived content; treat as directional not lab-proven. Whitespark stresses NAP consistency is 'simply vital' primarily for customer-service/conversion reasons — outdated phone/hours directly cost calls. This justifies NAP being a hard requirement of the on-site build (already in our rubric as addressOrMap/clickToCall/hours).

claim: Exact-match NAP must be rendered as real text on-site (footer + contact section) and mirrored in LocalBusiness JSON-LD, not baked into images, so Google and aggregators can crawl a single canonical NAP. This is the one piece of the citation ecosystem we fully control at build time and ship in every site.
notes: Maps directly onto our existing ContactNAP.astro + LocalBusinessJsonLd.astro components and the rubric items addressOrMap, clickToCall, hours, localBusinessJsonLd. Recommend the launch checklist enforce that site NAP, GBP, and JSON-LD use a byte-identical formatted address + phone.

claim: The Canadian citation core (tier-1, free, claim-by-hand at launch) is: Google Business Profile, Bing Places, Apple Business Connect (Apple Maps/Siri), Yelp.ca, Facebook/Meta business page, YellowPages.ca, Canada411.ca, and 411.ca. These are the highest-authority general directories Canadian consumers and devices actually use.
notes: Yellowpages.ca/411.ca/Canada411 are the Canada-specific high-authority general directories beyond the US-default set. Apple Business Connect matters disproportionately for iPhone/Siri/CarPlay searches in Canada.

claim: GBP and Bing Places are cheap to do well because Bing Places can directly import the Google Business Profile (name, address, phone, categories, description, hours, photos) and keep it auto-synced — so claiming/cleaning GBP first and importing to Bing is the efficient launch sequence, not duplicate manual entry.
notes: Workflow: claim/clean GBP -> import to Bing Places -> claim Apple Business Connect (free, businessconnect.apple.com) -> then Yelp.ca/YellowPages/Facebook. This is a strong candidate for a deploy-shop-site skill runbook step. Bing import goes live in ~10-14 days.

claim: US/North-American data aggregators (Data Axle, Foursquare) DO cover Canadian businesses and feed dozens of downstream directories/maps/apps — so a Canadian shop benefits from aggregator accuracy. However Neustar/TransUnion (Localeze) is US-only, and ongoing paid aggregator submission/management is the recurring upsell we explicitly do NOT sell as a one-time shop.
notes: Data Axle has ~1.1M verified Canadian business addresses; Foursquare powers Apple/Uber/etc. Aggregator submission is subscription-based (e.g. ex-Localeze ~$99/yr/listing) and only US — a poor fit for our one-time, client-owns-accounts model. Keep it OUT of scope and out of the deliverable.

claim: Vertical/industry directories meaningfully boost relevance per niche and should be claimed once at launch where they exist for the shop's category — but they are vertical-specific, so the launch deliverable should pick the 2-4 relevant ones rather than a generic list.
notes: Restaurant/cafe: TripAdvisor, OpenTable, Zomato, (delivery: Uber Eats/DoorDash/SkipTheDishes in Canada). Dental/health: RateMDs (2.6M+ reviews, strong in Canada), Healthgrades, Zocdoc. Law: Avvo/FindLaw (note Avvo is US-leaning; in Canada lean on provincial Law Society + Lawyer Referral). Auto: CarGurus, plus manufacturer/CAA. Spa/fitness/retail: Yelp + niche booking platforms. Trades: HomeStars (Canada), BBB, Houzz.

claim: Review-platform listings (Google reviews first, then Yelp.ca and the vertical review site for the niche) are now a higher-leverage local factor than generic citation volume — reviews rose to ~16-20% of local pack weight while raw citation quantity is steady/flat. So the launch deliverable should prioritize claiming review-capable profiles over chasing long lists of low-value directories.
notes: Whitespark/BrightLocal both show reviews growing and citation quantity NOT a strong lever. Practical: our on-site Reviews.astro should link out to the GBP/Yelp review profile; we set up the profiles at launch so the client can request reviews themselves (fits client-owns-accounts model).

claim: Chasing high citation VOLUME (hundreds of low-authority directories) is low-impact and is the classic recurring 'citation management' retainer that does not move rankings for a small Calgary shop — Whitespark explicitly counters the volume narrative, emphasizing quality/relevance and accuracy over quantity. This is the work we should explicitly NOT sell.
notes: Defines the scope boundary: one-time launch = consistent on-site NAP + JSON-LD, GBP claim/cleanup, Bing import, Apple Business Connect, Yelp.ca + ~6-8 tier-1 Canadian directories + 2-4 vertical directories. OUT of scope (recurring, declined): paid aggregator subscriptions, ongoing citation auditing/cleanup retainers, mass low-DA directory blasts.

claim: AI-search/LLM visibility is the one area where citations are rising in weight (BrightLocal puts citation signals at ~13% for AI search visibility, tied 3rd), because assistants pull structured/unstructured business mentions. This strengthens the case for accurate NAP + a clean handful of authoritative citations at launch, but still does NOT justify an ongoing volume program.
notes: Useful sales framing: 'accurate NAP + clean tier-1 citations also feed AI assistants (ChatGPT/Apple/Siri)' is true and future-proof, and is fully achievable in a one-time deliverable. Reinforces shipping JSON-LD and consistent NAP rather than retainer work.

claim: Review signals are the third-largest local-pack ranking factor category in Whitespark's 2023 Local Search Ranking Factors survey at ~16% of weight (behind GBP signals ~32% and on-page ~19%), and most local-SEO practitioners report the weighting has grown toward ~20% since. Reviews materially move local-pack rank, so the site/handoff should drive review acquisition, not just display them.
notes: Whitespark surveys ~40+ local SEO experts across 180+ factors; treat exact % as directional, but the category rank (top 3) and growth trend are consistent across sources.

claim: Controlled case studies show review COUNT has a noticeable ranking effect at low milestones (notably reaching ~10 reviews) but diminishing returns above that; going from 10 to 11 or from 16 to 31 reviews produced negligible local-pack movement. The practical implication for a new client: getting the business from 0-9 to ~10+ reviews is the high-leverage win, not chasing hundreds.
notes: Sterling Sky 2025 update; 3 test cases saw a bump at 9→10 reviews, none at 10→11. Count alone cannot overcome weak proximity/category signals.

claim: Review velocity and recency outweigh raw lifetime count for ranking: a steady stream of recent reviews (within ~60-90 days) is weighted more than a large stagnant total, and practitioners report rankings can decline if review inflow stops for as little as a few weeks. The handoff should set up an ONGOING review-request habit, not a one-time burst.
notes: Recency/velocity emphasized by both Sterling Sky and Whitespark synthesis; 'falls off a cliff after 3 weeks' is a practitioner claim, treat the exact threshold as directional.

claim: Deliberately seeding keyword-rich review TEXT to boost local-pack rank does not work: a controlled Sterling Sky test (6 keyword-stuffed reviews on a low-competition business) produced flat or worse rankings. Owners should NOT be coached to script keywords into customer reviews; ask for honest reviews instead.
notes: Keyword-in-review relevance may act as a tiebreaker in tightly contested top-position races per some surveys, but the rigorous controlled test found no causal ranking lift; review text mainly drives query-matching 'justification' snippets after ranking is earned.

claim: Responding to reviews is both a consumer-trust and a freshness/relevance signal: ~89% of consumers expect owners to respond, 80% are more likely to use a business that responds to all reviews, and responses add Google-indexed text to the profile. Aim for replies within 24-48h (81% of consumers expect a response within a week). A response habit is a free, owner-runnable lever with dual rank + conversion payoff.
notes: Response-as-ranking-signal is practitioner consensus rather than a controlled test; the consumer-expectation stats are from BrightLocal LCRS.

claim: Star-rating thresholds gate conversion sharply: BrightLocal's survey finds a large majority of consumers will only use a business rated 4.0+ stars (reported 55% in 2025 rising to ~68% in 2026), and a growing share require 4.5+. Below ~4.0 stars, ranking gains are wasted because click-through/conversion collapses — protecting rating (volume of genuine positive reviews + responding to negatives) matters as much as count.
notes: Year-over-year figures vary by survey wave; the 4.0+ threshold dominating consumer choice is stable across years.

claim: Self-applied aggregateRating/Review schema for the business itself is explicitly ineligible for star rich results per Google: 'If the entity that's being reviewed controls the reviews about itself, their pages that use LocalBusiness or any other type of Organization structured data are ineligible for the star review feature' — and this explicitly includes embedded third-party widgets (Google Business / Facebook reviews widgets). Shop sites must NOT emit self-serving aggregateRating in LocalBusiness JSON-LD.
notes: Since 2019 Google drops self-serving review stars. Doing it anyway won't show stars and risks looking like spammy/mismatched markup; the audit's localBusinessJsonLd check should flag self-review aggregateRating as a defect, not a feature.

claim: Mismatched or hidden review markup risks a manual 'spammy structured data' penalty that can strip ALL rich snippets site-wide: aggregateRating values must exactly match what's visible on the page, and marking up reviews not visible to users or fake reviews is a violation. The safe default for these shop sites is to omit Review/aggregateRating schema for the business entirely.
notes: Penalty risk is highest with fabricated/mismatched numbers; the conservative omit-it stance avoids both the ineligibility and the penalty surface.

claim: Review/aggregateRating schema CAN legitimately earn star rich results when it is about specific Products or Services the business sells (third-party reviewed), not the business as a whole. For retail (product pages) and some service menus this is a real opportunity; for a single-page LocalBusiness brochure site it usually is not applicable.
notes: Product review snippets are well-supported; service review snippets are valid per schema.org but less reliably shown. Only relevant where the site actually has per-product/service pages.

claim: Live GBP/third-party review widgets keep on-site social proof fresh and auto-updating (no manual editing — fits the no-maintenance handoff model) and well-built widgets load asynchronously/lazily so they need not harm LCP/CLS. Static testimonials go stale and require us or the client to hand-edit. For these sites, a live widget is the better default for the reviews section PROVIDED it is async/lazy-loaded and given a reserved height to avoid layout shift.
notes: Vendor sources (EmbedSocial) so treat performance claims skeptically — third-party JS widgets DO add weight and can cause CLS if unsized; reserve a fixed container height and lazy-load. A curated static testimonial block remains a valid lightweight fallback where CWV budget is tight.

claim: Embedding a live reviews widget does NOT make the business eligible for star rich snippets and does not substitute for off-site review acquisition — its value is purely on-site conversion/trust, not SEO stars (which Google controls in the GBP listing itself). Set client expectations: the stars that matter for the local pack live on the Google listing, not the website.
notes: Reinforces claim 8: widget = trust UI, GBP = ranking stars; do not conflate.

claim: The single most effective owner-runnable review CTA is a direct Google review short link (the GBP 'Get more reviews' link / g.page short URL) distributed via SMS, post-service email, and an in-store QR code. SMS sent ~1-2h after service performs best (95%+ open vs ~20% email); print QR codes should always print the plain URL alongside. This is a zero-cost flow a non-technical owner can run from their phone.
notes: Owner gets the link free inside their GBP dashboard; we can pre-generate the QR and a copy-paste SMS/email template at handoff so it's truly self-run.

claim: Review GATING (selectively routing happy customers to Google while diverting unhappy ones to a private feedback form) violates Google's policy AND the FTC's 2024 fake-review rule (effective Oct 2024, penalties up to ~$51,744/violation) and can get a GBP profile suspended. Hand-off CTA flows must ask ALL customers for a public review unconditionally; we must not build a star-picker that branches negative ratings away.
notes: Critical compliance guardrail — many 'review funnel' vendors sell gating; our build/skill must explicitly prohibit it. Incentivizing reviews with discounts/free goods is likewise banned.

claim: More reviews lift website/listing click-through and conversion even when ranking is unchanged: products/businesses with reviews convert substantially better, with vendor-cited figures around a ~2.8% conversion lift per ~10 new reviews and large lifts past 5 reviews. So review acquisition pays off through CTR/conversion regardless of the count plateau in ranking.
notes: Sterling Sky notes count's value is mainly CTR even where ranking flatlines; the exact 2.8%/10-reviews figure is vendor-sourced — treat as directional.

claim: Review-platform priority is vertical-dependent and the CTA flow should target the platform that matters per vertical: Google is the universal must (all verticals), but dental/health verticals also weigh Healthgrades/RateMDs, auto weighs Google + manufacturer/Cars.com, trades weigh Google + HomeStars/Better Business Bureau, and restaurants/cafes additionally weigh Yelp/TripAdvisor. Handoff guidance should name the 1-2 secondary platforms per vertical rather than only Google.
notes: Consumers consult ~6 review platforms before deciding (BrightLocal). Google is dominant for ranking; secondary platforms are trust/CTR plays. Platform-per-vertical specifics are general industry knowledge, lightly evidenced — flag for re-verification per Calgary market.

claim: Pure service-area businesses (no public storefront; staff travel to the customer) must hide/remove their street address in Google Business Profile and instead define service areas; showing a non-visitable address violates Google's guidelines.
notes: Google: 'If you don't serve customers at your business address, such as for a service-area business, you can choose to not show your address.' For Calgary trades (plumber, electrician, HVAC, mobile detailing) this is the default GBP posture. Hiding the address removes the map pin.

claim: Storefront and hybrid businesses (customers can visit during stated hours) must keep a visible, accurate address in GBP; this is the correct posture for auto shops, spas, dental, fitness studios, and law offices.
notes: Hybrid = staffed visitable location AND travel-to-customer service; address stays visible. Of the five requested verticals, auto/spa/fitness/law are storefront-default; only trades are SAB-default.

claim: Hiding the GBP address removes the Maps pin and weakens proximity ranking signals, so service-area businesses must compensate with relevance (tight categories) and prominence (reviews, local citations, local backlinks) rather than proximity.
notes: Practical consequence: for a Calgary trades client, on-site work (reviews CTA, NAP-consistent citations) matters more than a map embed, which storefront verticals lean on.

claim: For LocalBusiness JSON-LD, a single-location storefront should emit explicit address + geo coordinates; a service-area business should emit areaServed (city/region/GeoCircle) and may omit the public address property.
notes: Our current LocalBusinessJsonLd component requires name+address. For a trades SAB it should switch to areaServed and use a more specific @type (Plumber, Electrician). This is a template gap: the component needs an SAB mode.

claim: areaServed in JSON-LD is implemented as a string, an array of City/AdministrativeArea objects, or a GeoCircle/GeoShape, and coexists with (rather than always replacing) address for hybrid businesses.
notes: Array-of-City form (with @id Wikipedia refs) is the common pattern. Hybrid (e.g., auto shop that also does mobile service) can carry both address and areaServed.

claim: A service-area business serving multiple distinct cities/suburbs structurally benefits from dedicated service-area (city) landing pages, because they can rank organically for 'service + city' queries where there is no storefront pin to lean on.
notes: This is the structural trigger to break our single-page rule: a Calgary trades client targeting e.g. Airdrie, Cochrane, Okotoks beyond Calgary needs a small multi-page IA (one page per priority area).

claim: Service-area / city pages must each be genuinely unique (local conditions, regulations, landmarks, distinct testimonials); duplicate pages with only the city name swapped are treated as doorway pages and can be filtered or penalized by Google.
notes: Mueller: 'You can't swap out the city name or a few pictures and call it good.' Industry rule-of-thumb cited: ~40-60% unique content per page. This is the cost that makes multi-page IA a tier-up scope item, not a free template toggle.

claim: Service-area page count should be capped at the genuine priority coverage zones (commonly cited as ~15-20), not every neighborhood/zip; large auto-generated location-page sets (e.g., 1,300) are doorway pages per John Mueller.
notes: Gives a concrete scoping ceiling: for a Calgary trades SAB, build 3-6 priority-suburb pages, not a programmatic city matrix. Keeps multi-page builds inside one-time-fee economics.

claim: True multi-location businesses (more than one staffed storefront) need one dedicated location page per location, each with its own unique NAP, hours, geo, and its own LocalBusiness schema — a single homepage schema covering all locations is far weaker.
notes: Second structural trigger to leave single-page IA: each location also needs its own GBP, and the location page should be linked from that GBP. Applies to a 2-location auto shop, a small dental group, a multi-studio fitness brand, etc.

claim: Each location/service-area page should be cross-linked with its matching Google Business Profile (page linked from GBP, GBP NAP matching the page) to reinforce local authority and ensure NAP consistency.
notes: Ties the multi-page IA back to GBP; the website page is the canonical NAP target the GBP links to. Single-location single-page sites get this for free (homepage = the target).

claim: The decision rule for our template: single page suffices when the business has exactly one customer-facing location and one primary city; a small multi-page local-SEO IA is structurally required when (a) there are multiple physical locations, OR (b) it is a service-area business genuinely serving multiple distinct cities/suburbs beyond its home city.
notes: Synthesis claim answering the scoping question directly. Maps cleanly to our tiering: single-location storefront verticals (auto/spa/fitness/law/dental/barber/cafe/retail) = single-page default; trades multi-suburb or any multi-location = tier-up to multi-page IA. Most single-shop Calgary clients stay single-page.

claim: Google's LocalBusiness structured-data spec requires only name and address; everything else (telephone, geo, openingHoursSpecification, menu, priceRange, aggregateRating, review) is 'recommended', and Google warns 'the more properties you provide, the higher quality the result.' So a JSON-LD block can validly exist as LocalBusiness while omitting hours/phone/geo — meaning the audit's single localBusinessJsonLd boolean conflates 'has a name+address stub' with 'has the rich local fields Google rewards.'
notes: Rubric gap: localBusinessJsonLd is binary. Recommend decomposing into sub-checks (address present in JSON-LD, telephone present, openingHoursSpecification present, geo present) rather than a single pass/fail, since a bare name+address stub passes today but carries little of the local signal.

claim: The audit's localBusinessJsonLd regex `"@type":"[a-z]*(business|restaurant|salon|barber|cafe|store|shop)"` misses most schema.org LocalBusiness subtypes Google recommends using (the most specific subtype). It will NOT match Dentist, Physician, MedicalClinic, LegalService, Attorney, AutoRepair, AutoBodyShop, HealthClub, DaySpa, BeautySalon, BarOrPub, Bakery, Plumber, Electrician, HVACBusiness — i.e. dental, law, trades, auto, spa, fitness verticals are systematically under-detected (false negatives).
notes: Rubric fix: detect any schema.org LocalBusiness subtype. Either match the literal `LocalBusiness` plus an allowlist of its ~40 subtypes, or (more robust) parse JSON-LD and resolve @type against the LocalBusiness subtype tree rather than a 7-word substring regex. Current substring approach also risks false positives (e.g. matches 'GroceryStore' but also any @type ending in those tokens).

claim: Google's `menu` (and `hasMenu`) property is defined as the fully-qualified URL of an HTML menu page, NOT an inline Menu object — and Google/industry guidance is that most restaurants/cafes should at minimum link a crawlable HTML menu URL, optionally enriched with Menu/MenuSection/MenuItem/Offer. The audit's menuSchema regex only matches `"@type":"menu"`, so it returns false for the overwhelmingly common (and Google-blessed) case of a `menu`/`hasMenu` URL or a plain crawlable HTML menu, misclassifying compliant restaurants/cafes as failing.
notes: Rubric fix: rename/repurpose menuSchema to 'crawlable menu' = (a) `"menu"`/`"hasMenu"` JSON-LD property with an http URL, OR (b) inline Menu/MenuItem schema, OR (c) a visible HTML services/menu list with prices. Also: this check should only count against food verticals; for barber/spa/auto/dental the equivalent is a crawlable service list with pricing, not 'Menu'.

claim: A PDF-only menu/price list is an AI-search and SEO anti-pattern: guidance is explicitly to 'prefer an HTML page over an unparseable PDF' and lists 'using only a PDF for menus' as a common mistake.
