The audit has no signal that distinguishes a crawlable HTML menu/service list from a linked PDF, so a site whose only menu is a PDF can still trip the bookingLink/menu heuristics and look fine while being effectively invisible to crawlers and LLMs for dish/service/price extraction.
notes: Rubric add: detect href ending in .pdf near menu/service/price context and flag as 'menu not crawlable'. This is a positive differentiator our builds can win on (we ship HTML service lists with prices).

claim: Machine-readable openingHoursSpecification (structured day/opens/closes objects) is what lets Google and AI assistants answer 'is it open now?' and power 'open now / near me' results accurately; visual-only hours (a styled table or, worse, an image of hours) are not reliably parsed, and hours are 'one of the most searched and most commonly misconfigured' local fields. The audit's hours regex matches day-name/'hours' TEXT anywhere on the page, so it counts visual-only hours (and even nav copy) as a pass and cannot tell structured from cosmetic hours.
notes: Rubric fix: split into hoursVisible (current text regex, still useful for UX) and hoursStructured (`openingHoursSpecification` or `openingHours` present in JSON-LD). Grade the structured one higher for local/AI discovery. Current single 'hours' pass over-credits visual-only hours.

claim: AI/LLM local answers (AI Overviews, ChatGPT, Perplexity) do not use live GPS/IP geolocation; they depend on explicit, structured business data and cross-reference structured directories as 'truth anchors.' Content with proper schema markup is reported to have a markedly higher chance of being cited in AI answers. This means structured NAP + hours + services on the site (not just visually styled text) is the lever for AI 'near me' visibility — a signal the current inventory captures only as coarse text presence.
notes: The widely-cited '2.5x more likely to appear in AI answers with schema' figure comes from vendor blogs (stackmatix/keyword.com), not a primary study — treat the multiplier as directional, not a hard number. The qualitative claim (LLMs lean on structured data, no live geo) is well-supported.

claim: An embedded interactive Google Map (iframe to google.com/maps or maps.app) carries a location/topic signal and reinforces NAP/GBP consistency, whereas a static map IMAGE (img of a map) carries no such embedded location reference. The audit's addressOrMap regex matches `google.*/maps`, `maps.app`, `` that merely visually depicts a map with no crawlable address or map link.
notes: Rubric fix: separate addressTextCrawlable (real <address> or postal text, ideally inside JSON-LD) from mapEmbed (iframe src to maps) from mapImageOnly (img depicting a map, no link/text). The first two are real signals; the third is a false pass today. Note: an embedded map's CWV/LCP cost is a real tradeoff — flag heavy iframe maps that hurt the LCP<2.5s target.

claim: Crawlable, real HTML address text (or the same address inside JSON-LD) is what feeds Google's entity resolution / Knowledge Graph and AI truth-anchoring; Google's own guidance is to 'only mark up content visible to users' and an address rendered as an IMAGE is not extractable as NAP. The audit's addressOrMap can pass on a maps link alone with no human/crawler-readable street text on the page, so 'address present' is over-counted and image-only addresses go undetected.
notes: Rubric fix: require crawlable postal-address text (regex for postal code / street pattern in text nodes, or PostalAddress in JSON-LD) as its own check, distinct from a map link. Calgary-specific: detect Alberta postal code pattern (T#X #X#) + 'AB' as a strong NAP signal.

claim: tel: click-to-call is correctly the conversion-critical mobile signal the audit checks, but the audit's clickToCall only matches `href="tel:`; it does not verify the phone number is also present as crawlable text NAP (which Google needs for the 'P' in NAP / entity matching). A site can have a tel: button whose number is baked into an image or only in the href attribute, passing clickToCall while contributing nothing to NAP-consistency entity resolution.
notes: Minor gap. clickToCall is a good UX/conversion check as-is; just don't treat it as evidence the phone is crawlable NAP. Add a separate 'phone as visible text matching GBP format' check for NAP scoring.

claim: Citation/NAP consistency between the website and the Google Business Profile is a corroboration signal for the local algorithm (NAP consistency ~6-8% direct weight, but it underpins entity trust and the Knowledge Graph), and exact-match name/address/phone between site and GBP is repeatedly cited as the requirement. The audit inspects the site in isolation and has no GBP cross-check, so it cannot detect the highest-leverage NAP failure mode: site NAP that disagrees with the business's GBP/citations.
notes: Out of scope for a pure on-page regex, but worth a rubric note: the on-page audit can only assert NAP is *present and crawlable*, not *consistent with GBP*. Flag NAP-consistency as a manual/GBP-API follow-up rather than implying the green check means consistent.

claim: Google explicitly recommends using the most specific LocalBusiness subtype (e.g. Restaurant, DaySpa, HealthClub, Dentist) rather than the generic LocalBusiness, because specificity improves entity understanding and rich-result eligibility. A correct site therefore likely uses a subtype string the audit's narrow keyword regex won't recognize, so 'use a specific subtype' (a quality best practice) is precisely the case the current detector penalizes as a miss.
notes: Reinforces the subtype-allowlist fix. Our own shared LocalBusinessJsonLd.astro component should emit the correct per-vertical subtype; check what @type it currently outputs so our own demos don't get under-detected by our own audit.

claim: openingHoursSpecification supports special/holiday hours via validFrom/validThrough and multiple specs; AI 'is it open now' answers and Google's prominent hours display depend on these structured objects, not on visually rendered exceptions. Few small-shop sites encode special hours, and the audit has no concept of hours quality (regular vs special) at all — so 'hours' as a single boolean cannot surface this high-value, low-prevalence gap.
notes: Lower priority. Worth one line in the rubric/build skill (emit openingHoursSpecification with holiday overrides) but not a core audit gate. The structured-vs-visual hours split (separate claim) is the higher-leverage fix.

claim: Google's free local listings and the free local inventory app are available in Canada, so Calgary brick-and-mortar retailers can surface in-store inventory across Google Search, Maps, the Shopping/Products tab, and the 'See what's in store' unit at no cost — this should be a build default for the retail template.
notes: Canada is explicitly in the eligible-country list for both free local listings and the local inventory app. Requires a linked Business Profile location and product approval. Unique to retail; not applicable to service verticals.

claim: OpenTable's Reserve with Google integration is available in Canada and places a native blue 'Reserve a table' button directly on the restaurant's Google Search/Maps listing — so for Calgary cafes/restaurants that take reservations, a native GBP booking button (not just a website link) is genuinely achievable today.
notes: Dining is one of the verticals where Reserve with Google is live in Canada. For low-volume cafes a plain GBP booking/menu link or Google's own free 'Book online' table feature can substitute. Restaurant vertical not in the target subset but cafe inherits the dining behavior.

claim: Reserve with Google for beauty & wellness (hair/barber/nail salon, spa, massage, waxing) is currently US-only with Canada and the UK only on Google's 2026 roadmap — so a Calgary barber or spa CANNOT yet get the native in-listing 'Book' button via Fresha/Booksy and the build default must instead be a plain GBP appointment LINK pointing to the provider's booking page.
notes: This is the most important Calgary-specific correction vs generic US local-SEO advice, which assumes a salon can flip on Reserve with Google. In Canada today the realistic CTA is the free GBP appointment-link field to Booksy/Fresha/Square Appointments, NOT a native Reserve booking button. Watch for 2026 rollout.

claim: A plain GBP 'appointment link' (free field pointing to your own/provider booking page) is universally available in Canada and is the correct booking-CTA default for service verticals where native Reserve with Google is not yet live — it differs from the native Reserve button in that the customer leaves Google to your booking page rather than booking inside Maps/Search.
notes: The appointment link is free, vertical-agnostic, and works regardless of Reserve-with-Google country gating. The shared booking-CTA component should map to this for all appointment-based Calgary shops as the safe default.

claim: For Calgary dental practices, the native 'Book Now' button on the Google Business Profile is achievable via practice-management providers (e.g., NexHealth) that integrate Reserve with Google at no extra cost, redirecting patients from 'dentist near me' searches to the provider's online-booking page.
notes: Dental is treated under health/medical scheduling rather than beauty-&-wellness, so its Google booking path differs from barber/spa. Canada-specific availability should be confirmed per-practice with the PM vendor; default to a booking link if uncertain.

claim: Fitness/wellness class booking via Reserve with Google exists through Mindbody (and similar class-scheduling platforms), letting clients book a class directly from Google Maps/Search; for Calgary studios the practical default is a 'Book a class' link to the studio's Mindbody/Square scheduler, with the native Google class-booking button as an upgrade where the provider supports it.
notes: The Mindbody-Google integration originated as a US pilot; treat the native in-Google class booking as provider/region-dependent and default the build to a clear class-schedule/booking link.

claim: English-only is the correct default for Calgary local-shop websites: there is no legal requirement for Alberta private businesses to be bilingual, and southern Alberta/Calgary has a small French-speaking population with no French commercial district — so EN-only content and schema are market-appropriate and bilingual builds should not be a default.
notes: Contrasts with Quebec (Bill 96 French-first obligations) and federally-regulated entities. For Calgary local shops, skip the bilingual overhead; reallocate effort to reviews/NAP/geo-targeting.

claim: Calgary-specific geo-keyword targeting by quadrant (NW/NE/SW/SE) and by named community (e.g., Kensington, Beltline, Inglewood) materially improves local relevance and should be wired into title tags, headings, NAP service-area copy, and LocalBusiness schema rather than generic 'near me' phrasing alone.
notes: Calgary's quadrant addressing system (NW/NE/SW/SE) is a locally-distinctive targeting axis. Build defaults should expose a neighbourhood/community + quadrant slot in content so each shop reads as hyper-local, e.g. 'Beltline barber' / 'NW Calgary dentist'.

claim: Reviews (steady volume, recency, photo-rich) plus the correct primary GBP category are the highest-leverage local-discovery levers in 2025, outweighing raw citation count and outweighing proximity (which now contributes only ~15% of ranking) — so the build should hard-wire review display, a review-request CTA, and category-aligned copy for every vertical.
notes: Proximity (~15%, down from 25-30% in 2020) is largely uncontrollable, so the site should invest in the controllable 85%: reviews + relevance/category + prominence. The existing audit 'reviews' conversion item is correctly weighted; add review recency as a talking point in outreach.

claim: NAP (Name/Address/Phone) consistency across Google, Apple Business Connect/Apple Maps, Bing Places, and Canadian directories like YellowPages.ca is a foundational, must-have local-discovery factor; fixing major NAP inconsistencies has been associated with ~17% local-pack ranking improvement, and the website's rendered NAP must match the GBP exactly.
notes: The ~17% figure is a vendor study (treat as directional). The build's role is to render a single canonical NAP (ContactNAP component) and emit it in LocalBusiness JSON-LD so the site reinforces rather than contradicts the GBP; ongoing citation maintenance is client-owned per the no-maintenance model.

claim: LocalBusiness JSON-LD with name + address (and where applicable Restaurant/Dentist/HealthAndBeautyBusiness subtypes) is the schema baseline every Calgary shop site should ship; menu schema (schema.org/Menu) additionally applies to cafe/restaurant and Product+Offer to retail, but these are machine-readability aids, not guaranteed rich-result snippets.
notes: Already in the repo's audit inventory (localBusinessJsonLd, menuSchema). Use the most specific schema.org subtype per vertical for relevance; do not over-promise rich results. Product+Offer pairs with the retail free-local-listings feed.

claim: Square (Square Appointments / Square Online) is a strong Canada-available booking+commerce provider spanning multiple target verticals (barber, spa, fitness, retail), making it a sensible default booking/ordering integration to recommend for Calgary shops that do not already use a vertical-specific tool.
notes: Square is among the booking providers integrating with Reserve with Google and operates in Canada. Because native Reserve-with-Google for beauty/wellness is not yet live in Canada, the realistic Square value today is the booking-link CTA plus the provider's own widget, not the in-Google button.

claim: Auto-services (mechanics, detailers, tire/auto repair) are not a native Reserve-with-Google appointment vertical the way salons/dining are; the dependable local-discovery default for Calgary auto shops is GBP completeness + click-to-call + a quote/appointment-request form/link and quadrant geo-targeting, rather than an in-Google booking button.
notes: Reserve with Google verticals are Dining/Activities/Beauty/Fitness/Shopping/Financial/Local Services; auto repair generally relies on call/quote rather than slot-based online booking. Prioritize click-to-call and a contact/quote form for this vertical.

claim: Customers form a credibility verdict on a local-shop site within ~50ms, driven primarily by visual appeal (layout, typography, color) before reading any content — so visual 'dated tells' damage trust before copy can recover via a halo effect. This is the foundational reason an automated screenshot audit of visual markers is valid.
notes: Lindgaard et al. 2006 (50ms first impression); Stanford Web Credibility Project found 46.1% of users justified credibility judgments on visual design — more than content/function. Justifies a visual-only screenshot scoring rubric.

claim: Generic/staged stock photography (smiling models, 'business handshake', salad/laptop scenes) instead of real photos of the actual shop, staff, location, vehicles, or work reads as fake and lowers trust and conversion. Real photos of people are scored more trustworthy and important than stock; A/B tests show real photos converting ~35% higher than stock. This is the single highest-leverage, screenshot-detectable visual tell for local shops.
notes: NN/G eye-tracking: purely decorative stock images are ignored; product/real-people photos are scrutinized. Auditable tell: reverse-image-searchable stock, watermarks, models clearly not the business. Especially damaging for trades/auto/dental/spa where customers want to see the real place/people.

claim: Low-resolution, blurry, dark, distorted/stretched (wrong aspect-ratio), or pixelated images signal low effort and instantly degrade perceived quality and trust. Screenshot-detectable via visible compression artifacts, non-uniform aspect ratios, or upscaling blur.
notes: NN/G lists low-quality imagery and 'high-quality imagery displayed prominently' as opposite poles of design-quality credibility. Stretched/clip-art and dark blurry team photos are repeatedly named.

claim: Default system body fonts used as the brand voice — centered Times New Roman / Arial / Georgia body text, or novelty fonts (Comic Sans, Papyrus, Brush Script) — read as dated and amateur. No display+body type pairing is itself a tell. Screenshot-detectable from rendered typefaces and center-aligned long-form text.
notes: Sources explicitly name Comic Sans/Papyrus and 'old templates from a different era'. NN/G lists consistent professional typography as a positive credibility marker. Center-aligned long paragraphs are a classic dated tell.

claim: 'Wall of text' — long unbroken paragraphs with no scannable structure, headings, or breaks — reads as outdated and overwhelms visitors. Modern users expect scannable, chunked content. Screenshot-detectable as dense text blocks with low whitespace-to-text ratio.
notes: Repeatedly cited as a top dated tell. Pairs with the next claim (no whitespace).

claim: Cramped layout with little/no whitespace and no clear visual hierarchy (nothing guides the eye) reads as cluttered and unprofessional; generous whitespace is a positive credibility cue. Screenshot-detectable via element density and absence of a dominant focal element.
notes: NN/G: 'Strategic use of white space makes content feel organized.' Practitioner sources rank cluttered/no-hierarchy among highest-impact 10-second tells.

claim: Too many competing colors, mismatched/clashing palette, rainbow accents, or washed-out low-contrast color schemes confuse brand perception and read as dated; a coherent, vertical-appropriate palette is a trust cue. Screenshot-detectable by counting distinct accent hues and checking text/background contrast.
notes: NN/G: appropriate color scheme matching business type is a credibility cue; mismatched colors confuse brand. Sources also name dated palette trends (teal-and-orange, washed-out pastels). Low contrast doubles as a WCAG AA failure.

claim: Skeuomorphic styling — beveled/glossy 3D buttons, heavy drop shadows, embossed text, faux-textured backgrounds, busy gradients — is a strong era-marker that reads as ~2008-2012 dated. Screenshot-detectable from button rendering and background texture.
notes: Named explicitly: 'skeuomorphic design with bevels, shadows, and textures mimicking physical buttons.' Strong dated signal but lower direct trust damage than fake photos / wall-of-text.

claim: Non-responsive layout / poor mobile rendering — horizontal scrolling, tiny unreadable text, overflowing images, desktop-only layout shrunk on phone — is a top trust-killer given ~60% of local-shop traffic is mobile. Screenshot-detectable by capturing at a mobile viewport and checking for overflow and legibility.
notes: Complements the repo's existing mobileViewport conversion-inventory check; this is the *rendered* version (audit the mobile screenshot, not just the meta tag).

claim: Tiny tap targets and crowded links/buttons on mobile (below ~44x44px iOS / 48x48dp Android; WCAG 2.5.8 AA minimum 24x24px) cause mis-taps and read as not-built-for-phones. Screenshot-detectable on a mobile capture by measuring interactive element size/spacing, especially the click-to-call and booking CTAs.
notes: Apple HIG 44pt, Material 48dp, WCAG 2.2 SC 2.5.8 = 24px AA min / 44px AAA. MIT Touch Lab: fingertip 16-20mm. Directly affects the conversion CTAs the repo cares about (clickToCall, bookingLink).

claim: Autoplay homepage carousels/sliders/rotators in the hero are an outdated pattern that wastes above-the-fold space: Notre Dame found only 1% of visitors clicked the slider and ~84-89% of those clicks were on the first slide; later slides get near-zero engagement. Screenshot-detectable via slider dots/arrows and rotating hero. Auto-advancing motion also harms WCAG AA.
notes: Strong era-marker plus measured ineffectiveness. The repo engine ships a single static hero — explicitly the right call. Detectable as slider nav dots/arrows in screenshot.

claim: Visible template/builder watermarks or branding ('Powered by Wix/GoDaddy/Weebly', 'Free website provided by…', builder ad bars) signal a free/abandoned DIY site and erode credibility. Screenshot-detectable in footer/header banners.
notes: Tied to 'old templates that look like they're from a different era.' Rarer than the photo/type/whitespace tells but a hard binary signal when present.

claim: Visible typos, spelling errors, and broken/placeholder elements (Lorem ipsum, 'image coming soon', broken-image icons, dead links) on the homepage quickly destroy credibility — NN/G calls typos/broken links a direct degrader of trust. Partly screenshot-detectable (visible text errors, broken-image glyphs, placeholder blocks).
notes: NN/G: 'Typos, broken links, and other mistakes quickly degrade credibility.' High impact when present though less common than stale-design tells; visible portion is screenshot-scorable, the rest needs DOM/crawl.

claim: Intrusive immediate pop-ups/interstitials and autoplaying audio/video on load read as spammy and dated, blocking content and breaking trust on first impression. Screenshot-detectable as a modal overlay/cookie wall covering the hero on initial capture; autoplay audio needs a runtime check.
notes: Named as aggressive pop-ups with hidden close buttons and auto-playing media. Google also penalizes intrusive mobile interstitials.

claim: Stale/outdated visible content — old copyright year in footer, 'Christmas hours' out of season, expired promos, decade-old design — signals the business may be inactive or neglected, reducing trust. Partly screenshot-detectable (footer year, seasonal banners) and the strongest cheap binary signal of neglect.
notes: NN/G: missing/current-content failures 'instantly destroy credibility'; outdated footer year is the cheapest auditable proxy for neglect.

claim: Excessive parallax/scroll-jacking and heavy decorative motion/animation reads as gimmicky and dated, and harms performance and accessibility (prefers-reduced-motion). Hard to score from a single static screenshot — needs runtime/scroll observation.
notes: Named as 'parallax scrolling overload.' Lower trust impact and not reliably screenshot-scorable, so weight it low in an automated rubric.

claim: Users form a visual-appeal judgment of a webpage within ~50 ms (and as fast as 17–500 ms), and that snap judgment correlates very strongly with judgments formed over longer exposure — so visual design is the first thing assessed, before any content is read.
notes: Lindgaard et al. (2006), Behaviour & Information Technology 25(2):115-126: visual appeal assessable within 50 ms; 50ms ratings highly correlated with 500ms ratings. Tuch et al. (2012) replicated aesthetic effects down to 17 ms. Defensible basis that visual quality is judged pre-cognitively and first.

claim: Visual 'design look' (layout, typography, whitespace, images, color schemes) is the single most-cited basis for credibility judgments — referenced in 46.1% of comments in Fogg's 2,684-participant Stanford study, ranking above information structure (28.5%), information focus (25.1%), and company motive (15.5%).
notes: Fogg et al. (2003) 'How Do Users Evaluate the Credibility of Web Sites?', 2,684 completed participants, 100 sites, 10 categories. Verified exact figures from the primary PDF: Design Look 46.1%, Info Design/Structure 28.5%, Info Focus 25.1%, Company Motive 15.5%, Usefulness 14.8%, Accuracy 14.3%, Name Recognition 14.1%, Advertising 13.8%, Info Bias 11.6%. Authors' own recommendation #1: 'Visual design matters--invest here.'

claim: Reliance on visual design for credibility is high across every vertical but strongest for commerce/transaction sites: in the Stanford/Consumer WebWatch consumer study (~4,500 people) visual design drove credibility for finance 54.6%, search 52.6%, travel 50.5%, e-commerce 46.2%, and was still substantial for health 41.8%, news 39.6%, nonprofit 39.4%.
notes: Per-vertical breakdown maps to local-shop verticals: trades/auto/retail/spa/fitness are transactional (commerce-like, higher visual weight ~46-55%); dental/law are closer to health/professional (~42%). Implication: visual quality matters most where money/booking changes hands — all our shop verticals.

claim: Visual quality triggers a halo effect: a good-looking site is inferred to be more usable, more trustworthy, and a better business ('looking good is being good') — aesthetics carry over to perceptions of competence the user has not actually verified.
notes: Tractinsky et al. (2000) 'What is Beautiful is Usable' — aesthetics correlate strongly with perceived usability (Kurosu & Kashimura found r≈0.59 in Japan). Fogg notes the same source-credibility halo documented in social psych since 1939. Reinecke et al. (2013) CHI: split-second appeal judgment 'is influential enough to later affect opinions of a site's usability and trustworthiness.' This is the conversion mechanism: visual quality is read as business quality.

claim: Lower visual complexity drives a more positive first impression: highly visually complex / cluttered pages are rated less aesthetically pleasing than low- or medium-complexity pages, and this effect registers within the first 17–50 ms — favoring clean, generous-whitespace layouts.
notes: Tuch et al. (2012), IJHCS 70(11). High VC → more negative first impression than medium/low VC. Of the two named properties, VC had a more pronounced (and faster, ≤17 ms) effect than prototypicality. Directly supports the repo's 'generous whitespace / don't ship plain-but-also-not-cluttered' design bar.

claim: Prototypicality / familiarity of layout (matching the expected template for the site's category) increases perceived aesthetics and first-impression quality — unfamiliar, non-standard layouts are rated lower, and the effect appears within 50 ms.
notes: Tuch et al. (2012): prototypical websites create a better first impression than less prototypical ones; effect present at 50 ms (less pronounced than VC at 17 ms). Supports a template-engine strategy: conventional, category-recognizable section order (hero → services/menu → reviews → hours/NAP → CTA) is an asset, not a liability — familiarity reads as quality.

claim: Visual complexity and colourfulness are computationally quantifiable from a screenshot, and together with demographics explain roughly half the variance in aesthetic-appeal ratings made after only 500 ms — meaning visual quality is measurable, not purely subjective, and could be added as an audit signal.
notes: Reinecke et al. (2013) CHI: models from 548 raters × 450 sites; image-statistic models of perceived visual complexity + colourfulness + demographics explain ~half the variance in 500ms appeal ratings. The two named measurable properties carrying weight: (lower) visual complexity and colourfulness. The current packages/audit rubric scores only binary conversion items + perf/seo/a11y; it has NO visual-quality metric — a defensible gap to close for outreach (e.g., screenshot-derived complexity/colourfulness score).

claim: High image quality / photography is a named visual property that drives the first impression at the same pre-attentive speed as layout and colour, supporting hero imagery and real photos over generic stock or text-only blocks.
notes: Lindgaard's 50ms judgment is driven by overall visual impression incl. imagery; Fogg explicitly codes 'images' within the 46.1% design-look category. Image quality is one of the four properties the literature consistently names (with complexity, colourfulness, layout/typography). Most relevant to visually-driven verticals (food, grooming, fitness, vehicles, retail); least load-bearing for law/trades where credibility cues lean on proof/NAP.

claim: Professional design is one of the ten Stanford Guidelines for Web Credibility, and is positioned as a credibility lever independent of content quality — i.e., a dated or amateur-looking site loses trust even when its information is accurate.
notes: Stanford Guideline #6: 'Design your site so it looks professional (or is appropriate for your purpose).' Fogg: 'No matter how good a site's content, the visual aspects will have a significant impact on how people assess credibility.' This is the core sales argument for a paid redesign of a dated local-shop site: credibility/conversion loss is attributable to visual quality alone, separable from content.

claim: Body text must be at least 16px (1rem) effective size; long-form reading copy is better at 18px. Below 16px reads as amateur and harms mobile readability. Audit check: computed font-size of body/paragraph text >= 16px.
notes: USWDS (US gov design system) and Google mobile-friendliness both treat 16px as the minimum readable body size; 18-20px recommended for long passages. 16px is the universal browser default. A concrete, checkable guardrail: parse rendered HTML / computed styles and flag body copy < 16px.

claim: Body line-height should be at least 1.5 (unitless) for running text. This is both a readability fundamental and the WCAG 2.1 1.4.12 reference value. Audit check: line-height of paragraph text >= 1.5x font size.
notes: WCAG 1.4.12 Text Spacing sets the canonical numbers: line-height >= 1.5x font size, paragraph spacing >= 2x font size, letter-spacing >= 0.12x, word-spacing >= 0.16x. USWDS independently recommends >= 1.5 line-height for long text. Headings can/should be tighter (~1.1-1.25) for hierarchy. Checkable: paragraph computed line-height >= 1.5.

claim: Line length (measure) for body copy should fall in roughly 45-75 characters per line, with ~66 cpl as the target (USWDS allows up to 90). Full-bleed body text spanning the viewport reads as amateur. Audit check: constrain text columns with a max-width (~60-75ch) so measure stays in range.
notes: Bringhurst's 45-75 cpl is the classic guideline; ~66 cpl optimal. USWDS recommends 45-90 with 66 as a good target. Practically enforced with max-width ~60-75ch on text containers. Eye-tracking (Dyson & Haselgrove) supports a medium measure. Hard to audit purely from HTML without rendering width, but a paragraph max-width token/utility is a checkable design artifact.

claim: Body text needs >= 4.5:1 contrast against its background (WCAG AA); large text (>= 24px regular, or >= 18.66px/19px bold) needs >= 3:1. Low-contrast grey-on-white body copy is a top amateur/dated signal AND an a11y failure. Audit check: compute contrast ratio of text vs background; flag body < 4.5:1, large text < 3:1.
notes: Primary source W3C. Exact pixel thresholds for 'large text': 18pt (~24px) regular or 14pt bold (~18.5-19px). This is deterministically auditable and overlaps the existing a11y rubric — the design dimension's contribution is flagging design-led low-contrast (e.g. muted-foreground used for body) even where it technically passes, and tying the failure to premium/credibility perception.

claim: A professional site pairs a distinct display/heading typeface with a separate body typeface (or uses one well-chosen brand typeface with deliberate weights) rather than falling back to a single browser-default system font (Times New Roman / unstyled Arial). Default fonts signal no brand decision was made. Audit check: site declares a font-display + font-sans (font-family) rather than relying on the UA default serif/sans.
notes: Maps directly to the repo's --font-display + --font-sans tokens. The evidence base for 'pairing strictly beats one font' is industry/practitioner (Webflow, Google Fonts knowledge) rather than a controlled study, hence should-have not must-have — a single strong brand typeface with type-scale hierarchy can be professional. The hard amateur signal is the unstyled UA default. Checkable: theme.css declares non-default font-family on body and headings.

claim: Visual hierarchy should come from a consistent modular type scale (a base size multiplied by a fixed ratio, commonly 1.2 Minor Third to 1.333 Perfect Fourth, up to 1.5), producing clearly distinct heading vs body sizes — not near-identical sizes or ad-hoc per-element values. Audit check: heading sizes follow a ratio off the 16px base (e.g. ~20/25/31/39px at 1.25) and are visibly larger than body.
notes: Concrete numeric guardrail: 16px base x 1.25 -> 20, 25, 31, 39, 49px. A ratio >= 1.2 gives enough step contrast that h1 vs body is unmistakable. Flat hierarchy (h2 only 1-2px larger than body) is the dated/amateur tell. Practitioner-sourced but mathematically precise and easy to encode as type-scale tokens.

claim: Overall visual design (layout, typography, font size, color scheme) is a primary driver of perceived credibility for local small-business sites — roughly 46% of users in Stanford's web-credibility study judged credibility partly on visual design, more than any content factor. This makes the typography/whitespace bar a conversion/trust lever, not just aesthetics. Audit framing: a site that fails the type/contrast/whitespace guardrails above is credibility-impaired regardless of copy quality.
notes: Stanford's primary guidelines page states Guideline 6 ('Design your site so it looks professional') and that people evaluate sites rapidly on visual design alone, but does NOT itself quote the 46.1% figure; that statistic comes from Fogg et al.'s associated study (~2,500-4,500 participants) as reported in the Wikipedia/encyclopedia summaries. Cite the study, not the guidelines page, for the number. This claim justifies WHY the visual dimension is weighted, not a per-site checkable threshold.

claim: Visual design is the single most-cited factor people use to judge a website's credibility — Stanford's Web Credibility study found 'design look' was mentioned in ~46% of comments, more than any content factor — so a local shop's visual polish is a primary credibility signal, not a cosmetic nicety.
notes: Foundational justification for why the 'visual' audit dimension matters at all. ~48% in a parallel survey cited design as the #1 credibility factor.

claim: Visual-appeal judgments are formed in as little as 50ms and act as a 'halo' that colors subsequent judgments of credibility, usability and trustworthiness (Lindgaard et al. 2006) — meaning a shop's visual tier is decided before a prospect reads a word of copy or sees a price.
notes: The halo effect is the mechanism by which a cheap-looking site contaminates perception of an otherwise premium business. Primary academic source: Behaviour & Information Technology 25(2).

claim: Consumers apply a price-quality heuristic — higher price raises expectations of quality, and conversely a cheap/templated visual presentation signals a low-quality, low-price offering — so a visibly budget site caps the price a prospect will believe the business can command (Kurz 2023, Psychology & Marketing).
notes: Core mechanism behind the 'price-point fit' sub-dimension. Caveat from Kurz: raised expectations don't always survive actual experience — but for a website (pre-purchase), the expectation is exactly what governs the call/booking decision.

claim: Greater visual richness/complexity in design is read by consumers as a signal of higher production cost and therefore higher value ('visual complexity = higher production cost' lay belief) — this is the causal lever for theming richer for premium shops, because more apparent design effort directly inflates perceived expensiveness.
notes: Supports the principle: tune visual richness UP for premium-positioned shops. Effort/time cues (more bespoke imagery, motion, type pairing) raise perceived monetary value.

claim: A premium- or higher-priced local business presenting a visibly cheap, generic, or templated site incurs a credibility penalty: the design-price mismatch makes the brand read as unprofessional/unreliable and undermines belief in its premium pricing — this is the strongest 'flag this prospect' signal in the visual dimension.
notes: Direct answer to the cost-of-mismatch question (premium business + cheap site). Highest-value prospects to flag: shops whose service/price tier clearly outranks their current site's visual tier (e.g. high-end med spa or law office on a dated template).

claim: Whitespace/spacing is the most reliable lever separating premium from budget aesthetics: budget sites cram content (banners, promos, clutter) while premium sites give content room to breathe — adjusting generous whitespace is the cheapest, highest-leverage way to raise a shop's perceived tier through the token/layout system.
notes: 'More white space instantly feels more expensive.' Cheap-cue to flag in audits: dense, cramped layouts. Maps directly to spacing utilities in the engine.

claim: A refined, restrained color palette (cohesive/muted tones, ~2 neutrals + 1 accent) reads as premium and intentional, while clashing or overly bright/many colors cheapens a site instantly — so the OKLCH token palette should be tightened (not expanded) to lift a shop's tier.
notes: Maps to the theme.css palette discipline. Cheap-cue to flag: rainbow/clashing palettes or many bright colors. Premium-cue: restrained, harmonious tones.

claim: Real type pairing (a display/serif heading font + a clean sans body) signals premium intent and tonal congruence, whereas default system fonts read as budget/DIY — the --font-display + --font-sans token pair is a direct premium-tier lever, but the font must match the brand's tonal territory rather than be 'luxury' in isolation.
notes: Congruence matters: serif suggests tradition/legacy/high-end (law, fine dining, spa); a trades/auto shop benefits more from a sturdy, legible sans than an elegant serif. Maps to CLAUDE.md's 'real type pairing' design-bar item.

claim: Low-quality, stretched, or generic stock imagery instantly lowers perceived value, while high-resolution authentic brand-aligned photography elevates it — imagery quality is a top premium/cheap discriminator and a per-vertical differentiator (a barber must feel different from a law office largely through imagery).
notes: Reinforces CLAUDE.md's imagery-slot tokens and 'per-vertical feeling driven by imagery, not just text.' Cheap-cue to flag in audits: obvious generic stock or pixelated/stretched photos.

claim: Visual-tier expectations differ by vertical: aesthetic/high-touch services (spa, dental, law, fine cafe) are judged on sophisticated polish and warmth, while trades/auto are judged primarily on clarity, trust signals, and fast problem-solution matching — over-polishing a trades site adds little, while under-polishing an aesthetic-service site is disqualifying.
notes: Directs theming intensity per vertical. For trades/auto, invest the 'visual budget' in trust signals (technicians, vehicles, real job photos, reviews) and conversion clarity over decorative polish; for spa/dental/law, sophisticated restraint and warmth are mandatory.

claim: The reverse mismatch — a genuinely budget shop over-designing into a luxury aesthetic — is a real but lower-frequency risk: heavy luxury cues (deep minimalism, exclusivity, slow reveals) can misprice the offering and feel incongruent for an affordable, high-volume local shop; the goal is 'looks professionally made,' not 'looks expensive/exclusive.'
notes: Practical asymmetry: under-design penalty is far more common and severe than over-design penalty for local shops. For budget/everyday shops, optimize for trustworthy/clear/professional rather than aspirational-exclusive; reserve full luxury minimalism for genuinely premium positioning.

claim: Visual cohesion/consistency (uniform colors, fonts, spacing, and tone across the site) is itself a premium signal, while inconsistent branding page-to-page reads as disjointed and cheap — the engine's single-theme-file, shared-component model structurally enforces this consistency, which is a built-in tier advantage over hand-rolled small-business sites.
notes: Validates the 'one engine, themed per shop / never restyle components per shop' rule as a premium-tier feature, not just a maintenance convenience.

claim: An outdated-looking design (old layouts, dated styling) is a distinct and common signal that erodes credibility independent of content quality — modern consumers equate design currency with business legitimacy, and a dated site is a high-value flag because it reliably under-prices an otherwise capable shop.
notes: Prospect-flagging heuristic for outreach: a dated site on a clearly-established/premium local business is the highest-conversion audit target (large gap between business tier and site tier = clear value proposition for our service).

claim: A clean, professional, credible visual look is a universal table-stakes requirement: users judge a site's visual appeal in as little as 50ms, and a 'clean, professional look' was the single most-cited credibility factor (46.1%) in Stanford's web-credibility research. This applies to every vertical regardless of price point.
notes: Lindgaard et al. 2006 (50ms first impression) + Stanford WCP / Fogg (clean professional look = 46.1%). Halo effect: an appealing site gets subsequent interactions judged less harshly. This is the umbrella justification for the whole visual bar.

claim: Legible, mobile-first visual hierarchy (clear heading/body sizing, emphasized headline, standout CTA, familiar layout patterns like top-left logo) is a universal must-have. Over 60% of web traffic is mobile, so the hierarchy must read on a phone first.
notes: Maps to repo's existing mobileViewport conversion check and CWV/CLS targets. Hierarchy is what makes the headline + booking CTA legible on mobile.

claim: WCAG AA color contrast (4.5:1 for normal text, 3:1 for large text 18pt/14pt-bold and for UI component boundaries) is a universal, objectively-checkable minimum. Text below this threshold is unreadable for low-vision users and reads as low-quality.
notes: Already encoded in CLAUDE.md ('WCAG AA contrast'). Deterministically auditable — good candidate to add as a pass/fail check in packages/audit alongside the a11y score. Large text = 18pt (24px) or 14pt (18.66px) bold.

claim: Real, authentic photography of the actual business (storefront, team, products/work) over generic stock or clip-art is a universal must-have. Real photos drove ~35% higher conversion in a tested case; 83% of consumers trust businesses more when they show actual photos, and 88% value authenticity. Generic stock is a named 'outdated/cheap' signal.
notes: The 35%/83%/88% figures come from vendor/agency blogs (CXL cites a real test) — treat as directional, not certified. Prevalence flagged common-but-missing because prospects frequently DO use stock; supplying real photos is a core differentiator. CLAUDE.md already mandates 'real photography over stock/clip-art' via hero imagery slots.

claim: A single, consistent brand color system (one primary applied consistently, not random per-section colors) plus disciplined typography (2-3 typefaces max, ideally a display+body pairing) is a universal must-have. Too many fonts/colors reads as chaotic and amateur.
notes: Directly maps to the repo's OKLCH token system (one theme.css per shop = one consistent palette) and the --font-display + --font-sans pairing. The 'never restyle components per shop' rule enforces color consistency by construction.

claim: Generous, intentional whitespace (uncrowded layout, breathing room) is a universal must-have; cramped/cluttered layouts are an explicitly named 'outdated' and low-credibility signal that reduces comprehension.
notes: CLAUDE.md design bar already lists 'generous whitespace'. Lack of whitespace is one of the listed cues that scream 'outdated'.

claim: A favicon and a present logo are universal must-haves: a custom favicon signals a polished, legitimate site, and a MISSING favicon makes users question credibility. Favicons also appear in Google mobile/desktop search results, affecting CTR.
notes: Already in the repo's 12-item conversion inventory as 'favicon'. Trust-signal claim ('missing favicon makes users question credibility') is anecdotal/vendor-sourced but consistent across sources. Cheap to ship, frequently absent on prospect sites — high-leverage.

claim: No broken or default-template chrome (placeholder text, 'Home | About' demo content, builder watermarks, default theme fonts/colors, dead/lorem links) is a universal must-have. Default template appearance and untouched stock chrome are explicit 'amateur/outdated' tells that erode credibility.
notes: 68% of businesses say website design impacts their credibility (Midas/secondary). The repo's 'one engine, themed per shop' approach inherently avoids generic-template chrome IF theme.css is genuinely customized — the risk is shipping an under-themed site that still looks default.

claim: A modern, non-dated overall aesthetic that fits the business's price point is a universal must-have. Outdated typography, low-quality images, cramped layouts, and buttons that don't stand out collectively make a business 'look smaller, less active, or less credible than it really is' — and a premium-priced shop with a cheap-looking site creates a credibility mismatch.
notes: This is the 'dated vs premium' and 'price-point fit' part of the dimension. Directly supports the scoping rubric's D-F 'dated look hurting credibility' rebuild trigger. Per-vertical FEELING (barber != law office) is achieved via tokens+imagery, not per-component restyling.

claim: Per-vertical visual differentiation (a barber feeling different from a cafe from a law office) is a should-have achieved through tokens (color, radius, font) and imagery — not a universal hard gate, but expected to clear the design-quality bar. It is reused on every shop but its EXPRESSION is situational per vertical.
notes: Mechanism is universal (every shop gets a tailored theme.css), the output is vertical-specific. Distinct from a hard must-have because a competent-but-generic-modern look still ships acceptably; vertical character is what elevates it.

claim: Subtle, purposeful motion (hover states, sticky nav, gentle transitions) is a should-have polish layer — and when present it MUST respect prefers-reduced-motion (WCAG 2.3.3). Excessive/decorative animation is a NICE-to-have that often backfires (reads amateurish, harms vestibular-sensitive users).
notes: Split point: hover/sticky-nav micro-polish = should-have (CLAUDE.md lists 'subtle transitions, hover states, sticky nav'). Elaborate animation = optional and risk-bearing. The prefers-reduced-motion clause is the only HARD part of this claim (accessibility), not the motion itself.

claim: Custom illustration, bespoke iconography, and elaborate photography art-direction are NICE-to-have polish, not table-stakes. A consistent built-in icon set (e.g. the repo's inline-SVG Icon.astro) and well-chosen real photos clear the bar without custom artwork.
notes: These are the 'genuinely optional' end of the split. Repo ships Icon.astro (consistent dependency-free icons) which satisfies the iconography need without bespoke work. Custom illustration is differentiating but not a ship gate.

claim: Dark themes are a niche/situational stylistic choice, not a universal requirement. They suit some verticals (nightlife, certain fitness/auto/barber brands) and fail others (most cafes, dental, law, spa lean light/airy). Choice is brand-driven, not a quality bar.
notes: Listed verticals are illustrative of where dark themes can fit; not prescriptive. A dark theme must still pass AA contrast. Implemented purely via theme.css tokens, so it's a per-shop palette decision, not engine work.

claim: Hero imagery / a real visual masthead (photography or strong brand visual above the fold) is effectively a universal must-have for a local shop: it carries the 50ms first impression and the authenticity signal, and 'flat blocks with default system fonts' is the named anti-pattern.
notes: Repo Hero.astro provides a full-bleed image masthead slot. The first-impression evidence applies most strongly above the fold, which is why hero imagery sits in the must-have tier rather than nice-to-have.

claim: Low-contrast text is the single most common visual-design failure on the web, present on ~79% of home pages, with ~30 distinct instances per page on average — making it the highest-frequency visual problem to detect and fix.
notes: WebAIM Million 2025: low contrast text on 79.1% of homepages, avg 29.6 instances/page; it is the #1 detected accessibility/visual error 5 years running. Directly checkable: audit should programmatically flag text/background pairs failing WCAG AA 4.5:1 (3:1 large). The build defaults already enforce WCAG AA contrast via the OKLCH token system, so this is both a high-frequency fix AND a built-in differentiator if competitors' default-template sites fail it.

claim: 94.8% of homepages have detectable WCAG failures, and 96% of all errors fall into six categories (low contrast, missing alt text, missing form labels, empty links, empty buttons, missing document language). A site that clears these six clears the vast majority of real-world visual/accessibility defects.
notes: WebAIM Million 2025: 94.8% fail WCAG 2 A/AA; avg 51 errors/page. The six checks (contrast 79.1%, missing alt 55.5%, missing form labels 48.2%, empty links 45.4%, empty buttons 29.6%, missing doc lang 15.8%) are individually scriptable. Audit prioritization: weight these six. Build defaults (semantic HTML, alt text fields in content model, labelled forms, lang attr) should make passing all six the baseline.

claim: Poor mobile/non-responsive layout is one of the most common and most damaging visual problems for local-business sites, since the majority of local and 'near me' traffic is mobile (~57% of local searches, ~70-88% of 'near me' searches on mobile).
notes: Mobile is the primary surface for local-shop visitors (57% local searches mobile; 88% of 'near me' on mobile). Reported figures for SMB sites lacking mobile optimization are high but vary widely by source (one widely-cited 91% figure is poorly sourced — treat skeptically). Conservative framing: poor-mobile is common and high-impact. Audit already checks mobileViewport; should ALSO visually verify mobile rendering (tap targets, no horizontal scroll, legible font sizes), not just the meta tag. Build is mobile-first by default.

claim: Visual design dominates first impressions and credibility judgments far more than content: users form an aesthetic verdict in under 50 milliseconds, and the majority of trust/credibility judgments are driven by design, not copy. This is the core reason the visual dimension is high-leverage.
notes: Google/Reinecke: aesthetic judgments form in 17-50ms. Stanford Web Credibility: ~46% of consumers assess credibility partly on overall visual design (largest single factor cited). This is a justification claim for the dimension's weighting, not a per-site checkable defect. The widely-quoted '94% of first impressions are design-related' traces to a small Northumbria/Sillence trust study, not Stanford — cite carefully.

claim: Generic or low-quality stock imagery is a common credibility-killer on local-shop sites; authentic photos of the real business/people/owners measurably outperform stock (real-photo swaps have produced ~35% conversion lifts in case studies), and users' eyes skip obvious stock 'filler' images entirely.
notes: Eye-tracking (Nielsen) shows real-people photos get scrutinized while stock filler is ignored; MarketingExperiments-style case: ~35% conversion lift swapping stock for real founder/employee photos. Impact is largest in trust-heavy verticals (dental, law, spa) and 'see-the-craft' verticals (barber, cafe, auto, trades). Audit should visually flag obvious generic stock. Build implication: the content model already has imagery slots — guidance should push clients toward real photos; default demo imagery should be vertical-appropriate and look intentional, never canned corporate-handshake stock.

claim: Default/unmodified template themes and DIY-builder defaults make local-shop sites look generic and interchangeable — a distinct premium signal (custom brand color + type pairing + intentional layout) is therefore RARELY present and strongly differentiating.
notes: Strong hard prevalence numbers for 'unmodified template' are scarce (couldn't find a rigorous survey quantifying %), so prevalence rated 'common' on qualitative/practitioner evidence rather than a single survey. This is precisely the gap the repo's token-system thesis targets: per-vertical color/radius/font + real type pairing is the differentiator vs builder defaults. Treat the differentiation value as the load-bearing point, not a precise statistic.

claim: Inconsistent or absent brand identity (no consistent signature color, weak/absent logo, mismatched colors across pages) is a common defect; consistent brand color materially boosts recognition (the often-cited '+80%' figure is real but originally about color-vs-monochrome processing, not branding specifically).
notes: Important caveat captured for honesty: the '80% brand recognition' Loyola/Hoadley claim is widely misquoted; the underlying study is about color improving information processing/comprehension, not an 80% branding lift. Use the directional point (consistent signature color aids recognition) without overstating the stat. Build implication: a single OKLCH --primary applied consistently via tokens guarantees brand-color consistency by construction, which is the differentiator.

claim: Cluttered visual hierarchy / high visual complexity reduces perceived quality and conversion: users judge high-complexity layouts as less appealing even when familiar, and cleaner navigation/hierarchy measurably lowers bounce and raises task success.
notes: Google/Reinecke: high visual complexity -> lower perceived beauty even when prototypical. Practitioner data cited: better navigation can cut bounce 10-15% and raise task success up to 40% (secondary, treat as directional). Audit: flag cramped sections, too many fonts/colors, no clear single primary CTA above the fold. Build: generous whitespace + one primary CTA per section is already a design-bar rule — this is a differentiator since clutter is the common default.

claim: Slow-loading / heavy pages are a common visual-experience failure on mobile, where most local visitors are: only ~33% of sites pass all Core Web Vitals and LCP is the top failure (~58%), with mobile pages averaging ~8.6s — well past the 3s abandonment threshold.
notes: Bridges visual and perf dimensions (slow image-heavy heroes are a visual-quality choice with perf cost). Only ~33-43% pass CWV; LCP worst (~58% fail). Build already targets LCP<2.5s via static Astro output + the repo's stated CWV targets, so shipping fast static sites with optimized hero images is a differentiator vs builder bloat. Audit already grades perf — visual checks should account for hero-image weight as both an aesthetic and LCP factor.

claim: Default system fonts / no real type pairing (display + body) is a common 'plain' tell, while an intentional, readable type pairing is a rarely-present premium signal — making typography a high-differentiation, low-cost build default.
notes: Direct survey quantification of 'default-font' prevalence is thin (rated common-but-missing on practitioner consensus, not a hard %). The repo already encodes --font-sans + --font-display tokens and the design bar mandates real type pairing, so this is a cheap, near-automatic differentiator. Audit should note when a site uses only a default system stack with no display face / hierarchy. Lower impact than contrast/mobile but high polish-per-effort.

claim: Visual/brand quality materially drives business outcomes for local-shop sites: users form an aesthetic first impression in ~50ms that correlates strongly with their 500ms judgment (halo effect), and in Stanford's credibility study ~46% of users justified a site's credibility by its visual design above content. This justifies adding a 'visual/brand' category to the existing perf/SEO/a11y/conversion rubric rather than treating look-and-feel as out of scope.
notes: Lindgaard et al. 2006 (Behaviour & IT 25:115-126): 50ms ratings correlate with 500ms ratings. Stanford Web Credibility (Fogg): design appearance is the dominant credibility cue; ~46.1% of users cite visual appearance.

claim: A set of validated, fully-computable image metrics can be extracted from a single rendered screenshot to proxy visual quality: colourfulness, visual clutter (feature/subband-entropy congestion), edge/contour density and congestion, figure-ground contrast, symmetry, and color-cluster count. In Miniukovich & Oulasvirta's studies these six automatic metrics explain ~36-51% of human aesthetics ratings and ~40-50% of perceived-complexity ratings. This is the empirical basis for a deterministic 'visual' sub-score.
notes: Metrics take a GUI screenshot as input and apply to any UI. Reported variance explained: ~36% aesthetics / ~40% complexity (Miniukovich 2014-15); up to ~51% aesthetics / ~50% complexity in later validation; an AGUI-style set explains up to 49% of webpage aesthetics variance.

claim: An open, reusable implementation already exists: Aalto Interface Metrics (AIM, interfacemetrics.aalto.fi, Python, Apache-licensed on GitHub) runs ~17 empirically-validated metrics on a screenshot captured via Selenium/Chrome — including colourfulness, contour density/congestion, figure-ground contrast, subband entropy, feature congestion, white-space, grid quality, symmetry, saliency, and PNG/JPEG file size (file size is itself a clutter proxy). The project's Playwright pipeline already produces the screenshots AIM-style metrics need, so this is implementable without new infrastructure.
notes: AIM = Oulasvirta et al., UIST 2018. Metrics operate on a Selenium/Chrome screenshot; the repo can be run locally or its individual metric formulas reimplemented in the audit package. Maps cleanly onto the repo's existing 'Playwright screenshots' verify stage.

claim: Web-font and font-pairing usage is a cheap, deterministic credibility/dated-vs-premium proxy detectable from the page itself (DOM/CSS), not a screenshot: presence of @font-face, Google Fonts API, Adobe Fonts/Typekit, or a curated system-font stack signals deliberate type choice, while reliance on bare Times New Roman/Arial default browser fonts correlates with dated/templated builds. Wappalyzer-class detection already does this; the audit can detect web-font links/@font-face and count distinct font-families (too many = amateur, 1-2 display+body = intentional).
notes: Caveat: system-font stacks (which modern, performance-minded sites use deliberately) are NOT fetched and so are invisible to network/DOM font detection — a fast, well-designed site can look 'font-less' to a crawler. So font presence is a positive signal but its absence is ambiguous; pair with the vision-LLM check. Aligns with the repo's own --font-display/--font-sans token requirement.

claim: Color-palette discipline is computable from the screenshot and discriminates premium from amateur design: extract dominant colors via k-means/median-cut quantization and count distinct prominent hues. A tight, intentional palette (≈2-5 dominant colors plus neutrals, consistent with the repo's OKLCH token set) reads as premium; a high distinct-color count / high colourfulness reads as cluttered or templated. This maps directly onto the repo's token philosophy and is a concrete deterministic sub-metric.
notes: Colourfulness and 'number of dominant colours / colour depth' are among the AIM/Miniukovich metrics that load on the aesthetics regression. Implement with sharp + a quantizer in Node, or AIM's color-cluster metric.

claim: Whitespace / visual-density is a strong, computable proxy: edge-density and text/element-coverage ratio from the screenshot quantify how 'crammed' a page is, and generous whitespace correlates with higher perceived aesthetics and the premium feel the repo's design bar demands. This is one of the highest-loading factors in the GUI-aesthetics regressions and is straightforward to compute (Canny/Sobel edge density, foreground-pixel ratio).
notes: Edge congestion / contour density and white-space are explicit AIM metrics; 'Relationship Between Visual Complexity and Aesthetics of Webpages' (CHI 2020) confirms the complexity-aesthetics link for webpages specifically.

claim: Image quality is partly checkable deterministically and partly needs vision: raw resolution, intrinsic-vs-displayed dimensions (upscaling/blur), aspect-ratio distortion, and missing/oversized hero images are computable from the DOM + bytes, and EXIF/IPTC fields plus reverse-image-search can flag generic stock photography. Authentic, high-resolution, non-stock imagery is a premium signal for local shops; pixelated or obviously-stock photos read as cheap.
notes: Stock detection: check IPTC copyright fields + reverse image search (TinEye/Google/Bing) or an AI classifier (Ximilar Stock Photo AI). Caveat: reverse-image-search is rate-limited / needs external APIs, and 'stock photo present' is not strictly a defect — judgment of fit is better left to the vision-LLM. Photography-led verticals (food, salon, gym) are most affected.

claim: Color-contrast (WCAG 1.4.3) is the one accessibility-adjacent visual metric that automated tools detect reliably, so it can anchor the 'visual' category's deterministic floor. Automated engines (axe-core) catch ~57% of WCAG issues overall and contrast is among the well-covered categories. Failing AA contrast is both an a11y defect and a 'looks amateur' visual signal, giving a high-confidence, hard-fail sub-check.
notes: Contrast can be computed from rendered colors without a screenshot (axe-core / Lighthouse a11y already run in the repo's PSI probe). This is the most accuracy-trustworthy single visual sub-signal. Repo already mandates WCAG AA in CLAUDE.md.

claim: A vision-LLM screenshot critique with a fixed rubric is the most accurate available method for the subjective half of visual/brand quality and should be the primary scorer, with computable proxies as cheap pre-filters/sanity checks. UICrit (UIST 2024) shows a hierarchical rubric — aesthetics (1-10), usability sub-scored as learnability+efficiency (5-pt) and overall (1-10), rolled into an overall design-quality score — and that few-shot visual prompting raises LLM critique validity ~55% over zero-shot. The repo's existing Playwright→Claude path can run this directly.
notes: Concrete method: feed mobile+desktop screenshots to Claude with a fixed rubric (palette discipline, type pairing/hierarchy, whitespace/balance, imagery quality, depth/polish, dated-vs-premium, price-point fit), require numeric sub-scores 1-5 + a one-line justification each, map the average to the A-F grade. Ground it with 1-2 few-shot exemplars (a known-good and known-bad shop screenshot) per UICrit's finding.

claim: Vision-LLM design scores must be treated as noisy and bias-prone, not authoritative: UICrit reports zero-shot LLM design comments were only ~13% valid and even the best few-shot setup (0.48) stayed well below human critique quality (0.75) and still hallucinated. Mitigations for the rubric: prefer pairwise comparison over absolute 1-10 (UI-Bench uses pairwise + TrueSkill because absolute scores are unstable), fix temperature low, average multiple runs, anchor with calibrated exemplars, and report the visual category as lower-confidence than perf/SEO/a11y.
notes: UI-Bench (2508.20410) scores designs by pairwise VLM preferences converted to TrueSkill ratings rather than asking for absolute scores. For a single-site audit, a practical analog is to score the prospect against fixed reference screenshots ('better/worse than this B-grade exemplar?') to stabilize output. Known VLM biases: position/verbosity/self-preference — randomize order, cap response length.

claim: The recommended concrete scoring method for the 'visual/brand' rubric category is a two-tier hybrid: (1) a deterministic floor from computable proxies — AA contrast pass, palette-discipline band (2-5 dominant colors), web-font/type-pairing present, whitespace/edge-density in target band, no upscaled/blurry hero, mobile viewport — and (2) a vision-LLM rubric critique on the Playwright mobile+desktop screenshots producing 1-5 sub-scores across ~6 dimensions. Combine into an A-F grade weighted toward the deterministic floor for hard defects and the LLM for taste, and flag the category as 'partial/lower-confidence'. This mirrors the existing computeGrade pattern (per-category grade, structural hard-caps, confidence flag).
notes: Fits packages/audit/src/rubric.ts: add byCategory.visual computed from a deterministic sub-inventory (like the 12-item conversion inventory) plus an LLM sub-score; keep the confidence:'partial' mechanism since no proxy explains >~50% of human aesthetics variance and LLM critique is sub-human. Hard-fail visual to D on AA-contrast failure or non-mobile, analogous to the existing cwvPass/structural caps.

claim: Per-vertical 'price-point fit' should be an explicit vision-LLM rubric dimension, because the correct visual register differs by vertical (a spa/law office should read calm/premium; a barber/auto/trades shop should read rugged/utilitarian) and a mismatch — over-slick trades site or sterile barber — is itself a defect that no single computable metric captures. Stanford's credibility work frames this as 'design appropriate for your purpose,' not maximal polish.
notes: Stanford guideline: site should look 'appropriate for its purpose'; Fogg notes appealing-but-not-too-slick. Implement by passing the detected/known vertical into the LLM prompt and asking it to score register-fit, since this is exactly the per-vertical feeling the repo's design bar demands. Marked 'rare' as a measured signal because almost no audit tool checks register-fit today (common-but-missing in practice).

claim: Personal-care service verticals (barber, cafe, spa) should ship a full itemized fixed per-item price list, because customers browse the price/menu before booking and each line item is a low-variance, standardized service; the price list doubles as the booking entry point. Engine default: render every ShopService with a populated, fixed `price` string (no ranges, no 'call for quote') and pair each line with a per-service book CTA.
notes: Booksy: the online price list 'is what clients will browse before booking' and each service should have a clear Select button that opens online booking. The engine's ShopService.price is already optional-string and Services.astro doubles as the cafe/restaurant menu, so this is a content-default, not a code change.

claim: Cafe/restaurant menus must publish a fixed price next to every item (not ranges) and render as in-page HTML, never a PDF; local diners check the menu (including price) before visiting or ordering. Note: online-ordering/delivery prices may legitimately differ from in-house prices due to platform commissions (15-30%), so the published web menu reflects in-house prices.
notes: Reconfirms the settled HTML-not-PDF menu finding from the restaurant/retail anatomy research; adds that fixed per-item prices are the norm and that delivery pricing can diverge from in-house. Avoid reusing refuted '58% more orders' / 'PDF SEO alone' stats.

claim: High-consideration professional/trade verticals (trades, dental, law, auto) should NOT publish a full fixed per-item price list as the primary pattern; the right granularity is ranges / 'starting at' anchors plus a consultation, estimate, or quote CTA, because the true price depends on a site visit, diagnosis, case, or treatment plan and a hard per-item price either misleads or exposes the business. Engine default for these verticals: omit per-line `price`, show optional 'from $X' range copy where a meaningful anchor exists, and lead with a consultation/estimate CTA.
notes: Distinct from the barber/cafe/spa default. The engine already supports this: ShopService.price is optional, so a service line can render name+description with no price, and the CTA component carries the consultation/estimate ask.

claim: For trades/home-services, publishing a 'starting at' price or a typical project RANGE (not a fixed quote) improves lead QUALITY by filtering budget-mismatched homeowners early, attracting prospects who value quality work, and improving conversion efficiency of the leads that do come in.
notes: ServiceTitan: listing a starting price or common range reduces time on unqualified leads and attracts homeowners who understand the value of quality work. Estatehub gives the surrounding conversion benchmark context (home services avg 7.8% lead->customer in 2026; phone leads 46%) but does NOT itself address pricing display.

claim: Trades/auto businesses should NOT publish a binding fixed price for work that requires a site visit or diagnosis; a hard quote on unseen work is not legally binding and exposes the business when hidden conditions (rot, panel upgrades, internal damage) change the real cost. The web pattern is range/'starting at' + 'request an estimate' / book-an-inspection CTA, never a checkout-style fixed price.
notes: An estimate is non-binding and may exclude tax/overhead; a quote is the binding finalization and isn't possible until details are ironed out. This is the legal/risk reason the engine default for trades/auto must be range+CTA, not fixed per-item prices.

claim: Auto repair should publish its hourly LABOR RATE and/or fixed prices for standardized/menu services (oil change, inspection, tire rotation), plus transparent estimate practice, while still routing diagnostic/repair work to a written per-job estimate. Disclosing the labor rate and consistent estimates is a primary trust and repeat-business driver. Independent shop labor rates run ~$75-$125/hr (dealers ~$125-$175/hr).
notes: Auto is hybrid: a short fixed-price menu for routine services + a published labor rate + estimate CTA for everything else. Suggests the engine's auto template support a 'labor rate' / 'menu services with price' slot alongside the estimate CTA.

claim: Dental practices increasingly publish price RANGES for high-ticket, search-driven, often-elective treatments (implants, All-on-4, ortho, cosmetic), and this improves lead quality: prospects who contact after seeing prices are pre-qualified and serious, and consult-to-treatment conversion rises because expectations are realistic. Routine/insurance-billed services stay quote/consultation-based. Example: Aspen Dental publishes $3,700-$6,100 single implant, $14,600-$29,900 full-mouth.
notes: Source is a vendor blog with assertions, not peer-reviewed data, and it concedes some prospects self-disqualify (claimed to be low-converting anyway). Treat the conversion-lift direction as reputable-opinion, the Aspen Dental ranges as a concrete published example. Cost is cited as a primary barrier to treatment acceptance (53% of hesitant patients cite cost).

claim: Law firms should make the BILLING MODEL transparent (flat/fixed fee vs hourly vs contingency) in plain language and publish a fixed fee or 'from $X' where the matter is standardized (e.g. simple will, incorporation), but default to a free/low-cost consultation CTA rather than a full itemized price list, because clients want certainty and a fixed fee provides it where the scope is knowable. In regulated jurisdictions (e.g. UK SRA) price transparency for certain services is mandated; in Calgary/Alberta it is a trust/conversion choice, not a regulatory requirement.
notes: SRA price-transparency rules are UK-specific and do not bind Alberta firms, but they evidence that fixed-fee disclosure for standardized services is a recognized norm. For the engine: law template ships billing-model explainer + consultation CTA, with optional fixed-fee line items for standardized matters.

claim: Local retail stores should publish product prices on individual product showcases when selling online or doing click-and-collect, but a 'visit us'/catalog pattern without per-item prices is acceptable when the site's job is foot traffic. Price perception is a top in-store conversion driver, and a perceived too-high store price pushes shoppers to click-and-collect or a competitor, so online and in-store pricing should be consistent. Product pages that show price also need Product+Offer schema (price, priceCurrency, availability, condition).
notes: Builds on the settled retail anatomy finding (catalog+'visit us' vs buy-buttons vs full e-commerce). Adds the pricing-presentation nuance: show prices when transactional, optional when foot-traffic-only, and keep online/in-store consistent. Online retail conversion ~2.86% (browsing) vs in-store impulse-buy strength.

claim: The pricing-presentation default is a per-vertical, content-layer decision the engine should encode as a template default, not a per-shop code change, because ShopService.price is already an optional string and Services.astro renders with or without it. Defaults: barber/cafe/spa = full fixed itemized prices + per-line book CTA; trades = ranges/'starting at' + estimate CTA; auto = labor rate + short fixed menu + estimate CTA; dental = ranges for high-ticket elective + consult CTA; law = billing-model explainer + optional fixed-fee for standardized matters + consult CTA; retail = per-item prices when transactional, catalog 'visit us' when foot-traffic.
notes: Synthesis/engine-default claim. Current ShopContent.vertical only enumerates salon|cafe|trades|retail, so dental/law/auto/spa/barber/fitness are not yet first-class verticals; encoding these defaults implies extending the vertical union and giving each template a pricing-presentation default. 'common-but-missing' = norm exists in the wild but the engine does not yet ship the per-vertical default.

claim: Across all verticals, the universal rule is: publishing a price (fixed item, range, or labor rate) raises lead QUALITY by self-qualifying prospects, while withholding all price signal raises lead VOLUME but lowers quality and conversion efficiency; the harm of publishing comes only from publishing the WRONG granularity (a hard fixed price on variable/site-dependent work). So the engine should never ship 'no price signal at all' — every vertical gets at least a range, 'starting at', labor rate, or consultation-cost anchor.
notes: Cross-vertical synthesis. The volume-vs-quality tradeoff and self-qualification mechanism recur in trades (ServiceTitan), dental (dentalprice.ai, which concedes self-disqualifiers were low-converting anyway), and auto (autoleap trust). The differentiator between verticals is granularity, not whether to show any price.

claim: Homepage body copy of roughly 300-500 words minimum is needed to read as credible and have a chance to rank; pages under ~300 words risk being classified as thin/low-quality and rarely rank for competitive local keywords.
notes: Word count is not a direct ranking factor; it is a proxy for covering intent. The 300-word floor is the practical 'reads as credible' threshold. Our current 12-item inventory checks structural presence but not copy depth — many AI/template builds ship near-empty hero+section stubs that fall under this floor.

claim: A dedicated per-service description (roughly 500-1,000 words on a focused service page, or a substantial paragraph per service when consolidated) outperforms a bare price-only service/menu list for both local ranking and conversion, because it lets the page target the service+location keyword and answer buyer questions.
notes: Our Services.astro currently renders name+price+short blurb. The research distinguishes service businesses (trades/law/dental/auto/spa) where each service can be its own keyword-targeted page, from a cafe/retail where the consolidated list is fine. For a one-page demo, the equivalent is a real 1-3 sentence description per service rather than a bare price row.

claim: A real About/owner-story block with the human(s) behind the business and a real photo is a primary trust/E-E-A-T 'experience' signal that lifts conversion; visible leadership/ownership measurably increases trust.
notes: This is the single biggest copy gap vs. the 12-item structural inventory, which has no 'about/owner story' item. It is always-reused across all verticals (only the tone/length differs). Cited: 64% trust brands more when leadership is visible (Remarqz). Suggests adding an About/Story section + headshot slot to the engine and an audit item 'hasAboutOrOwnerStory'.

claim: Neighborhood / service-area copy that names the actual Calgary areas served and references local context (not just a generic city name swap) is required to rank for 'near me' and area-modified queries; templated location text with only the place name changed is treated as near-duplicate thin content and suppressed.
notes: Most relevant to service-area businesses (trades, mobile auto, law, multi-area clinics) that serve multiple Calgary neighbourhoods/quadrants. Less critical for a single-storefront barber/cafe that ranks on proximity to its one address. The hard rule: unique area copy, never a city-name find-and-replace.

claim: An FAQ block with real, specific questions remains worth shipping as copy even though Google removed FAQ rich-result snippets (Aug 2023 restricted to gov/health; fully removed May 7 2026) — its value is now on-page conversion, featured snippets, voice, and AI-search (GEO/AEO) citation rather than a SERP rich result.
notes: Correction to common assumption: do NOT pitch FAQ schema for a rich-result/star-snippet payoff anymore. Faq.astro should keep FAQPage schema for AI/voice/featured-snippet value, but the copy itself (3-6 real Q&As, 2-3 sentence answers) is what reduces friction and phone-time. Always-reused block; only the questions are vertical-specific.

claim: FAQ copy answering the practical pre-visit questions (parking, walk-in vs appointment, payment/insurance, pets/kids, what to bring) reduces inbound phone load and improves voice-search matching; ~58% of voice searches are local.
notes: Phrase questions in natural language ('Can I bring my dog?') not labels ('Pet policy'). Vertical-specific question sets: dental/spa -> insurance & first-visit; auto -> loaner/wait/warranty; law -> free consult & confidentiality; cafe -> wifi/seating; fitness -> trial/drop-in. The question template differs per vertical even though the FAQ block is reused.

claim: For restaurants/cafes, menu item descriptions plus photos of best-sellers/high-margin items materially out-convert a bare price-only list; online-ordering conversion rises ~25-30% with item imagery and ~84% of diners want to see food photos before choosing.
notes: Vertical-specific (cafe/restaurant). Photograph only best-sellers/high-margin items — 'one eye magnet per category', not every item. Implies the cafe/restaurant menu component needs an optional per-item description + image slot, not just name+price. This is the menu-detail analogue of the per-service-description claim.

claim: Real reviews/testimonials surfaced as on-page copy (quoted words, not just a star number) are the single most powerful trust signal for local businesses and double as keyword-rich content; review keywords are a 2026 supporting local-ranking signal.
notes: The engine already has Reviews.astro (rating) and Testimonials.astro (quotes). The content lesson: ship actual quoted testimonial copy, not just an aggregate star count — the quoted words are both trust signal and indexable review-keyword content. Always-reused across verticals.
