claim: Local trust/credentials copy — licenses, certifications, association/Chamber/BBB membership, awards, years-in-business, warranties/guarantees — is an E-E-A-T 'trustworthiness' content block that lifts conversion, and is most load-bearing for higher-consideration verticals.
notes: Vertical-specific weighting: licensed trades (electrician/plumber), regulated pros (dental/law), and warranty-driven auto need this most; a barber/cafe needs far less. Suggests a 'credentials/trust-bar' content slot. Not in the current 12-item inventory.

claim: Total minimum credible-and-rankable copy for a single-location one-page local shop is roughly 600-1,200 words of real body copy across hero + about + services + FAQ + area/contact, matching the service/local-landing-page range; below ~300-400 words the page reads as a thin brochure and is at risk under Helpful Content quality systems.
notes: This is the headline 'how much is the minimum' answer. 600-1,200 words is the local-landing-page sweet spot; quality/intent-coverage matters more than hitting a number, so the floor is a guardrail not a target. Actionable: add a copy-depth/word-count check to packages/audit so a thin demo is flagged, and brief clients to supply real per-service and about copy.

claim: Always-reused copy blocks (present on essentially every vertical) are: hero value proposition, about/owner story, services-or-menu with real descriptions, hours, NAP/contact, reviews/testimonials quotes, FAQ, and a closing CTA. Vertical-specific copy blocks are: menu item photos+descriptions (cafe), per-service keyword pages (trades/law/dental/auto/spa/fitness), service-area/neighborhood text (multi-area service businesses), and license/credentials/insurance copy (regulated/licensed verticals).
notes: Directly answers the 'always-reused vs vertical-specific' part of the question. Maps cleanly onto the engine: reusable blocks are core ShopContent fields/sections; vertical-specific blocks become optional content slots toggled per template (menu-detail for cafe, credentials-bar for licensed trades/regulated pros, service-area text for multi-area businesses).

claim: Real, business-specific photography (owner, staff, premises, products) measurably out-converts stock imagery on local-shop sites; a real-photo treatment lifted conversion ~35% over stock in tested A/B comparisons, and ~83% of consumers report more trust in companies using actual photos vs stock.
notes: Implication: template should default to real-image slots and treat stock as a temporary placeholder, never the shipped state. Stock erodes the exact local-trust signal the site exists to create.

claim: A genuine owner/staff photo is the single highest-trust image on a local site: BrightLocal's survey of 4,000+ consumers found genuine owner photos trusted most by 46% vs 33% for generic images and 21% for no image, and 79% trust a site more when it shows a staff or premises photo of any kind.
notes: Template should expose an owner/team headshot slot on every vertical; this is the cheapest single high-trust asset a client can supply.

claim: For in-home service trades (plumbing/HVAC/electrical/contractors), an owner/team photo carries disproportionate trust because the customer is inviting a stranger into their home: BrightLocal found a plumbing owner image trusted by 52% (highest of any image type tested) vs 16% for no image.
notes: Trades template should expose a prominent owner/crew slot (with vehicle/branded-uniform shot) above project galleries.

claim: Trades/contractor sites convert primarily on real before/during/after project galleries, organized by project type with city + scope captions; practitioners report real project visuals lifting conversions by ~80%+ because for trades the finished work is the product, and these should sit on the homepage, not buried in a gallery page.
notes: Default trades image slots: owner/crew, branded vehicle, 4-8 before/after project pairs with per-project city+type+scope caption. City captions double as local-SEO signal.

claim: For restaurants and cafes, real food photography is a direct revenue driver: menus/listings with food photos convert 25%+ better than text-only, and on delivery platforms photographed items see 30-44%+ more orders (DoorDash ~44% more sales per photographed item; high-quality photos lift orders ~35%); ~82% of diners say they'd order a dish purely on how it looks.
notes: Cafe template should expose per-menu-item or per-signature-item food-plating image slots, not just one hero. This is the per-vertical photography slot with the most direct conversion evidence.

claim: Barber/salon sites convert on a real results gallery (cuts, beard work, color, different styles) plus shop-atmosphere interior shots; the gallery functions as proof-of-skill that increases booking confidence, and is paired with team/stylist bio photos.
notes: Barber/spa default slots: stylist/team headshots, 6-12 results gallery, interior atmosphere. Results photos are client work, not patients, so consent is lighter than dental/medspa.

claim: Dental and medical-spa before/after (clinical results) photos require explicit, written, purpose-specific patient consent before public website use; a general treatment consent does not cover marketing/website publication, consent must name the publication platform, must be revocable, and (US/HIPAA practice) consent records retained ~6-7 years after the image is removed.
notes: Template should make before/after an OPTIONAL slot for dental/spa, gated behind a consent attestation in the build checklist; default to non-patient imagery (clinic interior, staff, equipment) so a site can ship without any patient photo.

claim: In Alberta, using a client's recognizable likeness (including before/after photos) for marketing falls under PIPA, which generally requires the individual's consent for collection, use, and disclosure of personal information; de-identification (blacking out eyes) is not sufficient to remove identifying features, so consent is still required.
notes: Alberta context per seed (PIPA not PIPEDA). Applies to any client transformation photo: medspa, dental, and fitness body-transformation photos. Default templates to non-client imagery to keep clients compliant by default.

claim: Accounting/professional-office and similar premises-visited businesses depend more on quality interior/premises photography than on owner headshots: BrightLocal found a professional-looking premises inspired 42% trust vs 24% for an unimpressive one, and a generic industry object can outperform a poor building shot.
notes: For office-visited verticals (dental reception, law office), expose a clean interior/premises slot; clients visit so the space itself is a trust signal.

claim: Authentic imagery is now an explicit consumer expectation, not a nice-to-have: Getty reported ~98% of consumers agree authentic images/video are important for establishing trust, a pressure that has intensified as AI-generated and generic stock imagery proliferate.
notes: Reinforces a build guardrail: avoid obviously-stock/AI hero imagery; a minimum viable real-image set beats a polished all-stock set for local trust.

claim: A minimum viable real-image set for any local shop is roughly: one storefront/exterior shot (local recognizability + map/arrival confidence), one owner-or-team photo (highest trust), and one interior or work/results shot (proof of what you get) — sites with no images score lowest on trust (as low as 16-21%).
notes: Concrete MVP slot spec the template hero+section should expose for every vertical: heroImage (exterior/atmosphere), teamPhoto (owner/staff), and one proof slot (interior/menu/results) before any optional gallery.

claim: Retail/product sites convert on real product photography rather than manufacturer-stock product shots, because unique images of the actual product/service are trusted over generic stock (Cornell 2019), and product photos drive the same browse-to-purchase confidence menu photos drive for cafes.
notes: Retail default slots: storefront/exterior, interior/merchandising shot, and a set of real product/feature images (not catalog stock).

claim: Fitness/gym sites convert on real facility (equipment, floor, class) and authentic member/trainer imagery; member transformation (before/after body) photos, where used, are personal information requiring the member's written consent under Alberta PIPA before website use.
notes: Fitness default slots: facility interior, trainer headshots, class/atmosphere. Transformation gallery is optional + consent-gated, mirroring dental/spa before-after handling.

claim: Alberta auto repair/service businesses must hold an AMVIC business licence, and the Automotive Business Regulation (Alta Reg 192/1999 s.11(1)) requires their advertising to conspicuously show the business/trade name exactly as on the licence and (for print and TV) a statement that they hold an automotive business licence under the Act. This makes a visible AMVIC-licence indication a genuine legal display requirement on an auto shop's marketing.
notes: s.11(1) names the licensee name and (print/TV) the licence-holding statement specifically; a licence-number string is best practice but not the literal statutory wording. The shop must also display the physical AMVIC licence and posted shop rates on-premises.

claim: AMVIC enforces 'all-in' advertised pricing for Alberta automotive businesses: any price shown in advertising (including a website) must include all fees and charges except GST, with website pricing disclaimers held to the same standard. So an auto shop site advertising a price must show the all-in price, not a base price plus add-ons.
notes: Parallels the federal Competition Act drip-pricing prohibition (s.74.01(1.1)).

claim: Alberta auto repair shops operate under the Consumer Protection Act: they must give a written estimate, may exceed it only by 10% to a max of $100 without re-authorization, and must provide warranty information in writing. A site that surfaces 'written estimate' and 'warranty in writing' messaging reflects these consumer rights and builds trust, but the warranty itself is a transaction document, not a mandated website element.
notes: Alberta is the only province regulating auto service/repair like sales. Displaying 'written estimates, warranty in writing, AMVIC-licensed' is a strong conversion play unique to auto.

claim: Alberta lawyers are prohibited by the Law Society Code of Conduct (Ch. 4, rule 4.2-1) from calling themselves a 'specialist' or 'expert' (no Alberta specialist-certification program exists) and from superlatives implying superiority ('best', 'top', 'leading'). Law firm websites must instead use 'focuses on' / 'extensive experience in'. This is a hard content prohibition, not a style preference.
notes: Marketing must be 'demonstrably true, accurate and verifiable.' Combative imagery ('we fight hard', 'aggressive') also violates professional-standards expectations.

claim: Alberta law firm websites must not advertise specific settlement/verdict amounts or guarantee outcomes — settlement figures are typically confidential and unverifiable, so 'largest settlement' result pages should be removed. Contingency / 'no win no fee' messaging must disclose that disbursements may still be owed on a loss and the fee percentage; bare 'we get paid only if you win' is treated as misleading.
notes: This is the Alberta 'no-guarantee disclaimer' dimension. Substance content (e.g. 'what to expect after a car accident in Alberta') is the compliant conversion lever, not result-boasting.

claim: Alberta dentists and dental hygienists must be registered with the College of Dental Surgeons of Alberta / College of Dental Hygienists under the Health Professions Act to practise, and advertising must be ethical, truthful and verifiable per the CDSA Code of Ethics and CDSA Advertising Statement (2023). They may advertise services and fees, but professional advertising standards constrain testimonials, guarantees and superlative/comparative claims — mirroring the legal-profession bar.
notes: The 2023 Advertising Statement PDF would not parse cleanly; the exact enumerated prohibitions (e.g. specific 'painless'/'pain-free' bans) should be re-verified against the readable PDF before any client legal copy. Treat 'truthful, verifiable, no superlatives, no guarantees' as the safe default.

claim: In Alberta a contractor who takes any payment before the work is complete is a 'prepaid contractor' and must hold a Prepaid Contracting Business Licence from Service Alberta and post a government bond; the bond compensates customers if the contractor fails to perform. There is no statutory requirement to print the licence number on the website (confirmation is required only 'on request'), so displaying 'licensed & bonded prepaid contractor' is a conversion/trust signal rather than a mandated disclosure.
notes: The licence is a legal requirement to operate that way; surfacing 'licensed & bonded' on the site is the trust payoff. Many trades sites omit it, so it differentiates.

claim: Most Alberta trades businesses must carry WCB-Alberta coverage (account required within ~15 days of hiring a first worker in a mandatory industry), and a WCB clearance letter is the standard proof a hiring party requests before releasing payment. Displaying 'WCB-covered / fully insured / clearance available' on a trades site is a strong B2B and homeowner trust signal, though there is no rule mandating it appear on the website.
notes: 'Licensed, bonded, insured, WCB-covered' is the canonical trades trust cluster. Liability insurance + WCB clearance is what commercial clients screen for.

claim: Massage therapy is NOT a regulated health profession in Alberta as of mid-2026 — the 'RMT' title is currently unprotected and there is no regulatory College yet (proclamation under the Health Professions Act is pending). So spa/massage sites have no mandated registration to display; instead, displaying a recognized association membership (NHPC, CMMOTA, MTAA, CRMTA) and therapists' training hours is what makes clients' insurance receipts valid and drives conversion.
notes: This flips the usual assumption: for spa the trust content is association membership + insurance-receipt eligibility, not a government licence. Re-verify regulation status periodically — proclamation could land and protect 'RMT'.

claim: Alberta's Consumer Protection Act does NOT give a cooling-off / cancellation right for gym/fitness memberships signed at the gym's own premises (a 10-day cancellation applies only if signed away from the business's normal location). There is therefore no Alberta-mandated cancellation disclosure for an in-gym membership, so transparent cancellation-terms and contract-length content on a fitness site is a conversion/trust choice, not a legal requirement.
notes: Contrast with BC, which does mandate fitness-contract cooling-off. Membership pricing must still comply with Competition Act all-in/drip-pricing rules.

claim: The federal Competition Act (as amended by Bill C-59, with private parties able to bring deceptive-marketing applications to the Competition Tribunal from June 20, 2025) governs every vertical's website: testimonials/endorsements must be genuine and any material connection disclosed, performance/efficacy claims require 'adequate and proper testing', and 'drip pricing' (advertising a price that omits mandatory non-government fees) is deemed misleading under s.74.01(1.1). This caps fake reviews, unproven guarantees and partial pricing across all shop sites.
notes: Cross-cutting federal floor: real reviews only, all-in pricing, no untested 'guaranteed results'. Layers on top of every provincial/professional rule. The audit rubric's 'reviews' item should prefer genuine, attributable reviews.

claim: Guarantees/warranties function very differently by vertical and should be templated accordingly: trades and auto can prominently advertise workmanship guarantees / written warranties as a top conversion driver, whereas regulated professions (law, dental) must avoid outcome guarantees, and spa/fitness lean on satisfaction or trial offers. A one-size 'guarantee badge' component would be non-compliant for law/dental.
notes: Design implication for the engine: a 'guarantee/warranty' content slot must be vertical-gated — enabled for trades/auto, suppressed or reworded (no outcome guarantee) for law/dental.

claim: Professional association / accreditation badges are a high-value, low-risk trust slot across all six verticals but with different sources: law (Law Society of Alberta member, CBA), dental (CDSA, Alberta Dental Association, ADA&C), trades (BBB, trade-specific certs like Red Seal, manufacturer-certified installer, Alberta Master Plumber/Electrician), auto (AMVIC, ASE, manufacturer-certified, BBB), spa (NHPC/CMMOTA/MTAA), fitness (canfitpro/NASM/CSEP trainer certs). These boost conversion and, where the membership is also the licence, double as compliance.
notes: Suggests a reusable 'credentials/affiliations' strip component with per-vertical default logo sets. For licence-based verticals (auto/dental/law) it also satisfies the 'is this a real licensed provider' check.

claim: Liability/professional insurance disclosure is a conversion trust signal that varies by vertical: trades ('fully insured + WCB') and auto are where homeowners/clients actively screen for it; law carries mandatory professional liability indemnity via the Alberta Lawyers Indemnity Association (ALIA) but firms rarely advertise it; dental and spa rely on association-backed coverage. 'Insured' messaging is high-impact for trades/auto and largely irrelevant on-site for law/dental.
notes: For trades/auto, pair 'insured' with 'licensed & bonded' / 'AMVIC-licensed'. For law, ALIA coverage is automatic and not a marketing differentiator.

claim: Awards / 'best of' content is a double-edged trust element: it boosts conversion generally, but for regulated professions in Alberta it can collide with the marketing rules — law cannot run unverifiable superlatives, and dental advertising must stay truthful/verifiable, so 'Best Dentist in Calgary' or 'Top Lawyer' badges are risky unless tied to a verifiable third-party award. For trades/auto/spa/fitness, verifiable awards (BBB, local 'best of', manufacturer awards) are safe and effective.
notes: Rule for the engine: awards slot allowed everywhere, but for law/dental the copy must name the verifiable awarding body and avoid bare superlatives.

claim: At the item level, a menu item (cafe/restaurant) should carry, at minimum, name + price + a short description; a photo and dietary/allergen tags are strong second-tier additions but optional. schema.org/MenuItem supports exactly these: name, description, image, offers (price/currency), suitableForDiet (Diet/RestrictedDiet), nutrition (NutritionInformation), and menuAddOn for add-ons/sides.
notes: name+price is the must-have; description/photo/dietary are should/nice. Menu is the most-visited page on a restaurant/cafe site (settled in prior research).

claim: Roughly 40% of independent restaurants do not have a machine-readable online menu, and ~44% of independent restaurants have no website at all — so item-level menu detail (prices, descriptions) is one of the most commonly-missing-but-expected content elements for the cafe vertical, making it a high-leverage default to ship.
notes: Treat exact percentages as directional (single-source/aggregated). The gap itself is the durable claim.

claim: Dietary/allergen tags per menu item are optional and voluntary in Canada, not legally required: there is no law requiring allergen labeling on non-prepackaged (restaurant) food, and nutrition/calorie posting for restaurant items is voluntary federally. Alberta has no provincial menu-calorie mandate (unlike Ontario's Healthy Menu Choices Act for chains of 20+ locations). So for Calgary cafes, dietary tags are a should/nice-to-have trust feature, not a compliance must.
notes: Implication: ship a dietary-tag field as optional content, not a required field; no calorie/nutrition obligation for Calgary independents.

claim: For retail products, Google merchant-listing eligibility requires per-item name, image, and an Offer with price + priceCurrency; availability, condition (itemCondition), brand, description, review/aggregateRating, sku/gtin/mpn, shippingDetails, and hasMerchantReturnPolicy are recommended (not required). The already-settled 'price, priceCurrency, availability, condition' set is the Merchant Center automatic-item-update minimum, a stricter subset.
notes: Per-item minimum for a retail Product card: name, image, price, priceCurrency. Everything else is recommended/optional, including availability for plain rich results.

claim: When a single retail product has variant-based pricing (sizes/colors at different prices) shown on one page, use schema.org/AggregateOffer (lowPrice, highPrice, priceCurrency, offerCount) instead of a single Offer to express a price range. AggregateOffer is for summarizing multiple offers/variants; it is NOT valid for Google merchant-listing rich results (those require a single Offer).
notes: Engine implication: support an optional priceFrom/priceTo on a product to emit AggregateOffer; keep single-Offer items for merchant eligibility.

claim: Per-item retail fields beyond name/price that real product pages need to convert and rank are: at least one (preferably multiple, non-stock) photos, a unique 2+ sentence description (not a copied manufacturer blurb), variants/options, availability, and image alt text. Thin product pages (name + price + copied description, no alt text, blank meta) are one of the most common small-retailer failures.
notes: For a foot-traffic-driving local retailer the description/photo bar is lower than for a pure e-comm store, but alt text + a real photo are still high-impact.

claim: For service-oriented commerce verticals (spa, barber, auto, fitness) the item-level unit is a SERVICE, not a product. The schema pattern is: the LocalBusiness/Organization carries makesOffer -> Offer (with priceSpecification: price, currency, and per-session vs package) -> itemOffered: schema.org/Service (name, description, serviceType, hoursAvailable, areaServed). Service itself does not have makesOffer; the provider holds makesOffer and Service holds offers.
notes: Use the precise LocalBusiness subtype (BeautySalon/HairSalon/DaySpa/NailSalon/AutoRepair/HealthClub) as provider. Service is widely deployed (1M-10M domains) but is NOT a confirmed Google rich-result snippet type.

claim: Service-menu items (spa/barber) should carry name + price + duration, and 2-3 sentence selling descriptions; packages and add-ons should show both per-session and bundle pricing. A salon/spa price list is the page clients browse before booking, so item-level price + duration is the conversion driver, more so than photos.
notes: Add a 'duration' field to the service item shape for spa/barber/fitness; photos are nice-to-have for services, unlike retail/cafe where item photos matter more.

claim: For auto repair, item-level service detail should include honest price RANGES (e.g. 'brake pad replacement $250-$450 depending on vehicle') and turnaround time rather than fixed prices, because parts/labor vary by vehicle. High-revenue services (brakes, oil change, diagnostics, AC, transmission, tires, inspections) each warrant their own page with name+city H1, 300+ words, a tap-to-call and an estimate/booking CTA above the fold.
notes: Auto items map to price ranges (AggregateOffer-like) + per-service landing pages, not a flat priced menu. This is the deepest catalogue case among the service verticals.

claim: The decision between a flat in-page list and a CMS/content collection is driven by SHARED STRUCTURE and per-item editability, not a fixed item count. Astro content collections give type-safe, validated, repeatable item shapes; use them when items share one schema and the client needs to add/edit/reorder them independently. A small fixed menu/price list can stay a flat array in content; a growing or client-managed catalogue (per-item photo, variants, availability) warrants a collection/CMS.
notes: Practical threshold for this engine: flat array in shop.ts (or Storyblok repeatable blok) for <~15-20 stable items; promote to a per-item Storyblok collection/Astro collection when items have photos+variants+availability OR the client edits them frequently.

claim: Item-level photos are most commonly done (and most expected) in retail and cafe, and least commonly done in barber/spa/auto/fitness where the priced item is a service. Real local shops most often ship name+price; descriptions, photos-per-item, and dietary/variant tags are the commonly-missing tier. Low-quality or stock item photos actively hurt perceived professionalism, so a per-item photo slot should be optional and only used with real photography.
notes: Prioritize photo investment: retail product > cafe dish > spa/fitness service > barber/auto service. Better to omit a per-item photo than ship a stock/blurry one.

claim: Catalogue DEPTH should be capped to the conversion-relevant subset, not exhaustive. For service verticals, surface only high-revenue / commonly-searched items at the item level (auto: brakes/oil/diagnostics/AC/transmission/tires; spa/barber: signature + best-seller services) rather than every SKU. Depth that warrants a CMS collection is where per-item richness (photo + variants + availability + description) and client self-editing both apply; otherwise a flat list is cheaper to build and own (fits the no-maintenance, client-self-edit business model).
notes: Aligns with the engine's one-time-fee, client-self-edit model: prefer a flat Storyblok repeatable block clients can edit over a heavy collection unless item richness demands it.

claim: Non-technical Calgary shop owners reliably already possess and can supply unaided the factual NAP+hours core: exact business name, address, primary phone, and opening hours — because most already maintain these in a Google Business Profile. Roughly 64% of local businesses have claimed their GBP, so this data already exists in a structured, copy-pasteable form the owner controls.
notes: NAP+hours map directly to the audit rubric's hours and addressOrMap items. Treat the owner's GBP as the canonical pre-fill source rather than asking them to re-type it. Pulling NAP/hours from the existing GBP both removes intake friction and enforces NAP consistency (the website is the strongest source-of-truth the owner controls).

claim: Owners reliably have a basic, unpolished service/menu LIST (the names of what they sell and usually the prices) and can supply it unaided, but they consistently CANNOT supply per-service descriptive copy — they offer only an 'elevator speech' and 'you may have to help them with the answer.' Per-service descriptions are the single highest-value content that must be AI-generated or coached out of them.
notes: Splits the Services component cleanly: owner supplies {name, price} rows (have-it); we generate the {body/description} per service (coach-it). For cafe/restaurant the menu items+prices are owner-supplied; dish descriptions are generated. This is the boundary that most often stalls handoffs if you ask owners to write the descriptions themselves.

claim: Polished body/marketing copy (hero tagline, About section, benefit-led section headings, CTA wording) consistently must be AI-generated or written for the client. Owners deprioritize it because 'creating content is both time consuming and difficult,' and when they do write it, it is unusable — 'all about them' rather than customer-benefit framed.
notes: Maps to the engine's hero_kicker/hero_subcopy/hero_cta_label editable fields and the h{} headings object. Ship AI-drafted copy as the default content, framed to the owner as 'edit if you want' rather than 'fill this in' — a blank field is a stall; a good draft is a quick approval.

claim: Waiting on client content is THE dominant cause of stalled small-business website projects — projects routinely delayed by months (documented cases of 10-month freezes and clients taking up to 2 years), and tooling vendors quantify a ~67% reduction in stalled projects and ~71% less time gathering content when intake is systematized. The minimal-intake design is therefore the highest-leverage lever on delivery throughput, not a clerical detail.
notes: Directly answers the 'prevents stalled handoffs' part of the question. Implication: the AI build should generate-then-confirm rather than request-then-wait. Every field we can pre-fill (from GBP) or AI-draft (copy) is a field that cannot stall the project.

claim: The highest-leverage minimal content intake collects ONLY what the owner uniquely holds and AI cannot infer: (1) confirm/correct NAP+hours, (2) the raw service/menu list with prices, (3) booking/ordering URL or 'phone only', (4) any real photos they have, (5) 3-5 differentiators ('what makes you stand out') as bullet fragments, and (6) any existing reviews/testimonials. Everything else (descriptions, taglines, headings, alt text, meta) is generated from those inputs. Keeping intake short and asking only relevant questions is what drives completion.
notes: The 'differentiators as fragments' field is the key coaching prompt — owners can give bullet fragments even when they can't write prose, and those fragments are the seed for AI-generated Features/Testimonials/About copy. Short, relevant-only intake maximizes completion vs. a long questionnaire that gets abandoned.

claim: Photos are a partial-have: owners typically have SOME usable photos (often already uploaded to their Google Business Profile) but rarely enough professional, brand-consistent imagery to fill a hero + gallery + per-section needs. Stock photography is the standard, accepted gap-filler because most small businesses lack the budget for a brand shoot.
notes: Pull existing photos from the owner's GBP as a first source; budget for curated stock to fill the hero/gallery slots the engine's heroImage and gallery expect. Imagery is the per-vertical differentiator per CLAUDE.md, so a few owner photos plus vertical-appropriate stock clears the design bar without blocking on a photoshoot. Lower priority for law/dental where imagery needs are lighter.

claim: SEO/accessibility micro-content — image alt text, meta descriptions, and per-page title tags — must always be machine/AI-generated; owners do not produce these and small-business sites are routinely shipped with them missing, weakening rankings and creating accessibility/litigation exposure.
notes: Generate these in the build from the structured content (service names, business name, vertical) — never an intake field. Title tags are still a ranking signal; meta descriptions Google may rewrite but should still be authored; alt text serves both SEO and WCAG AA (already a design-bar requirement). Should be a defaulted output of the engine, invisible to the owner.

claim: Reviews/testimonials are an owner-supplied-but-prompted item: the raw quotes already exist (on the GBP and other review sites) and the owner can point you to them, but owners rarely curate or surface them unprompted, so testimonial selection must be coached. They cannot be fabricated, only collected.
notes: Maps to the Reviews and Testimonials components. Pull 3-5 real review quotes from the owner's GBP (with the owner pointing/approving) rather than asking them to write or remember them. Unlike copy/descriptions, these are the one content type that is gather-only, never generate.

claim: The self-edit/own-everything handoff model REQUIRES the AI-generated content land inside the client's owned CMS (e.g. one Storyblok story per shop) as the live, editable default — not as a one-off draft. The walk-away promise holds for CONTENT editing, not design/layout, so intake and handoff must set that boundary: owners can later change words, hours, photos, prices themselves; structural/nav changes still need a developer.
notes: Ties content strategy to the repo's CMS-driven-content layer: generate copy/descriptions/taglines AND seed them into the Storyblok story so the owner inherits editable text, not blanks. This makes the generate-then-confirm intake double as the handoff: the same AI drafts the owner approves become the content they own and self-edit, with zero maintenance dependency on us.

claim: Vertical determines the size of the service-detail intake but not the have/coach split: restaurants/cafes need a full itemized menu with prices (owner-has the list, we generate descriptions + Menu schema), retail needs a product list with prices/availability, while service verticals (barber/spa/trades/law/dental/auto/fitness) need only a short service+price list. The list is always owner-supplied; the prose is always generated.
notes: Consistent with the repo's vertical-anatomy research (Menu schema for cafe/restaurant, Product+Offer for retail). The intake form's only vertical-specific section is the structured list (menu vs products vs services); the copy-generation pipeline downstream is identical across verticals, preserving the one-engine model.

claim: NAP (Name, Address, Phone) must be byte-for-byte consistent across the website (footer + contact page), the LocalBusiness JSON-LD, and the Google Business Profile, because inconsistent citations signal to Google that the data is untrustworthy and dilute the Prominence ranking factor; this is the single most important citation-consistency rule.
notes: Citations/NAP consistency estimated at ~7-13% of local pack ranking weight; one 2025 study cited a ~17% local-pack improvement within 90 days after fixing major inconsistencies. For our engine this argues for a single source of truth (shop.ts content object) that fans out to footer, ContactNAP, and JSON-LD so the three can never drift.

claim: A shop site should derive NAP, hours, and area-served from ONE canonical content source (the typed ShopContent object) and render it into all surfaces (page body, footer, and LocalBusiness JSON-LD) rather than hand-typing it in multiple places, eliminating intra-site drift as a failure mode.
notes: Google's structured-data policy requires markup to match visible page content; a single source guarantees that automatically. This is directly actionable in this repo: ContactNAP, SiteFooter, and LocalBusinessJsonLd already take props from the same shop object — the guardrail should be that no NAP/hours string is ever typed twice.

claim: The website and JSON-LD are NOT a substitute for the Google Business Profile: the canonical NAP on-site must match GBP exactly, because GBP is the primary driver of Local Pack/Maps placement and any mismatch between site and GBP undermines both.
notes: Since the business model is one-time-fee with client self-ownership, the handoff should explicitly include 'make your GBP match the site' as a checklist item — the site can be perfect and still be undermined by a stale GBP the client controls.

claim: Call-tracking numbers placed in the raw page HTML or in citations break NAP consistency; the correct pattern is Dynamic Number Insertion (the real local number is in the HTML/JSON-LD that Google crawls, and only JS-rendered human visitors see the tracking number), or simply use one consistent local number everywhere.
notes: Relevant because the engine ships click-to-call. For one-time-fee local shops the simplest safe default is one canonical local number in shop.ts (no tracking layer), which the audit's clickToCall check should validate points at the same number as JSON-LD telephone.

claim: Hours must be published as openingHoursSpecification in the LocalBusiness JSON-LD using hh:mm:ss 24-hour format, with each day as its own object, so machines can parse hours independently of the human-readable Hours table — and the two must agree.
notes: The repo's Hours.astro renders a visible table but LocalBusinessJsonLd currently centers on name+address; emitting openingHoursSpecification from the same hours data closes a gap. Format gotcha: seconds are required (e.g. 09:00:00).

claim: Special/holiday hours must be authored as dated exceptions, not by editing the regular weekly hours; in JSON-LD this is openingHoursSpecification with validFrom/validThrough (YYYY-MM-DD), and in GBP it is the 'special hours' feature — list each holiday date individually rather than as a broad range.
notes: For a static one-time-fee site, dated holiday hours go stale and become a liability; pragmatic recommendation is to keep regular hours on-site and document GBP special-hours as the client's ongoing job (GBP is editable, the static site is not). 'Open now' showing wrong = trust damage + 1-star reviews.

claim: Outdated/wrong hours are a top conversion-and-trust failure: a customer who travels to a shop shown as 'open' and finds it closed leaves a 1-star review; Google also made 'openness' a ranking factor in late 2023, boosting businesses that are actually open at search time.
notes: Reinforces why hours are in the audit's 12-item conversion inventory. The damage is asymmetric: wrong hours cost a customer AND a public review AND ranking.

claim: A GBP closure of 7+ consecutive days must be set as 'temporarily closed', not via special hours (which only support up to 6 consecutive days); using the wrong mechanism produces a misleading or non-updating listing.
notes: Edge case for handoff documentation, not the static site itself.

claim: Storefront/hybrid verticals (barber, cafe, spa, dental, retail, fitness, sit-down auto/law offices) should frame content as 'located at Y': display the full physical address, embedded/linked map, and a PostalAddress in JSON-LD, because the visible address sends proximity signals and is required schema.
notes: Maps to the audit's addressOrMap check. Address + map should be authored, not omitted, for any vertical where customers come to the location.

claim: Mobile/service-area verticals (mobile trades, mobile auto detailing, home-visit services) should frame content as 'serves area X': use areaServed (AdministrativeArea/city list) in JSON-LD and list named service cities/neighbourhoods in copy, instead of (or in addition to) a street address.
notes: LocalBusiness schema requires name + (address OR areaServed); SABs satisfy it via areaServed. Copy should name realistic core areas (e.g. specific Calgary quadrants/communities), not an exhaustive list, to stay credible. The engine should add an areaServed slot to ShopContent for these verticals.

claim: On Google Business Profile specifically, hiding the address (per Google's own SAB guideline) frequently TANKS visibility in practice — controlled tests show rankings, call volume, and even Local Pack presence dropping when the address is removed; the on-site framing decision is separate from this GBP setup decision and must be flagged to the client.
notes: Important nuance: showing vs hiding address on GBP is debated — some sources say it has 'no impact', but Sterling Sky's tests show real drops. Hybrid SABs (home base + will meet clients) are advised to keep the address. This is a GBP-config caveat for the handoff doc, not something the static site controls; the site should still carry areaServed copy.

claim: Each physical location needs its own dedicated, uniquely-authored page (e.g. /locations/<city>) with that location's exact NAP and hours — never a single shared page listing all branches and never near-duplicate templated pages with only the city name swapped, which Google discounts as thin/duplicate content.
notes: Most Calgary single-shop clients are single-location so this is lower-prevalence, but when it applies it's a real trap. Uniqueness levers: per-location staff bios, parking notes, location-specific FAQs, neighbourhood intro (150-200 words, ~60-70% unique). Each page's NAP must match that location's GBP.

claim: JSON-LD address/hours/areaServed must reflect the actual visible page content (and the real business), not be padded or aspirational — Google's structured-data policy requires markup to match on-page content, and mismatches risk manual action and lost rich-result eligibility.
notes: Because the engine generates JSON-LD from the same shop object as the visible sections, this is satisfied by construction — but the guardrail must forbid adding schema-only fields (e.g. extra service areas) that aren't shown on the page.

claim: Minor address abbreviation differences (St vs Street, # vs No., Ste vs Suite) are tolerated by Google and are NOT a meaningful failure mode; effort should go to eliminating real discrepancies (wrong/missing digits in phone or postal code, different phone numbers, different business-name strings, old addresses in stale citations).
notes: Prevents over-engineering: don't burn effort normalizing 'St'/'Street'. The high-impact failure modes are a different phone number on the site vs GBP, a missing digit in the postal/zip code, a divergent business-name string, and abandoned duplicate listings with old data.

claim: Mobile local-shop pages should pair an above-the-fold hero CTA with a persistent (sticky) CTA so the primary action is reachable at every scroll depth; the hero CTA captures ready-now visitors while the sticky element re-presents the action after they scroll past the hero.
notes: Our Hero.astro already renders an above-fold CTA and SiteNav.astro keeps one button sticky, but there is no full-width persistent bottom-bar CTA component. The hero+sticky pairing is the consensus pattern: above-fold serves immediate-intent users, sticky serves users who scroll. Multi-point placement (above-fold, mid, end) is repeatedly recommended.

claim: A sticky/persistent bottom-bar CTA measurably outperforms a non-persistent CTA on mobile: a Contentsquare analysis of 58M mobile sessions across 400 e-commerce sites found sticky bottom-bar CTAs generated ~31% more conversions than non-sticky equivalents, and a Conversion Rate Experts A/B test of a sticky mobile CTA lifted sales 25% and revenue-per-visitor 22% (even a plain duplicate sticky button gave +8%).
notes: Figures are from e-commerce product pages, not directly local-shop, so treat magnitudes as directional. The mechanism (CTA never scrolls out of reach) applies equally to local-shop call/book/order CTAs. Recommendation: add a shared sticky bottom-bar CTA component (currently absent) that surfaces below the hero fold on mobile only.

claim: The persistent CTA should be placed in the mobile 'thumb zone' (lower portion of the screen, bottom bar or bottom-right float) with a tap target of at least 44x44px and high contrast against its background.
notes: 44x44px is the long-standing minimum touch-target guidance and aligns with WCAG. A bottom bar reaches the thumb zone better than a top sticky-nav button on tall phones. Our SiteNav button sits at the top, so a bottom bar complements rather than duplicates it.

claim: A single dominant CTA generally outperforms multiple competing CTAs of equal weight; when a secondary action is needed it should be visually subordinate (the primary as a filled button, the secondary as a ghost/outline or text link), not a second equal-weight button.
notes: The often-cited 371% email stat is email-specific, so do not port that magnitude to web pages. The transferable principle: one clear primary action reduces decision friction. Our CTA.astro currently renders Call and Book as two near-equal buttons (filled accent vs outline) — acceptable since one is visually subordinate, but the hero should carry exactly one primary action per vertical.

claim: For trades, auto, and dental, the primary mobile CTA should be click-to-call (tel: link), because these are high-urgency/high-trust phone-led purchases; click-to-call removes dialing friction and ~90% of local/mobile service searches convert via a phone call.
notes: Default for these verticals: Hero primary = 'Call now' (tel:), SiteNav button = phone, sticky bar = phone. The repo's audit rubric already scores clickToCall, reinforcing this as table-stakes. The '200% increase in call-to-conversion' and '90% of mobile searches lead to calls' figures are vendor-reported (acefone/rook) — directional, not peer-reviewed.

claim: For barber, spa (medspa), and fitness/yoga, the primary mobile CTA should be 'Book' / 'Book now' to an online scheduler, because forcing a phone call adds wait-and-callback friction that loses bookings; book-now should be the dominant action with call as the subordinate fallback.
notes: Matches the repo's existing Hero default ('Book now') and SiteNav bookLabel='Book'. Default: Hero/SiteNav/sticky primary = bookingUrl; CTA.astro keeps Call as the subordinate outline button. Keep the booking path short (few fields) per mobile friction guidance.

claim: For cafe/restaurant, the page should have ONE dominant primary CTA that is either 'Order online' or 'Reserve a table' (matched to the venue's model), made impossible-to-miss above the fold and persistent; mobile-optimized order CTAs are associated with materially more online orders.
notes: Reservation-led (table service) -> primary = 'Reserve'; counter/takeout (most cafes) -> primary = 'Order online'; if neither applies, 'View menu' as primary with call subordinate. Repo already sets Services heading to 'Menu' for cafe/restaurant. The +30-42% order/conversion figures are vendor case studies (chowly/restroworks) — directional.

claim: For retail, the primary mobile CTA should be 'Get directions' (maps deep-link) and/or 'Shop'/'Buy', because most retail visits are footfall-driven: ~60% of store-locator traffic is mobile and ~68% of local searches lead to a 'Get directions' click.
notes: Our ContactNAP.astro already renders a mapUrl link; for retail this 'Get directions' action should be promoted into the Hero/sticky CTA, with 'Call store' as a secondary. The 60%/68% figures are vendor-reported (singleinterface/sekel) — directional.

claim: For law, the primary mobile CTA should be a high-trust contact action — click-to-call for urgent matters, or a short 'Free consultation' contact form — because legal intake is consultation-led; a single clear consult/call CTA beats mixing call + form + email of equal weight.
notes: Vertical not directly studied in the sources; inferred from the click-to-call (service/intent) and single-CTA evidence. Default: Hero/sticky primary = 'Call' or 'Free consultation'; keep contact form short. Lower confidence than the directly-evidenced verticals.

claim: Trust cues (star rating, review count, key testimonial) placed immediately adjacent to the primary CTA lift conversion; reported lifts range ~15-34% for badges/testimonials near a CTA and a ~59% lift in one case where testimonials sat directly under the CTA.
notes: Actionable for the engine: surface the Reviews.astro rating/blurb near the Hero CTA and/or the closing CTA.astro rather than only mid-page. Lift figures are vendor/agency-reported and span B2B SaaS as well as local — treat as directional, not local-shop-specific.

claim: Recommended per-vertical default mapping for the engine's Hero/SiteNav/CTA components: call-to-action TYPE should be set by vertical (call vs book vs order/reserve vs directions), and the same primary action should be echoed in the hero, the sticky nav button, and a mobile sticky bottom bar to keep one dominant action persistent.
notes: Synthesis claim. Concrete defaults: trades/auto/dental -> Call (tel:); barber/spa/fitness -> Book (bookingUrl); cafe -> Order or Reserve; retail -> Get directions; law -> Call/Free consult. Implementation gap in repo: Hero.astro hardcodes label 'Book now' and only links bookingUrl, SiteNav defaults bookLabel='Book', and there is no sticky bottom-bar component — these should be parameterized by a per-vertical primary-action type (label + href, e.g. tel:/maps/booking) and a shared StickyCTA added.

claim: Phone is the dominant booking channel for auto repair: a CDK Global/Pied Piper 2025 study found ~64% of service customers still call to book versus only ~19% online, so an auto-shop site should treat click-to-call as the primary conversion path and an online booking widget as secondary.
notes: Two-thirds-of-customers-call figure corroborated by Pied Piper 2025 Service Scheduling Effectiveness study. For auto/trades, prioritize tel: prominence over a booking widget; widget is additive, not a replacement.

claim: Phone calls convert dramatically better than web forms for service businesses (commonly cited ~10-12x, and 3x-10x for service verticals), which is why phone-first verticals should make the tel: link the most prominent CTA rather than burying it beneath a contact form.
notes: Multiplier numbers are vendor-reported, not peer-reviewed; directionally consistent across sources. Treat as 'calls convert much higher than forms' rather than an exact figure.

claim: The phone number must be implemented as a real tel: anchor (e.g. href="tel:+1...") using E.164 format (leading + and country code, no spaces/dashes inside the href) so a single tap dials on mobile; a plain-text or image-only number that isn't a tel: link forfeits the one-tap call.
notes: Display value can be human-formatted (e.g. (403) 555-1234) while the href uses E.164. Audit rubric's clickToCall item should verify an actual tel: anchor, not just that a number string appears.

claim: Surface the phone number as visible text accompanied by an icon, not icon-only: a phone icon plus the readable number aids comprehension, and the link must carry a non-color visual cue (it is an anchor screen readers announce as a link), so an icon-only treatment hurts accessibility and recognizability.
notes: Visible number also doubles as a trust cue and lets desktop/landline users dial manually. Icon-only sticky buttons are acceptable as a redundant secondary control if the number also appears as text elsewhere.

claim: Click-to-call tap targets should meet WCAG 2.2 SC 2.5.8 Target Size (Minimum), Level AA: at least 24x24 CSS pixels (or 24px spacing from adjacent targets); mobile platform guidance is stricter (iOS 44x44pt, Android 48x48dp), so size call buttons to ~44-48px for thumb reliability.
notes: Repo's design bar already cites WCAG AA and tap targets; 24px is the hard AA floor, 44-48px the practical target. An inline tel: link inside body text is exempt under the 'inline' exception but a standalone CTA button is not.

claim: Redundant placement materially increases calls: showing the phone number in the header/sticky nav AND repeating it (header + footer generated ~19% more calls than footer-only in one cited test). Phone-first sites should place the number in the sticky nav, hero, and footer rather than a single location.
notes: The Ad Firm page 403'd on direct fetch but the header+footer ~19% figure surfaced via search snippet; treat the percentage as vendor-reported. Redundancy principle is the load-bearing claim. Maps to repo's SiteNav bookingUrl/CTA + SiteFooter phone + ContactNAP click-to-call.

claim: A persistent sticky mobile call bar/button (typically bottom of screen, ~50-60px tall, with phone icon + 'Call Now') keeps the conversion action one tap away during scroll; vendor tests report sticky call buttons lifting conversions ~24-31% and bottom-center placement out-tapping top-right.
notes: Bottom sticky sits in the thumb zone but can overlap content/footer; ensure it doesn't cover other CTAs and respects safe-area insets. Lift percentages are vendor-reported. The shared engine currently has no sticky call bar component — candidate addition for phone-first verticals.

claim: For law firms the phone is the highest-intent conversion and speed-to-answer dominates outcomes: ~84% of law-firm calls come from mobile, calls answered within ~8 seconds convert far higher than at 30 seconds, and form leads suffer heavy attrition — so legal sites should foreground click-to-call above the form.
notes: Implication for the build is placement (call CTA prominent, mobile-first), not answering — the client owns intake. A contact form remains a useful secondary capture for non-urgent matters.

claim: Phone-first is preferable to a booking widget when bookings need a conversation, when the audience skews older, or for urgent/emergency-driven jobs (plumbing/HVAC/electrical, emergency auto, legal intake); a self-serve booking widget wins for predictable fixed-slot appointments (dental cleanings, barber cuts) and can lift booking volume ~20-30% by being available 24/7.
notes: Best practice is to offer BOTH and weight prominence by vertical: trades/auto/law lead with call; dental/barber can lead with a booking widget while keeping a prominent tel: link. Maps cleanly to repo's CTA.astro (phone + optional bookingUrl).

claim: Online booking is now the stated preference for a large share of appointment customers (e.g. an Accenture figure of ~77% of patients preferring to book online), so dental and barber sites should expose a prominent bookingLink alongside click-to-call rather than relying on phone alone.
notes: Tempers the phone-first default for scheduled-slot verticals. Repo already tracks bookingLink in the conversion inventory; the nuance is per-vertical prominence weighting, not mere presence.

claim: If the client uses call tracking, implement it via Dynamic Number Insertion (DNI) so a JS swap shows the tracking number to human visitors while the canonical business number stays hard-coded in the HTML source and in LocalBusiness JSON-LD — preserving NAP consistency for local SEO; do not hardcode a raw tracking number into the markup/schema.
notes: Tension with the business model: no maintenance contract means a client-owned, self-serve call-tracking account (e.g. CallRail) is preferable to anything we operate. The build constraint is: keep the real number in static HTML + JSON-LD so DNI can be layered without breaking NAP. Mostly out-of-scope for the base one-time build but worth a documented seam.

claim: Place the primary click-to-call CTA above the fold (in the sticky nav and/or hero) rather than only in the footer, because phone-intent visitors on mobile decide fast; pairing it with a trust cue (star rating/review count or years-in-business) near the CTA reinforces the tap for trust-sensitive, into-the-home verticals.
notes: Maps to repo Hero (bookingUrl/CTA) + Reviews/Stats placement. For trades especially, BrightLocal notes reputation/trust matters because the pro enters the home — co-locating reviews with the call CTA is a deliberate trust-cue placement choice, not decoration.

claim: Embedding the third-party booking/order flow inline on the shop's own page (rather than redirecting off-site) reduces drop-off and lifts booking conversion, because it removes the context-switch step and keeps the high-intent user on-domain. Templates should default to an inline embed for the primary booking entry point where the vendor supports it.
notes: Carly case study: moving to an inline Calendly embed on a services page tripled the booking rate in six weeks with the same traffic. ChowNow keeps the order on the restaurant's domain to reduce drop-off and enable upsells.

claim: Requiring account creation before completing a booking/order is a measurable abandonment driver: 19% of US online shoppers have abandoned specifically because the site forced account creation. Templates' default booking/order path should be a guest flow (name + contact only), with account creation offered AFTER commitment (post-booking confirmation), never as a gate.
notes: Baymard 2026: 19% abandon due to required account creation (tied 3rd among non-browsing reasons). This is a vendor-selection criterion: prefer/configure booking tools that allow guest booking (Square, Calendly, OpenTable, ChowNow guest) over those that force signup.

claim: Every additional form field/step before commitment depresses completion: Baymard finds completion drops ~4-6% per field beyond the eighth, and the optimal checkout is 7-8 fields (12-14 total elements) vs an average of ~15 fields. Booking entry points should minimize fields shown before the user commits and surface only service + time + minimal contact first.
notes: Rework: each additional step ~+7% abandonment, each extra field ~-4% conversion. Baymard: 18% abandon due to overly long/complicated checkout.

claim: The lowest-friction appointment flow is a three-step pattern: service selection -> time selection -> confirm (with guest contact). Successful salon booking systems converge on this exact structure; templates for appointment verticals should expose at most these three steps before commitment.
notes: Both salon-industry sources independently describe the 3-step service->time->confirm flow as the conversion-optimized default.

claim: iframe embeds of third-party booking pages render poorly on mobile by default (no auto-resize, fixed heights cut content, small-screen layout breaks), so a raw iframe is the worst-on-mobile option. If a vendor only offers iframe, the template must set width:100%, a generous height, and test on a real phone; otherwise prefer the vendor's responsive inline/JS widget or a pop-up.
notes: Since these are Calgary local-shop sites where most traffic is mobile, iframe-only vendors are a friction risk. Ranking: inline JS widget > pop-up widget > responsive iframe > off-site redirect.

claim: The booking/order CTA should appear above the fold on the homepage AND as a persistent/sticky 'Book' (or 'Order') button, plus repeated on service/pricing pages, because high-intent users decide quickly and a single buried CTA loses them. Floating/sticky booking widgets are considered better UX for booking conversion than a one-time button.
notes: Our SiteNav already carries a booking CTA (sticky blurred nav); this confirms that pattern plus an above-the-fold hero CTA is correct. Place the inline widget on homepage above the fold and on service/pricing pages.

claim: For restaurants/cafes, the order/reservation handoff should be embedded on the site rather than sending diners to a separate page; off-site order pages increase drop-off, while keeping the order on-domain retains upsell opportunity. ChowNow embeds desktop ordering but still opens a new window on mobile, which is a known weak point to design around.
notes: Cafe maps to the restaurant pattern. For reservations, OpenTable's inline widget collects party/date/time on-page then opens the availability/commit step in a new tab — handoff occurs AFTER the user has set party size + time, which is the right late-handoff point.

claim: OpenTable's embeddable reservation widget collects date/time/party-size inline on the restaurant's own page, then hands off to OpenTable only for the final availability+commit step (new tab). This 'collect intent on-site, hand off at commit' pattern is the correct handoff location for reservation verticals and requires no diner login to start.
notes: Generalizable rule for redirect-only vendors: capture the first decision step(s) on your page, defer the redirect/handoff until the user is committed (has picked a slot).

claim: For dental, real-time instant online booking converts substantially better than a 'Request an Appointment' contact form: practices report 30-40% higher new-patient conversion when switching from phone/request-form to true online scheduling, and request forms are the dominant (friction-creating) default on dental sites. Dental templates should default to an embedded real-time scheduler, not a request form.
notes: DentistryIQ: most dental sites only offer a 'glorified contact form'. Patients compare practices at night and pick the one with instant booking. This is a differentiator opportunity for our dental builds.

claim: The booking entry point should always be paired with click-to-call as a parallel path, not replaced by it: many local-service customers still prefer phone, and offering both online booking and a tel: link maximizes the share of visitors who commit. Templates must keep clickToCall alongside the booking widget on every appointment vertical.
notes: Confirms our existing audit inventory items (clickToCall + bookingLink) should co-exist; do not let a booking widget remove the phone path. Already covered by the 12-item rubric.

claim: Pop-up/modal booking widgets (Calendly pop-up, Square button modal, floating widget) are a good middle-ground when full inline embed would bloat the page or break layout: they keep the user on-domain, trigger from a contextual CTA, and avoid the off-site redirect. This is the recommended fallback when a vendor's inline embed is too tall or not mobile-friendly.
notes: Calendly offers inline, pop-up text, and pop-up widget; pop-up widget gives a persistent Book button opening the scheduler on demand. Good default for nav/sticky CTA where inline lives on the service page.

claim: Showing full service pricing and durations next to the booking entry point (rather than hiding them behind the widget) correlates with higher conversion for appointment verticals, because it removes uncertainty before the user starts the flow. Templates should render the Services/menu list with prices+durations adjacent to the Book CTA.
notes: Maps to our existing Services.astro component carrying prices. Nanoglobals notes barbershops showing full pricing+durations alongside Acuity/Square integrations scored higher on conversion.

claim: Contact forms convert best with the minimum viable field count: 3 fields is the practical optimum, and forms under 5 fields consistently outperform longer ones. One documented case cut fields from 11 to 4 and lifted conversions 120%; HubSpot data shows reducing from 4 to 3 fields improved completion ~50%. The engine's minimal contact form should ship exactly Name + Email + Message (3 fields).
notes: WPForms cites Quicksprout (3 fields guarantees ~25% min conversion) and HubSpot (4->3 = 50% lift). Pagewiz/Unbounce cite the 11->4 = 120% case.

claim: A required phone-number field measurably depresses form completion. Including a phone field causes ~5% average conversion dip; making phone mandatory can cut conversions by >50%; one study found switching phone from mandatory to optional dropped abandonment from 39% to 4%. If the engine collects phone, it must be optional, not required.
notes: 39%->4% abandonment figure attributed to a Small Farm Design study via WPForms. Tension with quote-driven verticals (see phone-CTA claim) — resolve by keeping phone optional, never blocking submit on it.

claim: Single-column (linear) form layout should be the engine default. CXL's original 702-participant study found users completed a single-column form 15.4 seconds faster than a multi-column version (significant at 95% confidence). Multi-column only helps for long forms; local-shop forms are short, so single-column wins on speed and clarity.
notes: WPForms cites a HubSpot test where 2-column won for a LONG form; this does not apply to a 3-field local-shop form. Default single-column.

claim: Field labels must sit above inputs (top-aligned); placeholder text must not be used as the only label. NN/G testing found placeholder-only forms increased errors and completion time across every demographic (worst for older users), and gray placeholders typically fail WCAG contrast. Top-aligned labels are the joint NN/G + Baymard recommendation.
notes: Also an accessibility (WCAG / Alberta context) concern, not just conversion.

claim: Inline (real-time) validation reduces form errors and abandonment versus on-submit-only validation; Baymard found 31% of sites still lack it. Errors surfaced at submit force users to a full stop and cause abandonment when the error is hard to locate. Validation should be debounced/on-blur, not fired on every keystroke (overly aggressive validation is itself disruptive).
notes: Trigger on blur with calm tone; avoid pre-completion flagging.

claim: Mobile inputs must declare correct type/inputmode/autocomplete so phones show the right keyboard and offer autofill: email field = type=email autocomplete=email; phone = type=tel autocomplete=tel (triggers the numeric dial pad); name = autocomplete=name. Wrong/absent attributes force users onto a full QWERTY keyboard and lose autofill, adding friction on the majority-mobile local-shop audience.
notes: Cheap to implement, high mobile-UX payoff. Should be a default in any shared form component.

claim: Spam protection should use an invisible honeypot field (CSS-hidden, aria-hidden + tabindex=-1) rather than a visible CAPTCHA. CAPTCHAs add friction: ~15% of users abandon when faced with a challenge, and CAPTCHAs cost ~3% of conversions (Moz). A honeypot is frictionless and accessible for the low-volume spam a local-shop form sees; reserve CAPTCHA only if honeypot proves insufficient.
notes: Honeypot only stops simple bots; acceptable tradeoff for a no-maintenance local-shop site. Implement with aria-hidden=true and tabindex=-1 to stay screen-reader safe. Many form-delivery services (Web3Forms used by this repo) ship a built-in honeypot.

claim: Post-submit confirmation should clearly tell the user what happens next, not just collapse the form into a tiny 'success' string. A bare inline 'thank you' is functional for low-stakes forms but leaves users on a dead-end with no next step; a dedicated thank-you state/page that sets expectations (e.g. 'We'll call you within X hours') and offers a next action (call us now / view hours) improves experience and gives clean conversion tracking.
notes: At minimum, confirmation must restate the response-time expectation and re-expose the click-to-call CTA. For a static engine, an inline success state with these elements is acceptable; a /thank-you route is the stronger pattern for analytics.

claim: For quote-driven verticals (trades, auto, law, dental, spa), a phone/click-to-call CTA should outrank the form as the primary conversion path. Phone leads convert roughly 10-15x higher than web-form submissions in home services, ~83% of homeowners prefer to call a contractor, and ~78% hire the first contractor to respond. The form should be the secondary, low-friction fallback for after-hours / non-urgent leads — not the headline CTA.
notes: 10-15x is a vendor (pay-per-call) source — directionally consistent across multiple home-services sources but treat the multiplier as a high-end estimate. The strategic takeaway (phone primary, form secondary for quote verticals) is robust. The audit rubric already scores clickToCall AND contactForm separately, which is correct.

claim: Long qualifying/intake forms (case details, insurance, medical history for law; full diagnostic detail for trades/auto) convert worse as a first-touch capture and should be deferred to after the lead replies, not placed on the public site. Legal-intake guidance itself sequences general info first and pushes detailed qualification to a follow-up/dynamic flow. The engine's public form should capture only enough to make contact; qualification happens in the callback.
notes: Detailed intake fields are right for the client's internal CMS/intake tool, wrong for the marketing site's first-touch form. Keep public form to Name/Email/optional-Phone/Message.

claim: The minimal contact form the engine should ship is: Name (required, autocomplete=name), Email (required, type=email autocomplete=email), optional Phone (type=tel autocomplete=tel), and Message (textarea, optional or short), in a single column with top-aligned labels, on-blur inline validation, correct mobile input attributes, a hidden honeypot, and an inline success confirmation that restates response time and re-exposes click-to-call. For quote verticals, render the phone CTA above the form.
notes: This is the synthesis/spec recommendation aggregating the individual claims above; it is the concrete answer to 'what minimal form our engine should ship.' Aligns with the repo's existing Web3Forms-based contact-form delivery (deploy-shop-site skill) which already supports honeypot + redirect/thank-you.

claim: A compact star rating + review count placed near the primary booking/call CTA (and in the hero, not the footer) is the single highest-leverage, always-reused trust cue for local-shop conversion.
notes: Implication for our engine: the Reviews component should expose a compact inline variant (stars + count) usable inside Hero and adjacent to CTA/SiteNav book button, not only as a standalone section. Use specific numeric review count, not stars alone.

claim: Specific review COUNT (e.g. '247 reviews') outperforms a bare star rating; star rating's standalone influence is declining and consumers increasingly want volume and recency.
notes: BrightLocal 2026: 47% won't use a business with fewer than 20 reviews; 31% require 4.5+ stars; 74% want reviews from the last 3 months. So the Reviews component should support rating + count + 'recent' framing, and the rating token/threshold matters (display only when >=4.2-4.5).

claim: Named testimonials with a real photo (and where relevant a role/credential) convert materially better than anonymous or text-only quotes; testimonials should be scattered near decision points (near CTA / contact form), not isolated on a separate testimonials page.
notes: Extend Testimonials.astro item shape to support an optional avatar/photo slot and role/location line. Keep a way to place one testimonial card adjacent to the CTA component.

claim: Real owner/staff and real work photography outperforms stock imagery for trust and conversion; visitors detect stock photos and trust drops. This is a token-driven imagery requirement, not a component change.
notes: Cited ~35% lift (Marketing Experiments) and 87-98% authenticity-trust figures come from vendor/agency blogs, not peer-reviewed studies — treat magnitude as directional. Actionable rule: our hero/imagery slots should default to a 'real photo required' content guideline; human faces (focus on face) raise first-impression trust.

claim: Security/privacy reassurance placed immediately at the contact/booking form ('Your info is secure and never shared', plus visible HTTPS) reduces form abandonment driven by spam/security anxiety.
notes: HTTPS is already in our audit inventory; add a small privacy-microcopy slot under the contact form. For Alberta, privacy wording should align with PIPA (per existing repo compliance research), not invoke US seals like Norton/McAfee which are e-commerce-centric and unnecessary for a simple contact form.

claim: Trust signals follow a placement hierarchy: compact high-credibility cue above the fold/in hero (rating+count), proof near the CTA (a named testimonial or guarantee), and reassurance at the form — pick 2-3 per page rather than stacking many.
notes: This is the core placement rule to encode as a template guardrail in shop-templates: hero=rating+count, CTA-adjacent=testimonial/guarantee, form=privacy line. Reported lifts (15-34%) are agency-blog ranges; cite as directional, not empirical.

claim: For trades (and home services), licensed/bonded/insured status plus a workmanship warranty and a recognized accreditation badge (e.g. BBB) are vertical-specific trust cues that should appear near the hero CTA and in Features.
notes: Add a 'credentials/badge strip' capability (reusable Features variant or a dedicated badge row) carrying licensed/bonded/insured + warranty + accreditation. In Alberta verify exact license/bond terminology rather than copying US 'state-licensed' framing.

claim: For auto repair, ASE-certified technician badges, a stated parts-and-labor warranty (e.g. 12mo/12k), and third-party certification (RepairPal/AAA Approved) are the decisive vertical trust cues.
notes: Same badge-strip mechanism as trades; content differs (ASE/RepairPal/warranty). Place near booking CTA and in Features.

claim: For dental (and med-spa/healthcare-adjacent), real patient before/after photos (with consent), staff credentials, and video/named patient testimonials are the strongest vertical trust cues and drive new-patient conversion.
notes: Requires a before/after gallery imagery slot (paired images) plus a credentials display. 'Up to 80% conversion lift from testimonials' is an agency claim — directional. Consent/PIPA matters for patient images.

claim: For law firms, third-party professional badges (Super Lawyers, Avvo rating, bar association membership, BBB) and case-results/named client testimonials are expected vertical trust cues; their ABSENCE is itself a red flag to prospective clients.
notes: Reuse the badge-strip + testimonials mechanism; content = legal directories/awards/bar membership. Canadian equivalents (e.g. Law Society of Alberta) should be verified vs US Avvo/Super Lawyers before shipping.

claim: For visual-service verticals (spa, salon, barber, fitness), a real before/after or work gallery is a primary booking driver and stock model photos actively hurt; gallery-first layouts plus an Instagram feed embed and practitioner credentials convert.
notes: '65% more bookings / 72% want before-after' are vendor-blog stats — directional. Needs a gallery imagery slot and optional Instagram embed slot. Practitioner-credential line reuses the credentials display.

claim: Years-in-business / local-proof ('Serving Calgary since 2008', local landmarks, owner story) is a broadly reused secondary trust cue that reinforces legitimacy across every vertical, best placed in Stats or near the owner photo, not as a standalone hero claim.
notes: Our Stats component already fits this ('Years in business', 'Local customers served'). Pair with real owner photo for emotional trust.

claim: Embedded live Google reviews / third-party review widgets add credibility via independent verification, but consumers consult multiple review sources (avg ~6 sites) so an embed is a supplement, not a replacement for on-page rating+count; weigh its Core Web Vitals cost.
notes: Third-party JS widgets can hurt LCP/INP (our targets LCP<2.5s/INP<200ms/CLS<0.1). Prefer statically rendered curated reviews + a 'See all on Google' link; reserve live embeds for cases where freshness is the selling point. Reviewer responding to reviews (BrightLocal: 80% prefer businesses that reply) is an operational cue, not an on-site component.

claim: Phone number is rendered as plain text (not a tel: click-to-call link), forcing mobile users to copy/retype it — a high-frequency, highest-ROI defect on local-shop sites. A tappable phone number in a sticky mobile header is the single highest-converting element for most local service businesses; calls convert ~10-12x (some sources 10-15x) better than web-form leads, and listings/sites with a visible Call button see up to ~30% more direct interactions.
notes: Rubric move: keep clickToCall binary but ADD a placement weight — phone must be tappable AND above-the-fold / in sticky mobile nav, not only in footer. Current rubric only checks tel: presence anywhere in HTML. Phone-call-driven verticals (trades, auto, dental, law, barber/spa without online booking) should weight this highest. Lead outreach #1 angle for call-driven shops.

claim: No clear primary call-to-action above the fold on the homepage. ~70% of small-business homepages lack a clear CTA; above-the-fold CTAs have ~73% visibility vs ~44% below the fold, and above-fold CTA placement is reported to outperform below-fold by a large margin. A single focused CTA outperforms multiple competing CTAs.
notes: Rubric move: add an 'above-the-fold CTA present' check (CTA detected within the first viewport / hero region, not just anywhere on page) and a 'single dominant CTA' check. The current inventory has bookingLink but does not assess placement or above-fold visibility. The 304%/682% precision figures circulate widely but trace to secondary marketing blogs — treat the >2x directional finding as solid, the exact multiplier as indicative not certified.

claim: Site is not mobile-responsive / fails Google's mobile-friendly check, despite mobile being the majority of local-shop traffic (58-60%+ of web traffic; ~69% of restaurant traffic is mobile). A large share of small-business sites remain unoptimized for mobile, and ~73% of users say a site's lack of responsiveness is a reason they leave. This makes every other CTA effectively invisible/unusable.
notes: Rubric already checks mobileViewport (viewport meta) — but a viewport tag is necessary-not-sufficient. Upgrade to a real mobile-usability signal (PSI mobile run / Lighthouse mobile-friendly) rather than just the meta tag, because many failing sites have the meta tag yet still aren't usable on mobile. The '91% not optimized' stat is a secondary aggregator figure; the directional 'majority mobile traffic, many SMB sites fail' claim is well-corroborated.

claim: Slow / janky mobile load makes the CTA invisible or causes abandonment before interaction. Google (11M landing pages) found bounce probability rises 123% as mobile load goes 1s→10s; ~70% of analyzed mobile pages took >5s for above-the-fold content. Per-second mobile delay can drop conversions up to ~20%; 40% abandon sites taking >3s. This compounds every CTA/booking defect.
notes: Already covered by perf/CWV scoring (LCP<2.5s/INP<200ms/CLS<0.1). The conversion link to placement: a CTA that loads after a CLS shift or 5s LCP is functionally absent. Weight perf as a conversion-blocker, not just an SEO metric. Our static-Astro rebuild is the natural fix and a strong outreach proof-point (before/after PSI).

claim: Restaurant/cafe menu (or trades/spa price list) is published as a PDF instead of HTML. PDFs force pinch-zoom on mobile, load slowly, are weak for SEO (poorly crawled, dish-level queries miss the page), and break the mobile menu-browsing flow for the ~69% of restaurant traffic that is mobile.
notes: Rubric move: add a 'menu/price-list is HTML not PDF' check (detect linked .pdf where a menu/services list is expected). Maps directly to our Services.astro (HTML menu) component as the fix. Primarily cafe/restaurant; also applies to any vertical that links a PDF service/price sheet (spa treatment menus, barber price lists, auto service menus).

claim: Contact/booking forms are too long or high-friction on mobile, causing heavy abandonment. Form conversion collapses with field count (median ~31.9% at 1 field → 23.1% at 3 → 17.0% at 5 → 11.4% at 7 → 6.9% at 10+). Most industries see 60-90% form abandonment; mobile lead-gen forms convert ~32-41% below desktop. Self-reported drivers: form length (~37%), unclear/unexpected fields (~22%), data-use trust (~19%), validation errors at submit (~14%).
notes: Rubric move: upgrade contactForm from binary presence to a friction score — count visible input fields (penalize >5), flag required phone/long forms, and prefer a click-to-call alongside any form. The field-count cliff numbers are from a CRO benchmark aggregator (digitalapplied) — directionally consistent across Baymard/Zuko/Venture Harbour, treat exact percentages as indicative. Note: for call-driven verticals, the fix is often 'add click-to-call' rather than 'optimize the form.'

claim: Online booking exists but is buried (not above the fold / multiple clicks deep) or the booking flow itself is high-friction (too many steps: service → date/time → personal info → deposit). Average booking/cart abandonment ~70%; each added step is an exit point, and these are warm, already-acquired leads being lost inside the funnel.
notes: Rubric move: bookingLink should not just detect a booking provider string — it should check the booking CTA is above the fold AND in the sticky nav (matches our SiteNav bookingUrl + Hero bookingUrl pattern). Most relevant to appointment verticals (barber/spa/fitness/dental); the booking-provider itself (Fresha/Booksy/Calendly) usually owns the multi-step flow, so our lever is placement + reducing clicks-to-booking, not rebuilding their funnel.
