claim: Hours and NAP (name/address/phone) are missing or not co-located with the CTA, so a ready-to-act visitor can't confirm 'are they open / where are they / how do I reach them' at the decision point. Trust/answer cues placed near the CTA materially lift conversion (testimonials near a CTA reported ~+34%); for local shops, hours+address+phone are the primary pre-call/pre-visit questions.
notes: Rubric move: add a proximity/co-location check — hours + address/map + phone present AND reachable near the primary CTA (not only scattered in footer). Current rubric checks hours, addressOrMap, clickToCall independently; the conversion value is in their co-location at the decision point. Our ContactNAP + CTA + Hours components already satisfy this when composed together.

claim: Weak/absent trust cues near the conversion point — no visible Google reviews/star rating or named testimonials placed at/above the CTA. ~92% of consumers read reviews before purchasing; for local businesses the highest-converting signals are real Google reviews and named testimonials, and placing the strongest review beside the action lifts conversion (embedded vs retyped reviews add ~5-15% credibility).
notes: Rubric already has 'reviews' as binary presence. Upgrade to placement-aware (review/rating visible near a CTA or above the fold) rather than anywhere on page. Maps to our Reviews.astro + Testimonials.astro. LTL Creative source is Calgary-specific, good for local outreach framing.

claim: Forms that silently fail or are unusable on mobile (broken submit, no success confirmation, validation errors only at submit, tiny tap targets). Validation-error-at-submit is a self-reported abandonment driver (~14%), and mobile forms already convert well below desktop; a form that errors or gives no confirmation loses the lead with no recovery path (the visitor doesn't retry).
notes: Hard to detect with a static HTML collector (requires actually submitting). Flag as a manual/visual-review item in the audit (Playwright can attempt a submit on built sites, but on prospect sites we'd be submitting real data). Lower prevalence than 'form too long' but high severity when present. Keep as a visual-review checklist item, not an automated binary.

claim: No HTTPS / insecure form submission, undermining trust at the exact moment of data entry. Data-use/trust concern is a top form-abandonment driver (~19% of self-reported abandonment); a 'Not Secure' warning on a contact/booking form directly suppresses submission.
notes: Rubric already checks https (binary). Now low prevalence (most hosts default to HTTPS), but where present it's a hard conversion blocker on the form specifically. Keep binary; weight as trust-cue-at-form rather than generic security.

claim: Conversion priority must be set per vertical, not uniformly: the template's PRIMARY CTA should map to the vertical's macro-conversion (booked appointment, submitted quote, order/reservation, or in-store visit), with micro-conversions (call, directions, hours, menu-view) as secondary/tertiary support. A single shared engine should theme the CTA stack per shop rather than treating all verticals identically.
notes: NN/G frames macro (primary business goal) vs micro (frequent, indirectly-related, often path-milestones). Google Ads 'local actions conversions' explicitly treats call + directions requests as distinct measurable conversions for physical stores. The loclweb tiered model (primary high-commitment / secondary mid / tertiary low) is the structural backbone for a per-vertical stack.

claim: A page should have exactly ONE visually dominant primary CTA above the fold; one or two muted secondary CTAs may support it, but equal-weight competing CTAs cause decision paralysis and depress conversion. The shared engine should enforce a single high-contrast primary action per vertical and de-emphasize the rest.
notes: Taqtics: highest-converting law firm sites had ONE primary CTA above the fold (a phone number and a single form). loclweb: secondary CTAs must not compete; multiple equal CTAs above the fold confuse mobile users. Implements as: primary = bg-primary high-contrast button; secondary = outline/ghost; tertiary = text link.

claim: For barber/spa/dental (and gym to a degree), the PRIMARY macro-conversion CTA should be 'Book Appointment' (online booking), because online booking is now the majority-preferred channel: surveys report ~78% prefer online over ~22% phone, ~80% of salon customers prefer to book online, and 73% of Gen Z/Millennial clients expect 24/7 online booking.
notes: Online bookings also have ~49% lower no-show rates than phone bookings. Dental: changing button copy from 'Contact Us' to 'Book Your Appointment' can lift clicks ~25%.

claim: For barber/spa/dental, click-to-call should be the SECONDARY CTA (not primary), because a meaningful minority still prefers phone and 77% of salon/spa regulars find calling easiest for CHANGING an existing appointment. Massage/spa skews more phone-heavy (~57% of bookings still via phone/in-person/recurring vs ~43% online), so spa templates should keep call prominent.
notes: Spa is the edge case among the appointment verticals: weight call nearly co-equal with book. Dental: keep both 'Request an Appointment' (primary) and 'Contact Us'/call (secondary) in the nav.

claim: For trades/home-services, the PRIMARY CTA should be click-to-call, NOT a quote form: ~83% of homeowners prefer to call a contractor, phone leads convert ~10-15x higher than web-form submissions, and 91% rate the phone experience as a primary contractor-selection factor. A 'Request a Quote' form should be the SECONDARY CTA for after-hours/considered leads.
notes: Response speed is part of conversion: 78% hire the first contractor who responds; 67% call a competitor after one unanswered call. This is the inverse of the appointment verticals - trades should default phone-primary, form-secondary.

claim: For law, the PRIMARY conversion is a 'Free Consultation / Free Case Review' offer delivered as BOTH a sticky click-to-call phone number and a single short intake form; vague 'Contact Us' underperforms specific high-intent copy (e.g., 'Schedule a Free Case Evaluation'). Highest-converting firms use one primary CTA above the fold (phone + one form), not many.
notes: Law and trades both treat the phone as the high-intent macro path, but law pairs it with a 'free consultation' framing and a sticky header phone number on every page. ~65% of law firm sites reportedly fail to convert, largely from CTA overload / vague CTAs.

claim: For cafe/restaurant, the conversion hierarchy is menu-view → (order online / reserve) → directions/hours, with the menu being the single most-visited page and often the deciding factor. PRIMARY CTA differs by sub-type: a quick-serve cafe should default PRIMARY to 'View Menu' + 'Get Directions/Hours' (walk-in micro-conversions), while a full-service restaurant with ordering/reservations should default PRIMARY to 'Order Online' or 'Reserve'.
notes: BentoBox priority order: menu > location/hours > online ordering > reservations > contact. Cafes are walk-in-led (menu/hours/directions are the realistic macro), whereas full restaurants monetize order/reserve. The engine should template these as two cafe/restaurant variants rather than one.

claim: For retail, the PRIMARY CTA branches on the business model: foot-traffic-led shops (few SKUs) should default PRIMARY to 'Get Directions' + 'Visit Us' / hours with click-to-call secondary (micro-conversions ARE the goal); shops selling online should default PRIMARY to 'Shop / Buy' with directions/pickup messaging secondary. The template should expose a model switch rather than assume e-commerce.
notes: For foot-traffic retail, 'Get Directions' / 'Call Store' are the canonical store-locator CTAs and the directions-click is itself a tracked local conversion (Google local actions). Matches repo's existing retail decision rule (catalog+visit-us vs full Shopify).

claim: For auto repair, the PRIMARY CTA should be 'Book Appointment' (online scheduling) with click-to-call as a strong SECONDARY: ~75% of customers prefer to schedule online vs by phone, online scheduling lifts bookings ~20-30%, and ~40% of appointments are booked after business hours (when a phone line is closed but a booking widget converts).
notes: Auto behaves like the appointment verticals (book-primary, call-secondary). The after-hours stat is the strongest argument for defaulting to a booking widget over phone-only.

claim: For fitness/gym, the PRIMARY CTA should be a low-friction trial offer ('Try a Free Class' / 'Claim Free Trial' / 'Get Started') above the fold and repeated in nav, because the trial is the entry macro-conversion that funnels to membership; urgency framing ('limited-time') and bundling (free PT/consult) lift trial conversion.
notes: Fitness differs from book-an-appointment verticals: the macro is a trial/lead, not a single booked slot. 'Get Started' in nav + 'Try Free Class' above the fold is the canonical stack.

claim: The standard CTA stack pattern for service/lead verticals is: low-friction exploratory CTA at top (e.g., the primary action) → mid-funnel lead CTA ('Request a Quote'/'Book') after the value prop → repeat the highest-intent CTA at the bottom after the full pitch. The engine should place the primary CTA in nav + hero + closing CTA section, consistent with this repeat pattern.
notes: Maps directly to the repo's existing component layout: SiteNav bookingUrl, Hero bookingUrl, and CTA.astro closing section all carry the same primary action. Confirms the existing 3-placement pattern is correct; the per-vertical work is choosing WHICH action fills those slots.

claim: On mobile, a sticky/persistent CTA (sticky header phone number for call-primary verticals; sticky bottom 'Book'/'Call Now' bar) materially raises local conversion; click-to-call converts ~10-15x typical online conversion. tel: links must be present and tappable. The engine should ship a sticky mobile CTA bar themed to each vertical's primary action.
notes: Repo already audits clickToCall as a conversion-inventory item. Recommend extending the engine with a sticky mobile action bar whose label/target is theme-driven (Call vs Book vs Directions) per vertical.

claim: Quote/intake/contact forms must be kept short to convert: conversion falls non-linearly with field count (~32% at 1 field, ~23% at 3, ~17% at 5, ~11% at 7, ~7% at 10+), with a steep 'cliff' between 5 and 7 fields. For forms above ~5 fields, a multi-step layout recovers conversion (+14% at 5 fields, +19% at 7, +32% at 10). Asking for a phone number reduces form conversion ~5%.
notes: Quote-request forms specifically see ~12% multi-step lift. Default the engine's contact/quote form to <=5 visible fields (name, contact, service/reason, optional message); switch to multi-step only when 6+ fields are genuinely required. Beyond 4 steps adds friction without benefit.

claim: Trust cues (reviews/star rating, testimonials, LocalBusiness JSON-LD, real NAP) should be placed adjacent to the primary CTA, not isolated, because they reduce friction at the conversion moment; high-converting templates pair the booking/call CTA with social proof. The engine's Reviews/Testimonials components should sit near hero and closing CTA, not only mid-page.
notes: Dental CRO and law-firm conversion guides both cite testimonials/proof near CTA as a 30%+ conversion lever. Repo already has Reviews.astro + Testimonials.astro + LocalBusinessJsonLd; the change is placement (flank the primary CTA) not new components.

claim: CTA button copy should be specific and action/outcome-oriented per vertical, not generic 'Contact Us' / 'Submit': e.g., 'Book Your Appointment' (dental, +~25% clicks), 'Call Now - Free Quote' (trades), 'Schedule a Free Case Review' (law), 'Try a Free Class' (fitness), 'Get Directions' (cafe/retail walk-in). The engine should drive CTA label from the theme/content, not hardcode one label.
notes: Repo already supports bookLabel/bookingUrl on SiteNav and headings in content - confirms label should be content-driven. Recommend a per-vertical default label set in the template/theme so the right verb ships by default.

claim: Surfacing any social proof at all (a star rating, review count, or testimonials) on the page is a baseline trust requirement: 93-97% of consumers read reviews before choosing a local business and 72% say positive reviews make them trust a local business more, so a page with zero visible proof loses first-time visitors at the consideration stage. This is the floor the current single 'reviews' boolean already detects.
notes: This is the existing reviews boolean. Detectable today via regex on review|testimonial|star|rating. Keep as the base sub-signal that the graded ones build on.

claim: Displaying a numeric/visible star rating (e.g. '4.8 stars') is a high-impact sub-signal distinct from generic testimonials: 52-55% of consumers will only consider a local business at/above a 4-star minimum, and a visible rating lets the visitor pass that threshold check on-page instead of leaving to verify. Detectable as a star-glyph or 'N.N stars / N.N out of 5' numeric pattern, separate from the word 'testimonial'.
notes: Detectable sub-signal: presence of '★'/star icons OR /\b[0-5]\.[0-9]\s*(out of 5|\/\s*5|stars?)\b/. Grade higher than a bare 'testimonials' string because it answers the 4-star threshold question.

claim: Showing review COUNT/volume (e.g. 'based on 240 reviews') is independently influential and arguably more so than the star value alone: consumers only begin to trust an average rating once a business has a meaningful volume of reviews (commonly cited ~10 reviews read before trusting a business; trust in the average rating builds with volume), and 43% prefer businesses with 100+ reviews. A rating with no count reads as cherry-picked. Detectable as a number adjacent to 'reviews/ratings'.
notes: Detectable sub-signal: /\(?\s*\d{1,4}\s*(google\s*)?(reviews|ratings)\b/. Grade as a separate point from the star value; ideally both present = full 'rating + count' credit.

claim: Embedded/live third-party reviews (a Google Reviews widget or a link through to the Google/Yelp profile) outperform static hand-written testimonials because they are publicly verifiable: a skeptical visitor can click through to the source, which a copy-pasted quote or screenshot cannot offer. This verifiability matters more in 2026 because AI-generated fake reviews have raised baseline skepticism (the FTC's 2024 rule bans fake reviews with penalties up to ~$51,744/violation). Detectable by the presence of a Google/Yelp reviews embed script, iframe, or an outbound link to a Google Maps/business profile.
notes: Detectable sub-signal: script/iframe src matching embedsocial|elfsight|sociablekit|trustpilot|shapo|tagembed OR href to google.com/maps|search.google.com/local|g.page|yelp.com/biz with review context. This is the highest-trust tier of the graded reviews signal and is usually absent on local-shop sites — a strong upsell flag.

claim: Named, photo-attributed testimonials convert materially better than anonymous quotes: anonymous quotes read as fabricated, while a real name (and role/company where relevant) adds credibility, and adding a customer photo next to a testimonial has been measured to lift conversions dramatically (one widely-cited test +102.5%). Casual, real-looking photos beat polished stock headshots. Detectable as an within/adjacent to a testimonial/quote block, or a quote attributed to a proper-name byline.
notes: Detectable sub-signal: testimonial/quote container that also contains an (photo) AND/OR a proper-name attribution pattern (— Firstname L.). Distinguishes a credible testimonial block from an anonymous '★★★★★ great service' string that the current boolean already accepts.

claim: AggregateRating / Review structured-data markup is a distinct, separately-detectable signal whose primary payoff is SERP click-through, not on-page trust: review-star rich snippets are widely reported to lift organic CTR ~15-35%, driving more qualified visitors before they even land. It is invisible on the rendered page, so it must be detected in JSON-LD/microdata, separate from the existing localBusinessJsonLd and menuSchema checks.
notes: Detectable sub-signal: /"@type"\s*:\s*"AggregateRating"/ or "Review" in JSON-LD, or itemprop="aggregateRating". Belongs to BOTH the SEO and trust legs. Google requires it reflect genuine on-page reviews — flag mismatch risk in remediation copy.

claim: Google Guarantee / Google Screened (merging into the unified 'Google Verified' badge from late 2025) is a high-impact third-party trust badge for eligible local-SERVICE verticals: Google reports Guaranteed-badged businesses get ~12% more clicks, and the badge carries a financial backstop (reimbursement up to ~$2,000). It is earned via Local Services Ads, so on a shop's own site it appears as a badge image/link rather than something we generate. Detectable as a Google Guarantee/Screened/Verified badge asset or LSA link.
notes: Detectable sub-signal: img/alt or text matching 'Google Guaranteed|Google Screened|Google Verified' or an l.google.com/local-services link. Vertical-gated: relevant to service businesses (home services/trades, healthcare, legal, auto), not retail/cafe. Earned off-site, so audit reports it as a present/absent badge, not a build deliverable.

claim: Generic static trust badges (notably the BBB seal) are decorative-to-risky rather than reliably high-impact, so the audit should NOT treat them as a positive trust signal by default: an A/B test found removing a BBB seal produced a 5.3% conversion LIFT (99% confidence), and real customer success stories outperform badge/certification reliance (cited ~67% higher conversion). Security/checkout seals (Norton/McAfee) help in cart contexts that local-shop sites rarely have. Fake or unverifiable badges actively erode trust.
notes: Detectable sub-signal (optional/neutral): img/alt matching 'BBB|Better Business Bureau|Norton|McAfee|Trustpilot'. Recommend recording it but NOT awarding trust points automatically — evidence is mixed/negative for BBB on non-cart pages. Prioritize verifiable Google reviews over decorative seals in remediation advice.

claim: Review RECENCY is a trust factor the audit cannot reliably grade from static HTML but should account for: 83% of consumers say reviews only hold value if recent, and trust skews to reviews from the last ~2-4 weeks. A static hand-written testimonial wall gives no recency signal, whereas an auto-syncing embedded Google widget inherently shows current reviews — reinforcing that the embedded/live sub-signal should grade above static testimonials.
notes: Not directly detectable on-page (no timestamp in most static markup). Use as justification for ranking the live-embed sub-signal highest. If a date pattern appears next to reviews it can be a weak positive, but treat recency primarily as a reason embedded > static rather than its own boolean.

claim: Recommended grading of the single 'reviews' boolean into ordered sub-signals (lowest to highest trust value): (1) any social-proof text present [current boolean]; (2) named/photo testimonial block; (3) visible numeric star rating; (4) visible review count alongside rating; (5) AggregateRating/Review JSON-LD (SEO-CTR payoff); (6) live embedded/linked third-party (Google/Yelp) reviews. Google Verified badge is a vertical-gated bonus for service businesses; generic BBB/security seals are recorded-but-neutral. This ordering is supported by the relative impact evidence across the claims above.
notes: Implementation map for packages/audit/src/inventory.ts: split 'reviews' into reviewsAny (existing regex), starRating (glyph/numeric), reviewCount (number+reviews), namedTestimonial (quote block + img or proper-name), aggregateRatingSchema (JSON-LD @type AggregateRating/Review), embeddedReviews (Google/Yelp widget script/iframe or profile link), googleVerifiedBadge (service verticals only). Most local-shop sites have only reviewsAny — so graded sub-signals create clear, defensible upsell findings.

claim: No-HTTPS (site served only over plain http://) is the single most common transport-security failure on older local-shop sites, and is the one the current audit already catches via url.startsWith('https://'). HTTP-only sites concentrate exactly in the small-business segment we target: HTTPS adoption is ~97% of homepages web-wide but only ~75-85% among small-business domains (i.e. 15-25% of small-business sites still HTTP-only), versus near-100% for ecommerce/finance.
notes: Web-wide ~97.3% mobile / 97.5% desktop homepages HTTPS (Web Almanac 2025); small-business segment 75-85% per the comparecheapssl Feb-2026 study combining Chrome/Cloudflare/Netcraft/Censys data. Trades/auto/barber/older-retail skew oldest and are likeliest offenders. This is the existing audit boolean — keep it, it is the highest-yield single check.

claim: Since Chrome 68 (July 2018) every plain-HTTP page shows a 'Not Secure' label in the address bar to all users — not only pages with password/credit-card fields. A no-HTTPS local-shop site therefore wears a visible 'Not Secure' badge on every visit, which is the concrete, screenshot-able trust harm to put in an outreach audit (not an abstract 'you should have SSL').
notes: Distinction matters: pre-2018 only sensitive-input pages were flagged; since Chrome 68 ALL http pages are flagged passively, and entering data on them escalates to a red warning (Chrome 70+). The audit can already assert this surfacing whenever https===false — no extra probe needed. Good outreach copy: 'Visitors see Not Secure in their browser.'

claim: Chrome is moving HTTPS-First (the 'Always Use Secure Connections' setting) to ON-by-default for all users in Chrome 154, ~October 2026. After that, visiting a no-HTTPS site shows a full-page warning interstitial the visitor must click through, not just an address-bar label — materially raising the cost of staying HTTP-only and strengthening the outreach pitch with a dated, near-term deadline.
notes: Time-sensitive selling point for 2026 Calgary outreach: 'By October 2026 Chrome shows a click-through warning before your site loads.' Web Almanac confirms browsers already attempt HTTPS-first then fall back. Verify the exact Chrome milestone number/date close to send, since it is a roadmap commitment.

claim: Detectability split: from a SINGLE page fetch + response, the audit can already deterministically detect (a) no-HTTPS — the final URL is http:// — and (b) http→https redirect gap — fetch http:// and check whether the final/redirected URL upgraded to https://. Both extend the existing boolean with no extra tooling. Everything cert-related (expired/self-signed/hostname-mismatch) and HSTS require either reading the TLS handshake error or parsing response headers — a deeper probe.
notes: Single-fetch-detectable: no-HTTPS, http->https upgrade-on-redirect, HSTS header presence (it is in the response headers the fetch already receives — currently discarded). Needs deeper probe: cert expiry/self-signed/altname (TLS-layer), www/non-www canonicalisation (needs a second hostname fetch), mixed-content (needs parsing sub-resource URLs in the HTML).

claim: CRITICAL audit bug: Node's fetch/undici THROWS on an expired, self-signed, or hostname-mismatched certificate (CERT_HAS_EXPIRED, UNABLE_TO_VERIFY_LEAF_SIGNATURE, ERR_TLS_CERT_ALTNAME_INVALID) before any HTML is returned. The current fetchPage.ts treats that throw as 'https unreachable' and silently falls back to http://, so a BROKEN-CERT site is mis-recorded as merely 'no HTTPS' (https:false) and the real, scarier failure (a 'Your connection is not private' full-page block) is lost.
notes: To distinguish, catch the TLS error code in attemptFetch before the http fallback and record a tlsCertError flag. Cert errors are a DIFFERENT (worse) visitor experience than plain HTTP: Chrome shows a NET::ERR_CERT_* / 'Your connection is not private' interstitial that blocks the page entirely, vs the dismissible 'Not Secure' label for plain HTTP. Worth a distinct rubric item.

claim: Expired / self-signed / hostname-mismatch certificates surface to the visitor as a full-page interstitial ('Your connection is not private', NET::ERR_CERT_DATE_INVALID / NET::ERR_CERT_AUTHORITY_INVALID / NET::ERR_CERT_COMMON_NAME_INVALID) that BLOCKS the page until clicked through — far more damaging than the passive 'Not Secure' label. They are rarer than plain no-HTTPS but recur on local-shop sites where a one-off paid cert lapsed, a self-signed/host-default cert was never replaced, or HTTPS was enabled on the apex but the cert only covers www (or vice-versa).
notes: Requires a deeper probe (TLS-layer): inspect the thrown error code, or run an explicit TLS handshake / openssl s_client / SSL Labs-style check. Hostname-mismatch (cert covers www but visitor hits apex) is the sneaky local-shop variant and ties directly to the www/non-www claim. Highest impact per occurrence because it blocks the page outright.

claim: http→https redirect gaps are common and detectable from one extra fetch: a shop can have a valid cert yet (a) not redirect http:// to https:// at all (both versions load, the http one shows 'Not Secure'), or (b) redirect via a chain that drops to http mid-way. The reliable test is: fetch the http:// URL and assert the final resolved URL is https://. This is cheap to add and catches sites that 'have SSL' but leave the insecure door open.
notes: fetchPage.ts already follows redirects (redirect:'follow') and exposes finalUrl — so an http-origin fetch that ends on https:// proves the redirect exists; ending on http:// proves the gap. Builder-hosted sites (Wix/Squarespace/GoDaddy) auto-redirect; the gap shows up on self-managed WordPress/cPanel and old custom hosting.

claim: Missing HSTS (no Strict-Transport-Security response header) is the norm even among otherwise-HTTPS sites: only ~36% of mobile pages send HSTS in 2025 (so ~64% lack it), and adoption is concentrated in the top-1k sites — local shops almost never have it. It is trivially detectable from the response headers the audit's fetch already receives (currently discarded). But it is a low-severity, NON-visitor-facing item: there is no browser warning for missing HSTS, so it is a 'nice-to-have' rubric line, not an outreach headline.
notes: Web Almanac 2025: 36% mobile HSTS (+6pp YoY), 96% of those with valid max-age, median 365 days. Detect by reading res.headers.get('strict-transport-security') — single fetch, no extra probe. Because there is zero visitor-visible symptom, weight it low: include for completeness/credibility, do not lead outreach with it.

claim: Mixed-content failures (an HTTPS page that pulls sub-resources over http://) split by type: ACTIVE mixed content (scripts/CSS/iframes/fetch) is silently BLOCKED by modern browsers — so the page renders broken/unstyled with no obvious cause; PASSIVE mixed content (images/audio/video) loads but strips the padlock and downgrades the lock icon to a warning. On local-shop sites this appears after a half-finished HTTP→HTTPS migration (hardcoded http:// image/widget URLs in WordPress content). Detecting it requires parsing the HTML for http:// sub-resource references — a deeper probe, not a header check.
notes: Detectable from the already-fetched HTML (no extra request): scan for src/href/srcset/url() values starting with http:// (and protocol-relative // edge cases) when finalUrl is https://. Active vs passive determines whether the visitor sees a broken page or just a downgraded padlock. WordPress migrations are the classic source; common on older retail/restaurant sites with embedded galleries/maps/widgets.

claim: www / non-www canonicalisation gaps are a distinct, common local-shop failure with two visitor-facing surfaces: (a) the cert covers only one hostname so hitting the other yields a NET::ERR_CERT_COMMON_NAME_INVALID block, and (b) both hostnames resolve with no 301 between them, splitting SEO link-equity and duplicating content. Requires a deeper probe: fetch BOTH https://example.com and https://www.example.com and compare cert validity + whether one 301-redirects to the other.
notes: Two failures bundled under one cause: cert-altname-mismatch (trust/blocking) and missing-301-canonical (SEO). Needs a second hostname fetch, so it is a deeper probe. Builder platforms (Wix/Squarespace/GoDaddy) handle this automatically; self-hosted WordPress/cPanel and old registrar-parked DNS are where it breaks. Ties the trust dimension to the existing SEO grade.

claim: Platform is a strong predictor of whether transport-security failures exist, which lets the audit prioritise: GoDaddy Website Builder, Wix, Squarespace, and WordPress.com all include free auto-provisioned/auto-renewing SSL and auto-redirect to HTTPS by default, so they almost never show no-HTTPS, expired-cert, or redirect-gap problems. The failures cluster on self-hosted/legacy stacks — self-managed WordPress.org on cPanel/shared hosting, hand-rolled custom sites, and old registrar/template hosting — exactly the prospect profile this service targets.
notes: Actionable: cross-reference with the existing stack.ts detector. If the site is on a managed builder, deprioritise transport-security checks (they will pass); spend the probe budget on self-hosted WordPress/custom sites where cert lapse, mixed content (post-migration), and www/non-www gaps actually occur. Self-managed WordPress is the highest-yield target for this whole dimension.

claim: A footer-linked privacy policy is the single most reliably auto-detectable PIPA artifact: scan the page DOM for an anchor whose href or visible text matches /privacy[\s-]?(policy|notice|statement)/i (also 'data protection', 'your privacy'). Treat a matching <a href> as PRESENT; this maps cleanly to a new boolean inventory key `privacyPolicy` alongside the existing 12 conversion items.
notes: Commercial scanners (ObservePoint, Nixon Pro, SitePrivacyScore) all implement exactly this href/anchor-text detection, confirming it is a standard, reliable auto-check. Main false-negative: footer link present only on inner pages, not the homepage we fetch — note this as a single-page-scan caveat. Add to packages/audit inventoryFromHtml as a regex over <a> tags, not raw html, to avoid matching body copy.

claim: Privacy policy absence is genuinely common on small-business sites — roughly two-thirds lack one — so this audit line item will fail for a large share of Calgary prospects and is a strong outreach hook, not a rare edge case.
notes: Cited figure: IAPP research that 'over 67% of small businesses don't have privacy policies on their websites'; corroborated by 'fewer than 40% of small business websites maintain adequate privacy policies' (attrib. Daniel Solove). These are secondary aggregations, not Calgary-specific primary studies — treat as directional. The trades/barber/cafe segments (lowest digital maturity) will skew toward missing; law/dental skew toward present.

claim: A cookie/consent banner is auto-detectable by fingerprinting known CMP scripts and banner DOM (e.g. ids/classes like #onetrust-banner-sdk, .cookie-consent, .cky-consent-bar, cookiebot, termly, iubenda, complianz) plus generic text /(accept|allow) (all )?cookies/i. It should be a SEPARATE scored item from privacy policy, because under PIPA most simple local shops need only meaningful cookie notice (not a GDPR-style blocking banner), so 'absent' is lower-severity than a missing privacy policy.
notes: Banner adoption among Canadian small local shops is low; consent-banner pressure is driven by Quebec Law 25 / GDPR, not Alberta PIPA per se. Score it as informational/bonus, not a hard fail. Detection via CMP-script fingerprint is high-precision; generic-text detection has false positives on body copy — prefer script + known-selector matching.

claim: A near-form purpose notice (a short 'we use your details only to respond to your enquiry / manage your booking' line adjacent to a contact/booking form, per PIPA s.13) is NOT reliably auto-detectable and must be flagged for manual review. It requires locating a <form> and semantically classifying nearby text as a collection-purpose statement — exactly the kind of provision NLP privacy-policy classifiers still struggle with.
notes: The repo already detects contactForm via /<form/. Extend to a manual-review flag: 'form present but no detectable purpose notice near it'. A cheap heuristic (form present AND no privacy-related text within N chars/siblings of the form) gives a useful low-confidence signal but should be surfaced as 'review', not a hard pass/fail. This is a prime thing the shippable component fixes by default.

claim: A named privacy officer / contact-for-privacy (PIPA s.5) is NOT reliably auto-detectable from the homepage and should be flagged for manual review. It lives inside the privacy policy body text (a name/role + email), so a homepage-only fetch will almost never see it; even fetching the policy page, extracting 'who to contact about privacy' is an NLP extraction task with known accuracy limits.
notes: Best automatable proxy: IF a privacy policy URL is found, fetch it and regex for an email + the token /privacy (officer|contact)/i; presence => likely-present, absence => manual review. Never assert 'no privacy officer' from a homepage scan. This is a guaranteed field in our shippable component, turning a frequent miss into an automatic pass.

claim: Offshore/cross-border service-provider disclosure (PIPA's requirement to name countries outside Canada where data is processed) is the most-missed obligation AND is detectable in two complementary ways: (a) BEHAVIOURAL — load the page in a headless browser and enumerate outbound third-party requests/scripts to known US processors (Google reCAPTCHA/Fonts/Analytics, Calendly, Mailchimp, Squareup, HubSpot, Cloudflare), which is high-precision; (b) TEXTUAL — check whether the privacy policy text actually names those transfers, which is NLP-hard and should be manual review. The gap between (a) detected US vendors and (b) disclosed = a concrete audit finding.
notes: SitePrivacyScore literally ships a 'Data Transfer Risk' scanner that 'maps visible external vendors and identifies likely cross-border processors' — proves (a) is practical. The repo's collector already uses Playwright; reuse it to capture network requests and match hostnames against a maintained US-processor allow/flag list (this doubles as the 'standard list of US providers' from the compliance report's action item #8). (b) — confirming the policy text names the countries — stays manual/LLM-assisted. reCAPTCHA→US transfer is a near-certain trigger whenever a form has CAPTCHA.

claim: HTTPS is already covered by the existing `https` inventory item and is the one PIPA-adjacent safeguard that is trivially and reliably auto-detected (url.startsWith('https://') plus a redirect check that http upgrades). No new work needed; it should be explicitly counted as the 'safeguards' signal within the PIPA scored line rather than only as a generic conversion item.
notes: HTTPS is now near-universal (browser HTTPS-everywhere, free Let's Encrypt), so it rarely fails — but when it does it's a serious safeguard gap. The repo upgrades http→https on fetch, so detect the safeguard via the final resolved scheme / redirect, not the input URL alone.

claim: The PIPA dimension should be scored as a small weighted sub-rubric of 3 reliable AUTO signals (privacy policy present, HTTPS, third-party/cross-border vendors detected) plus 2 MANUAL-REVIEW flags (near-form purpose notice, named privacy officer + policy names the offshore countries) — mirroring the existing conversion-inventory pattern (present/counted ratio → grade) so it slots into computeGrade without a new scoring model.
notes: Concrete shape for packages/audit: add a `privacy` sub-inventory { privacyPolicyLinked: bool, https: bool(reuse), crossBorderVendorsDetected: bool, purposeNoticeNearForm: 'pass'|'review', privacyOfficerNamed: 'pass'|'review' }. Auto-items feed the grade; review-items render as caveats in report.ts. Keep confidence='partial' when only the homepage was scanned. This is the 'PIPA as a scored audit line item' deliverable.

claim: The reusable shippable privacy-policy component spec should bake in, by construction, every artifact that is otherwise commonly missing: a footer-linked policy route, a named privacy-officer slot (name/role + email), a near-form purpose-notice slot, an explicit named offshore-provider list (driven by the same US-vendor table the auditor flags), and an unchecked opt-in for marketing. This guarantees a Studio0rbit build auto-passes the PIPA audit line that ~two-thirds of prospects fail.
notes: Component spec (one shared Astro component + content type): props { businessName, privacyOfficer{name,role,email}, offshoreProviders[{vendor,country,purpose}], lastUpdated }, a default offshoreProviders preset (Cloudflare/US hosting, Google reCAPTCHA/Fonts/Analytics→US, booking tool, email tool), a <PurposeNotice> sub-component to drop beside ContactNAP/forms, and a footer link. Mirrors the compliance report's action items #7/#8. Ship as new shared component dir => remember the Tailwind @source rule in base.css. Driving the auditor's US-vendor flag list and the component's disclosure list from ONE shared table keeps detection and remediation in sync.

claim: Single-page (homepage-only) scanning materially under-detects privacy artifacts because policy links, purpose notices, and officer details often live only on inner pages — so the auditor must either crawl one level (follow a detected privacy-policy link) or explicitly downgrade confidence and label unfound items 'not found on homepage (review)' rather than 'absent'.
notes: ObservePoint's own 1000-page scan found 62 pages where the privacy link/term was missing or non-standard — global-footer drift is a real false-negative source. Cheap mitigation that pays off: if a privacy-policy href is found, do one extra fetch of that URL and run the officer/offshore-text checks there; this converts two manual flags into semi-automated checks and is the highest-value follow-on for the collector.

claim: HTTPS/SSL is a necessary baseline trust signal: ~98% of US web traffic and ~87.6% of sites already use it, Chrome flags non-HTTPS pages 'Not Secure', and Chrome 154 (Oct 2026) will prompt before first access to any non-HTTPS public site. Its absence actively penalizes (browser warning + lost conversions) so every build must ship HTTPS by default.
notes: Confirms the audit rubric 'https' item. On Cloudflare/Netlify hosting this is automatic; treat as a deploy-stage guarantee, not a per-client option. Already covered by deploy-shop-site.

claim: HTTPS is a confirmed but very lightweight Google ranking signal (affects <1% of queries, weaker than content quality) — so HTTPS is a trust/UX baseline (browser warning, consumer confidence) far more than an SEO lever. Don't oversell it as an SEO fix in audits; sell it as a credibility/penalty-avoidance baseline.
notes: Google's own wording: 'very lightweight signal... carrying less weight than other signals such as high-quality content.' Useful nuance for audit copy so we don't over-claim ranking impact.

claim: A real reviews/social-proof presence is a necessary baseline: 97% of consumers read reviews for local businesses, 92-93% care about star ratings, 85% are more likely to use a business with positive reviews, and 77% are deterred by negative ones. A local-shop site without visible reviews/testimonials is a 'common-but-missing' conversion gap. Ship a reviews/testimonials section in every build.
notes: Maps to audit 'reviews' item. Engine already ships Reviews.astro (star rating + blurb) and Testimonials.astro (quote cards) — these are the always-reused trust components. Star thresholds rose sharply: 68% now require 4+ stars (up from 55%), 31% require 4.5+ (up from 17%).

claim: Google is the dominant review platform consumers check (71% in 2026, down from 83% in 2025 as AI tools like ChatGPT surged to 45%), so linking to / surfacing the shop's Google Business Profile reviews is the highest-leverage social-proof move. A live Google reviews link/widget or GBP rating should be a default trust component, not a static testimonial-only block.
notes: Reviews.astro currently takes a static blurb/rating. Consider a GBP-link variant or rating pulled from content. Note client-owns-everything model: we link to their GBP, we don't run a paid review-sync widget (recurring cost).

claim: Review recency now matters: 74% of consumers only consider reviews from the last 3 months and 89% expect owners to respond to reviews. This means static, baked-in testimonial quotes decay; the durable engine pattern is to link out to the live Google profile (always-fresh) rather than hardcode dated quotes as the only proof.
notes: Architectural implication for packages/shared: prefer a 'see all reviews on Google' CTA alongside curated quotes so the proof never goes stale (fits one-time-fee, client self-maintains model).

claim: A visible privacy policy link is a necessary baseline and a common-but-missing gap: over 67% of small businesses lack a privacy policy despite collecting form/booking data. For Alberta shops, PIPA requires a published policy, named privacy officer, purpose-at-collection notice, and named disclosure of US/offshore processors. Ship a reusable footer-linked PIPA privacy policy component on every site with a contact/booking form.
notes: Settled in docs/research pricing-tools-and-compliance.md (Alberta PIPA, not PIPEDA, for wholly-Alberta shops). Build a templated privacy policy + standard US-provider list into packages/shared; client fills officer name. The audit inventory does NOT currently check for a privacy link — candidate new rubric item.

claim: NAP + click-to-call + hours + map are necessary baseline trust/legitimacy signals reused on every build (consistent contact info and an About/transparency surface are core E-E-A-T trust signals for local businesses). The engine already ships these as always-reused components (ContactNAP, CTA click-to-call, Hours), so they belong in packages/shared by default, not per-client scope.
notes: Maps to audit items clickToCall, hours, addressOrMap. Already shipped: ContactNAP.astro, Hours.astro, CTA.astro, SiteFooter.astro. These are the trust 'plumbing' — always-reused.

claim: LocalBusiness JSON-LD is an always-reused structured trust/identity signal that should ship by default; trust/E-E-A-T for local 'near me' results depends on local schema + consistent listings. It's invisible to users but read by Google, so its absence is a silent gap rather than a visitor-facing penalty.
notes: Maps to audit 'localBusinessJsonLd'. Already shipped: seo/LocalBusinessJsonLd.astro. Always-reused; verify it renders in output HTML per CLAUDE.md discipline.

claim: Professional licensing/credential display is a niche, vertical-conditional trust signal for YMYL verticals (dental, law, and to a lesser degree trades). For YMYL queries E-E-A-T correlation roughly triples (~24% vs ~8% baseline); named-practitioner credentials, bios, and bar/college registration are credibility signals that convert. Scope a credentials/team-bio surface per client for these verticals rather than building it into every shop.
notes: Vertical-conditional. Suggest an optional 'Credentials/Team' section variant in packages/shared (named practitioner + qualifications) enabled for dental/law/medical; high impact where it applies, irrelevant for barber/cafe/retail.

claim: Licensed / bonded / insured / WCB display is a niche, vertical-conditional trust signal specific to trades/contractors: carrying and showing all three (licensed, bonded, insured) signals legitimacy and is a primary hiring-confidence cue for service contractors. Scope this per trades client (a 'credentials/guarantees' strip) rather than shipping it on every vertical.
notes: Vertical-conditional, high impact for trades, irrelevant elsewhere. Could share the same optional 'Credentials/Guarantees' component as dental/law but with trades-specific tokens (licensed/bonded/insured/WCB, warranty, service-area).

claim: On-site allergen/ingredient disclosure is niche and NOT legally required in Canada/Alberta for non-prepackaged restaurant food; only ~16% of Canadian restaurants include allergen statements and ~10% use allergen symbols. Treat allergen info as an optional per-client menu enhancement for cafe/restaurant, not a baseline trust component.
notes: Counters any assumption that allergen disclosure is mandatory. Voluntary value-add; keep menuSchema (audit item) as the cafe/restaurant-specific structured layer, allergen text as optional content scope.

claim: PCI/payment-security surfaces are niche and largely avoidable: local shops should offload payment to PCI Level 1 processors' hosted pages (Stripe Checkout, Square) so card data never touches the site, reducing the client from SAQ D to SAQ A. The engine should NOT build on-site card forms; payments/booking deposits link out, so PCI badges/surfaces are scoped only to e-commerce-leaning clients.
notes: Aligns with one-time-fee/client-owns-everything model: link to hosted checkout (Stripe/Square/booking tool), never collect cards on the static site — keeps us out of PCI scope entirely. Relevant only when a client sells products/packages online.

claim: An About/transparency surface (real business identity, named owner/team, genuine photography, clear contact) is a should-have reused trust signal: Google's own guidance treats Trust as the most important E-E-A-T component, and transparency (clear contact info, detailed About) is a recommended 2025 trust strategy. A default About/identity block belongs in packages/shared, with depth (named practitioners) scoped up for YMYL verticals.
notes: Engine has Features/Stats/Hero but no explicit 'About/owner identity' trust block. Candidate addition to packages/shared. Photography requirement already in CLAUDE.md design bar; ties trust to the design quality bar.

claim: Generic third-party 'trust badges' (security seals, payment-logo strips) are nice-to-have and largely unnecessary for local shops; the durable trust currency is real Google reviews/star ratings, not bought badges (review widgets can lift conversion materially, e.g. cited up to ~270% for products with 5+ reviews). Prioritize a reviews component over badge clutter; do not build a badge system into the engine.
notes: Steers build effort: invest in the Reviews/Testimonials + Google-link components (always-reused), skip generic badge libraries (clutter, often look like AI-slop, recurring third-party widget cost conflicts with no-retainer model).

claim: On a static-host shop site, the form POST/submit endpoint (the form's `action` URL or the JS fetch target) must itself use HTTPS, not just the page. An HTTPS page whose `<form action="http://...">` targets a plaintext endpoint is treated by Chrome (since Chrome 86, Oct 2020) as an 'insecure form': autofill is disabled, the user sees an inline 'not secure' warning, and a full-page confirmation blocks submission. The audit's `https` boolean only checks the page URL (`url.startsWith('https://')`) and entirely misses an insecure form action — a real trust/PIPA-safeguard gap.
notes: Extend audit: parse every <form action> (and obvious JS endpoints) and assert each resolves to https://. Modern third-party endpoints (Web3Forms api.web3forms.com, Netlify, Formspree) are HTTPS-only, so a flagged http action almost always means a hand-rolled/legacy mailer — high-value finding.

claim: Spam/bot protection on a contact form (Cloudflare Turnstile, Google reCAPTCHA, or even a CSS/JS honeypot field) is a legitimately checkable trust signal AND a concrete instance of PIPA's 'reasonable security safeguards' obligation (OIPC PIPA implementation Step 5: 'adequate security measures to protect personal information'). A bare `<form>` with no anti-bot mechanism is both a spam-liability and a weaker safeguards posture. The audit's `contactForm` boolean (`/<form/i`) detects form presence but nothing about whether the form is protected.
notes: New audit signal `formSpamProtection`: detect challenges.cloudflare.com / cf-turnstile class, google.com/recaptcha / g-recaptcha, or netlify-honeypot attribute. Honeypot is the lowest-friction option and is invisible to users.

claim: Cloudflare Turnstile is the privacy-preferable bot-protection choice for a Calgary PIPA-governed shop versus Google reCAPTCHA: Turnstile processes session-scoped signals (IP, User-Agent, TLS/browser fingerprint) without using data for advertising and without Google's cross-site tracking, whereas reCAPTCHA v2/v3 set cookies and feed Google's cross-site user profiling. Both still transmit the visitor's IP to a US-headquartered provider (Cloudflare Inc., San Francisco / Google), so either one is an offshore disclosure trigger — but Turnstile minimizes the PIPA data-minimization and consent exposure.
notes: Recommend Turnstile as the engine default. reCAPTCHA's cookie-setting + ad-profiling means EU/privacy lawyers now require prior consent before loading it; Turnstile is the recommended no-cookie replacement, which also reads better against PIPA consent norms.

claim: A static-host contact form necessarily transmits the submitted personal information (name, phone, email, message) to a third-party US-based form endpoint for delivery, since static hosts cannot run server-side mail logic. The common endpoints all store/process in the US on AWS or equivalent: Netlify Forms stores submissions in the US ('at present, that is in the US'), and Web3Forms processes/forwards via AWS with CleanTalk spam-screening that receives the submitter's IP and email. This cross-border transfer is exactly the 'service providers outside Canada' that PIPA requires be disclosed.
notes: Ties directly to the already-documented offshore-disclosure obligation (pricing-tools-and-compliance doc, item #8). The form vendor itself is the offshore service provider that must be named by country in the privacy policy — not just hosting/analytics. Web3Forms also pulls in CleanTalk as a sub-processor receiving IP+email, an extra disclosed party.

claim: Form data sent to a US endpoint should be transmitted over TLS end-to-end and the submission UI should avoid collecting more PII than needed (PIPA data-minimization). The mainstream endpoints satisfy the transport leg by design — Web3Forms states all submissions are transmitted over HTTPS, and Netlify/Formspree endpoints are HTTPS-only — but a custom or legacy `mailto:`/HTTP form does not, and exposes submitted data to eavesdroppers. The audit should treat 'form action is HTTPS to a recognized provider' as the secure-by-default case and flag mailto:/http/unknown actions for manual review.
notes: `mailto:` form actions are a frequent small-shop pattern: no TLS protection of the payload in transit to the user's mail client, no spam protection, and they break on phones without a configured mail app — a triple trust/conversion/safeguard failure worth flagging distinctly from a real POST endpoint.

claim: Form/data-collection trust should be evaluated as a graded cluster, not the audit's single `contactForm` boolean. A trust-complete contact form on a static shop site has: (a) a POST endpoint over HTTPS, (b) bot/spam protection (Turnstile/reCAPTCHA/honeypot), (c) a privacy-policy link visible at or near the form, and (d) a purpose/consent micro-notice. Presence of `<form>` alone satisfies none of (b)-(d). PIPA s.13 requires purpose notice at the point of collection and a designated privacy officer reachable via the policy — so a form with no nearby privacy link is a documented PIPA gap, not merely a polish issue.
notes: Proposed audit decomposition replacing/augmenting bare contactForm: contactForm (exists) + formEndpointHttps + formSpamProtection + privacyPolicyLink + formPurposeNotice. Grade the cluster; near-form privacy link is the single highest-leverage missing item on most Calgary shop sites.

claim: A linked third-party booking widget (Calendly, Square, Fresha, Booksy, Setmore, OpenTable, Jane) is itself an offshore data-collection surface, not just a CTA: the customer enters name/phone/email/appointment details directly into a US-or-foreign-hosted booking provider. The audit's `bookingLink` boolean detects the link's presence but ignores that this is a separate PII collection point requiring its own offshore-provider disclosure in the shop's privacy policy.
notes: For booking-heavy verticals (barber/spa/dental/fitness/auto), the booking widget is usually the PRIMARY data-collection channel — often more PII than the contact form. The privacy policy's named-US-provider list must include the booking vendor. Audit can map a detected bookingLink host to a known-provider/country table to confirm disclosure coverage.

claim: Booking links opened from a static shop page should target the provider over HTTPS and ideally open in a new context; the dominant booking providers (Calendly, Square, Fresha, Booksy, OpenTable, Setmore) serve HTTPS-only embed/scheduling URLs, so a detected http:// booking link signals an outdated/typo link rather than provider behavior and is worth flagging. This is the booking-side analogue of the insecure-form-action check.
notes: Low base-rate because providers force HTTPS, but cheap to check once bookingLink is detected: assert the href scheme is https. Mostly catches stale/broken legacy embeds.

claim: Because every static-host shop form/booking flow ships PII to a US provider, the offshore-disclosure obligation already documented for the engine is form-triggered: the moment the audit detects `contactForm` or `bookingLink`, it should also expect a privacy policy that names the specific US/foreign provider(s) and country. A site that has a form but no privacy policy is non-compliant under PIPA's combination of safeguards (Step 5), offshore-provider disclosure (Step 5/8), and purpose notice (s.13) — making 'form present + privacy policy absent' the single most defensible compliance finding for Calgary outreach.
notes: Connects this research back to the settled PIPA work (PIPA not PIPEDA; offshore disclosure required). The audit should gate the privacyPolicyLink expectation on detecting any data-collection surface (form OR booking), so the finding is precise rather than blanket. This is the through-line: form presence is the legal trigger, not an isolated UI feature.

claim: NAP consistency (identical business name, address, phone across the website, Google Business Profile, and directories) is a confirmed local-pack ranking factor: when sources agree, Google gains confidence the business is real and located where it claims, improving Local Pack visibility; mismatches suppress map rankings and cause duplicate-listing issues.
notes: Moz Local Search Ranking Factors consistently lists NAP/citation consistency among top local signals. This is a local-SEO trust signal more than an on-page conversion one.

claim: NAP that appears on the page (name, address, phone) can be extracted automatically from JSON-LD/microdata, tel: links, footer text, and Open Graph tags — but verifying it MATCHES the business's actual Google Business Profile requires a second data source (Google Places/Business Profile API), so the page-only collector can detect NAP PRESENCE but not NAP CONSISTENCY against GBP without a Places lookup.
notes: Actionable split for the audit rubric: PRESENCE of address+phone is auto-checkable from HTML (extends existing addressOrMap/clickToCall probes); CONSISTENCY vs GBP needs the Places API. This is the open item docs/research/open-questions.md #4 (GBP-completeness API).

claim: 62% of consumers say they would avoid using a business if they found incorrect information about it online (e.g. wrong address/phone/hours) — making NAP and hours ACCURACY (not just presence) a direct conversion/trust factor, not only an SEO one.
notes: BrightLocal Local Business Discovery & Trust Report 2023 (62%, was 63% in 2021). Accuracy is human/cross-source-verifiable, not auto-checkable from the page alone.

claim: A genuine business-owner photo materially raises trust versus generic/stock imagery or no images: BrightLocal found 46% of consumers trust a site more with a real owner photo vs 33% for generic images and only 21% with no images; 64% trust real owner photos over obvious stock/clip-art. For plumbers the owner-photo trust figure rose to 52%.
notes: Primary BrightLocal research. Strongest single data point that an About/owner section with a REAL photo beats stock. Whether a photo is real-vs-stock and owner-vs-clipart requires human/vision review — the collector can detect image presence and an About/Team page link but cannot judge authenticity.

claim: An 'About'/owner/team section with real team photos and bios is a recognized credibility signal that humanizes the business; leadership/owner visibility raises trust (Edelman: 64% trust brands more when leadership is visible) and adding team photos+bios measurably lifts About-page engagement and conversions.
notes: Presence of an About/Team page and images is auto-detectable (link text + count on that page); quality (real photos, real bios) needs human review. The shared engine has no About/Team component today — a gap worth a token-driven section.

claim: 'Years in business' / 'established YYYY' is a credibility shortcut consumers actively scan for, especially in considered/high-trust trades and professional services; seeing a long track record makes prospects feel safe contacting the business, and ~70% of consumers look for trust signals before purchasing.
notes: Auto-detectable as a regex pattern ('since 19xx/20xx', 'established', 'X years', 'family-owned') but the CLAIM's truthfulness is not verifiable from the page. Strongest in higher-consideration verticals; weak/irrelevant for impulse retail or cafe.

claim: Licensing, insurance, and industry-association/certification badges are conversion-relevant trust signals for licensed/risk-bearing verticals (trades, auto, dental, law); contractor-specific screened badges (e.g. 'Licensed & Insured', HomeAdvisor Screened & Approved) confirm background checks/licensing/insurance and lift conversion when placed on home/service pages.
notes: Largely vertical-specific: critical for trades/auto/dental/law, near-irrelevant for cafe/barber/retail. Badge/keyword PRESENCE ('licensed', 'insured', 'certified', association names) is regex-detectable; badge VALIDITY (real license number, current insurance, genuine membership) requires human review.

claim: Accurate, present opening hours are both a conversion and a local-SEO/GBP signal: profiles with updated hours get ~2x more visits than those with missing/outdated hours, and complete+accurate profiles earn ~7x more clicks and 70% more in-store visits; on-page hours that contradict GBP undermine the 62%-avoid-bad-info effect.
notes: Most impactful for walk-in/visit-now verticals (cafe, barber, retail, spa). The existing collector already detects an hours block (presence). ACCURACY vs current GBP hours is not page-checkable and needs Places API or human review. Stats are GBP-attributed (Google) via aggregator; treat 7x/70% as Google's own profile-completeness figures.

claim: A complete Google Business Profile makes consumers 2.7x more likely to consider a business reputable and 50% more likely to purchase — establishing that the trust payoff of NAP/hours/About signals is realized largely THROUGH a complete, consistent GBP that the website corroborates, not the website in isolation.
notes: Strategic framing for the service: the website's job is to be NAP/hours-consistent WITH the GBP. Suggests the audit should optionally pull the prospect's GBP (Places API) to score consistency — the single highest-leverage trust check beyond the current page-only inventory.

claim: Reviews remain the dominant trust signal but consumers now weight RECENCY and QUANTITY heavily: 74% only care about reviews from the last 3 months, ~47% won't use a business with fewer than 20 reviews, and ~81% read reviews on Google — meaning a site that surfaces a live, recent Google rating beats static/undated testimonials.
notes: Adjacent to the 'beyond reviews' scope but load-bearing for trust design: the engine's Reviews/Testimonials components show static quotes; surfacing a live/recent Google rating (count + recency) is the higher-trust pattern. Static testimonial presence is auto-detectable; recency/authenticity is not.

claim: The 'WebAIM Million' top six WCAG 2 A/AA failure categories — low contrast text, missing image alt text, missing form input labels, empty links, empty buttons, and missing document language — account for roughly 96% of all automatically-detectable accessibility errors on home pages. An engine that guarantees these six by construction eliminates the large majority of failures a real local-shop site would otherwise have.
notes: WebAIM tested 1,000,000 home pages with the WAVE engine (2026 report). 95.9% of pages had detectable WCAG failures, averaging 56.1 errors/page. The same six categories have led the list for 5+ consecutive years, so they are a stable engineering target. These are automatically-detectable only — they are necessary but not sufficient for full AA conformance.

claim: Low-contrast text (foreground/background below WCAG 2 AA thresholds of 4.5:1 for normal text and 3:1 for large text ≥18pt/14pt-bold) is the single most common failure, present on ~84% of home pages (83.9% in WebAIM Million 2026; 79.1% in 2025), averaging ~34 distinct instances per page. The engine can guarantee this by construction by enforcing AA-passing pairings between its OKLCH token pairs (e.g. --foreground/--background, --primary/--primary-foreground, --muted-foreground/--muted) at theme-definition time.
notes: Maps directly to this repo's token system: each shop's theme.css defines the brand color pairs. A build-time contrast check on the token pairs (e.g. via the audit package) would catch the #1 web-wide failure before it ships. WCAG 1.4.3 Contrast (Minimum). 3:1 also applies to UI components/graphics under WCAG 2.1 (1.4.11).

claim: Missing alternative text on images is the second most common failure, affecting ~53% of home pages (53.1% in 2026); about 18.5% of all home-page images lack alt text, and ~45% of images missing alt are LINKED images, which also produces non-descriptive links. The engine must require alt text for content/photographic images (hero, gallery, service photos) and use alt="" only for purely decorative images.
notes: WCAG 1.1.1 Non-text Content. In this repo Hero.astro already correctly uses alt="" because its image is a decorative full-bleed background behind text. The risk is content imagery (gallery/service cards): the ShopContent types should make alt a required field for any non-decorative image slot so the build can't ship a content image without it.

claim: Missing form input labels affect ~51% of home pages (51% in 2026) and are a top-3 failure. Any form the engine ships — primarily the contact form (one of the 12 conversion-inventory items) and any search/newsletter input — must associate every control with a programmatic <label for>/id (or equivalent aria-label), never placeholder-only fields.
notes: WCAG 1.3.1 / 3.3.2 / 4.1.2. Prevalence is 'common' rather than 'always-present' for THIS engine because many shop demos are brochure-style with only a contact form; but where a form exists this is a must-have. The contact form (e.g. sites/landing Web3Forms form) is the highest-risk surface and should be label-audited.

claim: Empty links — anchors with no discernible accessible text (icon-only links, image links with no alt, or <a> wrapping only an inline SVG) — affect ~46% of home pages (46.3% in 2026). The engine's icon-based links (social icons, click-to-call/phone icon, map link, nav booking icon) must carry an accessible name via visible text, aria-label, or sr-only text; inline-SVG Icon.astro usage inside links is the specific at-risk pattern here.
notes: WCAG 2.4.4 / 4.1.2. Icon.astro renders inline SVG with no text; any <a> or <button> that contains only an Icon (e.g. phone/map/social) is an empty link/button unless given aria-label. ContactNAP, CTA, SiteNav and SiteFooter are the components to audit. Easy to guarantee by convention: require aria-label on every icon-only interactive element.

claim: Empty buttons — <button> or input[type=button/submit] with no accessible text (icon-only or aria-less) — affect ~31% of home pages (30.6% in 2026). Any icon-only button the engine ships (mobile menu toggle, form submit rendered as icon, FAQ accordion toggles if button-based) must have a text label or aria-label.
notes: WCAG 4.1.2 Name, Role, Value. Lower prevalence for this static engine since most CTAs are text links ('Book now', 'Call'), but a mobile hamburger toggle or any icon submit is the at-risk case. Faq.astro uses <details>/<summary> (native, accessible) per the catalogue, which is a good pattern that avoids empty-button risk.

claim: Missing document language (no lang attribute on <html>) affects ~13.5% of home pages (2026) and is a top-six failure that is trivially guaranteed by construction: every page the engine emits should set <html lang="en"> (or appropriate locale) in the shared base layout. This is the single cheapest of the top-six to eliminate permanently.
notes: WCAG 3.1.1 Language of Page. A one-line guarantee in the shared base/page template covers it for every shop forever; should be asserted by the audit package on built HTML. Lower web-wide prevalence (~13.5%) than the others but disproportionately easy to fix, so worth hard-coding.

claim: Beyond the auto-detectable top six, two AA criteria materially affect local-shop sites but are NOT reliably caught by automated tools and need design-system guarantees: (a) visible keyboard focus indicators on all interactive elements (WCAG 2.4.7) and (b) target/tap-target sizing and contrast of UI component boundaries. The engine should keep default focus-visible styling (not remove outlines) and ensure CTA/nav links are large enough touch targets.
notes: These are 'should-have' because WebAIM Million can't measure them (manual-test criteria), so they don't appear in the top-six prevalence stats, but they are real AA requirements and align with the repo's existing mobile-first/tap-target design bar. Don't override focus outlines in shared components.

claim: Heading structure and a logical document outline (a single H1, no skipped levels) is an AA-adjacent best practice that automated tools flag and that affects most pages. The shared section components (Hero H1, Features/Services headings) should preserve a sane H1→H2 hierarchy so each shop ships a correct outline without per-shop work.
notes: Hero.astro emits the H1 (shop name). Section components should use H2 for section headings to avoid multiple H1s or skipped levels. WCAG 1.3.1 / 2.4.6. Guaranteed by construction if heading levels are fixed in the shared components rather than passed in freely.

claim: Because ~95% of all home pages (including the overwhelming majority of small-business sites) fail at least one detectable WCAG 2 A/AA criterion, a Calgary local-shop site that ships clean on the top-six categories is a genuine competitive/outreach differentiator — the site-audit and conversion-inventory should explicitly score a11y (contrast, alt, labels, link/button names, lang) as a graded dimension, which the repo's audit rubric already does.
notes: Supports the existing packages/audit a11y grading and the outreach use-case. The differentiator is real precisely because failure is near-universal among prospects' current sites. Note: AODA/Canadian accessibility law applies to larger orgs, not most micro local shops, so the value here is UX/SEO/competitive rather than strictly legal-compliance for Alberta micro-businesses.

claim: A Lighthouse/PageSpeed Insights accessibility score (including a perfect 100) is NOT a valid proxy for WCAG 2.x AA conformance: automated engines only fully evaluate roughly 30-40% of WCAG failures, so a high score confirms only that the automatable subset passes and says nothing about the majority of criteria requiring human judgment. Google's own Lighthouse docs state 'Only a subset of accessibility issues can be automatically detected.'
notes: Directly answers the rubric's core risk: the current audit grades a11y on the single Lighthouse score. It is a floor, not proof of AA. A site can score 100 and still be severely inaccessible (a developer famously built 'the most inaccessible site possible with a perfect Lighthouse score'). Recommendation: relabel the a11y grade as 'automated-detectable issues' rather than 'AA conformance', or never claim 'AA' off the score alone.

claim: Measured by share of WCAG 2.x AA success criteria, only about 13% (7 of 55 in WCAG 2.2 AA) can be reliably/accurately flagged by automated scans, ~45% (25 of 55) are only partially detectable, and ~42% (23 of 55) cannot be detected automatically at all because they require subjective evaluation of meaning, quality, and user experience.
notes: The 7 reliably-flaggable criteria are: 1.3.5 Identify Input Purpose, 1.4.3 Contrast (Minimum), 1.4.11 Non-text Contrast, 2.4.1 Bypass Blocks, 2.4.2 Page Titled, 2.5.8 Target Size, 3.1.1 Language of Page. Even these 'mostly accurate' flags need human confirmation. This is the criteria-count framing of automated coverage.

claim: Measured by volume of real-world issues (not criteria count), automated tooling catches more: Deque's study of ~2,000 audits / 13,000 pages / ~300,000 issues found axe-core detected 57.38% of all issues found in audits. The number is higher than the criteria-count figure because a few automatable issue types (especially color contrast) occur at very high frequency.
notes: Resolves the apparent contradiction between '13-40%' and '57%': the metric differs (criteria vs issue-volume). Both are defensible. For an honest claim, cite issue-volume coverage as ~57% upper bound and criteria coverage as ~13-30% — never round up to imply full conformance.

claim: An independent UK GDS audit of automated tools found that against 142 known accessibility issues, the best single tool detected only ~30-40%, and running 2-3 tools in combination reaches only ~50% coverage — corroborating that no single scan (Lighthouse included) approaches full WCAG coverage.
notes: Government/primary-source corroboration of the ~30% figure widely cited. Practical implication: even a multi-tool automated pipeline tops out near half of issues; manual checks are mandatory for an AA claim.

claim: The W3C's official evaluation methodology (WCAG-EM) states that conformance evaluation requires a combination of semi-automated tools AND manual evaluation by an experienced reviewer; the majority of Success Criteria require human judgment. Per W3C, 'Absence of detected errors does not indicate that a page is accessible or conformant.' Automated tools cannot, by themselves, establish WCAG conformance.
notes: Authoritative basis (the standards body itself) for the rule: you cannot honestly claim 'AA' from a tool score. WCAG-EM is the methodology a defensible AA claim should reference.

claim: Real-world prevalence data shows automated scans systematically under-report: the WebAIM Million 2026 found 95.9% of 1,000,000 home pages had detectable WCAG 2 failures (up from 94.8% in 2025), averaging 56.1 errors per page — and WebAIM stresses these are only the automatically-detectable failures, so true failure rates are higher. Most shop sites in the wild fail AA on automatable criteria alone.
notes: Useful for outreach/positioning: the baseline is that almost every site fails on detectable issues. Our builds clearing the automatable subset is already differentiated, but is not 'AA'.

claim: Six error types account for ~96% of all automatically-detected failures and are exactly the high-frequency, automatable issues a shop-site engine should systematically eliminate at build time: low-contrast text (83.9% of pages), missing image alt text (53.1%), missing form input labels (51%), empty links (46.3%), empty buttons (30.6%), and missing document language attribute (13.5%).
notes: Directly actionable for the engine/token system. Contrast maps to OKLCH token pairs (--primary/--primary-foreground etc.) — the token system can guarantee 1.4.3/1.4.11 contrast by construction. Form labels apply to contactForm; alt text applies to heroImage and any imagery slots; lang attribute and accessible names for click-to-call/booking buttons are template-level fixes. Knocking these out covers the bulk of automatable failures.

claim: Color contrast (WCAG 1.4.3 / 1.4.11) is the single most consequential automatable criterion — it is both the most frequent failure (~30%+ of all errors; on 83.9% of pages) AND fully verifiable in automation — so a shop-site design system should treat AA contrast as a hard, enforced constraint on its OKLCH token palettes (text vs background ≥4.5:1 normal, ≥3:1 large/non-text) rather than leaving it to per-shop judgment.
notes: Because brand palettes are author-chosen per shop (theme.css), contrast is the one place a 'pretty' palette silently breaks AA. Recommend a build-time contrast check on the token pairs in theme.css. This is where automation IS reliable, so lean on it.

claim: The minimum manual/assisted check set required to honestly claim AA on a one-page shop site (beyond the automated scan) is a short fixed list: (1) keyboard-only walkthrough — Tab/Shift-Tab/Enter/Space/Esc reach and operate every interactive element (nav, click-to-call, booking link, contact form, FAQ accordion) with no keyboard traps; (2) visible focus indicator on every focusable element; (3) logical focus/reading order matching visual layout; (4) one screen-reader pass (VoiceOver/NVDA) confirming heading structure (single h1, nested h2/h3), meaningful link/button names, and that decorative images are skipped while informative images have correct alt; (5) alt-text quality review (automation detects presence, not accuracy); (6) 200% zoom / reflow check; (7) form error/label association on the contact form. These cover the high-frequency criteria automation cannot judge.
notes: This is the practical answer to the 'minimum check set' part of the question. For a static one-page Astro site the surface is small and fixed (nav, hero CTA, services, hours, contact form, FAQ accordion, footer), so this 7-item manual pass is tractable (~15-30 min/site) and should be codified as a skill/checklist. The FAQ <details> accordion and the contact form are the two interactive components most likely to fail keyboard/SR checks and warrant explicit verification.

claim: For client-facing language, the engine should claim 'passes automated WCAG 2.x AA checks plus a manual keyboard + screen-reader pass' rather than 'WCAG AA conformant/compliant' unless the full WCAG-EM manual evaluation is performed — because automated-only 'AA compliant' claims are unsupportable and, in a regulated/litigation context, misleading. Honest scoping: automated scan = ~13-57% coverage depending on metric; the rest needs the manual set above.
notes: Risk-management framing tied to the one-time-fee / no-maintenance business model: overclaiming 'AA compliant' creates liability we can't stand behind long-term. Law and dental (higher regulatory/ADA-adjacent exposure) most sensitive, but applies to all verticals. Wording matters in proposals and the audit deliverable.

claim: Alberta has no provincial private-sector web-accessibility statute in force; it is one of only two provinces (with PEI) without any accessibility legislation. The proposed Accessible Alberta Act (Bill 206, 2025) did not pass — it was a private member's bill that stalled/was voted down — so there is no proactive WCAG-compliance legal mandate for a Calgary local-shop website.
notes: Re-verify each session: an Accessible Alberta Act could be re-introduced and passed. As of June 2026 nothing is in force. Analogous to the PIPA-reform staleness caveat in the existing compliance research.

claim: The federal Accessible Canada Act (ACA) does NOT apply to a typical Calgary local shop. The ACA scopes only to federally regulated organizations (banks, telecom, broadcasting, interprovincial/air/rail transport, federal government, Crown corporations). A provincially regulated barber, cafe, spa, dental clinic, auto shop, or retailer is outside its reach, so its WCAG 2.1 AA / EN 301 549 standard is not a legal obligation for our clients.
notes: An edge case: a federally regulated client (e.g., a credit union, an airline-affiliated service) would be in ACA scope — not our target verticals. The ACA reporting deadlines (100+ emp June 2026; 10-99 emp June 2027) are for in-scope entities only.

claim: Ontario's AODA does not apply outside Ontario. It binds public, private and non-profit organizations operating IN Ontario, with the web-accessibility obligations (WCAG 2.0 Level AA) hitting private organizations at the 50+ employee threshold. It is irrelevant to a Calgary shop except as a reference standard — citing 'AODA compliance' to an Alberta client would be a category error.
notes: Most 'Canada website accessibility law' marketing content centers on AODA and overstates its reach; do not let that leak into Alberta client copy.

claim: The real Alberta legal exposure for an inaccessible shop website is the Alberta Human Rights Act, s.4 — which prohibits discrimination in 'goods, services, accommodation or facilities customarily available to the public' on the ground of physical or mental disability, and imposes a duty to accommodate to the point of undue hardship. A public-facing website is plausibly a 'service customarily available to the public,' so a person with a disability who cannot use the site could file a human-rights complaint with the Alberta Human Rights Commission. This is the liability baseline, not a proactive WCAG statute.
notes: This is the accurate framing: the duty is reactive/complaint-driven and accommodation-based (undue hardship defence available), NOT a per-page WCAG audit mandate. Whether a website specifically counts as a s.4 'service' has not been settled by a published Alberta tribunal decision in the sources consulted — frame as 'plausible exposure,' not 'established.' This mirrors how the existing research treats PIPA: a real obligation, stated precisely, product/design guidance not legal advice.

claim: Disability is the single most-cited ground in Canadian human-rights complaints (49% of 663 accepted Canadian Human Rights Commission complaints in 2023), and tribunals can order compensatory remedies. So while web accessibility is enforced reactively (by complaint) rather than by statute in Alberta, the underlying disability-discrimination channel is active and well-used — the exposure is real even if web-specific cases are not yet common.
notes: The 49% figure is federal (CHRC) data; the Alberta Human Rights Commission is the correct forum for our provincially regulated clients. Use the federal stat only to show disability complaints are the dominant category nationally, not as Alberta-specific volume.

claim: The US-style web-accessibility demand-letter / lawsuit industry (4,500+ digital-accessibility lawsuits filed in 2023) is an ADA Title III phenomenon and does NOT transfer to Canada. The sources consulted cite no Canadian demand-letter campaign and no settled Canadian web-accessibility case against a small private business; the Canadian channel is a slower human-rights-complaint process, not serial plaintiff litigation. Do not import US risk framing into Alberta client conversations.
