notes: Accessibility-vendor marketing conflates US lawsuit volume with Canadian risk to sell overlay/audit products. The honest position: low litigation risk for a Calgary shop, but a genuine (if rarely exercised) human-rights duty-to-accommodate exposure. Avoid fear-selling; it would also conflict with the no-retainer, client-owns-everything model.

claim: Safe compliance claim for a no-retainer build: we should NOT claim 'WCAG 2.x AA compliant' or 'fully accessible' as a guarantee, because (a) accessibility is point-in-time and degrades as the client self-edits content/images in Storyblok after handoff, and (b) full AA conformance requires ongoing auditing we explicitly do not provide. The defensible claim is build-time and effort-based: e.g., 'Built to meet WCAG 2.2 Level AA best practices at handoff — semantic HTML, AA-contrast tokens, keyboard navigation, alt-text slots, and accessible forms.' Frame as best-practice adherence, not certified ongoing compliance.
notes: Directly parallels the repo's PIPA stance (build a compliant artifact, but client owns ongoing obligations). The CLAUDE.md design-quality bar already mandates 'WCAG AA contrast, tap targets' — so the engine already delivers the substance; the claim language just must be scoped to build-time best-practice, with a one-line disclaimer that the client owns content changes post-handoff and that this is not legal advice. WCAG 2.2 AA is the current best-practice target (supersedes the 2.0/2.1 referenced by older AODA/ACA); building to 2.2 AA over-satisfies every Canadian statutory standard.

claim: Building to WCAG 2.x AA is therefore a voluntary product-quality and risk-mitigation choice for Alberta shops, not a legal requirement — but it is the correct default: it pre-empts any Alberta Human Rights Act duty-to-accommodate complaint, improves SEO/usability, and is a cheap differentiator. The audit rubric's a11y grade should be positioned to clients as 'reduces your human-rights-complaint exposure and improves reach,' not 'makes you legally compliant.'
notes: 'common-but-missing' = most prospect sites we audit will fail basic a11y, so it is a real upsell/differentiation point; but it must be sold as quality + risk-reduction, not legal mandate. Ties the a11y dimension back to the existing engagement-scoping rubric (A-F grade -> tier).

claim: html-has-lang: assert the root <html> element carries a non-empty lang attribute. Detection: parse static HTML (regex /<html[^>]+\blang\s*=\s*["'][^"']+["']/i, or a DOM parser reading document.documentElement.lang). Maps to WCAG 3.1.1 Language of Page (Level A). This is fully deterministic from served HTML, needs no JS execution or browser, and slots directly into the existing inventoryFromHtml() regex-inventory pattern. Expected false-positive rate: near 0% (a present-or-absent attribute; the only edge case is a lang set client-side by JS, which is vanishingly rare on the Astro static sites this engine produces). WebAIM Million 2026 found 13.5% of home pages still lack a document language, so it is a real, common, cheaply-detectable miss.
notes: axe rule html-has-lang, tagged wcag2a / SC 3.1.1. Trivial regex check; lowest-risk new a11y check to add.

claim: html-lang-valid / valid-lang: when a lang attribute is present, assert its value is a well-formed BCP-47 language subtag (e.g. matches /^[a-z]{2,3}(-[A-Za-z0-9]{2,8})*$/ and the primary subtag is a known ISO 639 code). Detection: parse the lang value from static HTML and validate against a small allowlist (en, en-CA, fr, fr-CA cover virtually all Calgary shops) or a BCP-47 regex. Maps to WCAG 3.1.1 (html-lang-valid, Level A) and 3.1.2 Language of Parts (valid-lang, Level AA). Fully static, no browser. Expected false-positive rate: low (~2-5%) if a strict allowlist rejects legitimate-but-unusual tags; near 0% with a permissive BCP-47 syntax check. Pair with html-has-lang so the presence and validity checks compose.
notes: valid-lang is one of the few wcag2aa-tagged static checks. Use a permissive syntax regex to keep FP near zero; an over-strict allowlist is the main FP source.

claim: image-alt: assert every element has an alt attribute (present, including empty alt="" for decorative images) or an explicit role="presentation"/role="none". Detection: parse static HTML; flag any with no alt attribute and no presentational role. Best done with a DOM parser (cheerio/linkedom) to enumerate nodes reliably rather than fragile regex, but still 100% static, no browser, no JS. Maps to WCAG 1.1.1 Non-text Content (Level A). Expected false-positive rate: low-to-moderate (~5-15%): the check verifies alt PRESENCE only, not alt QUALITY (a meaningful image with alt="" or alt="image" passes the static check but fails real WCAG). It also cannot judge CSS background images used as content. WebAIM Million 2026: 53.1% of home pages have missing alt text, making this one of the two highest-volume real-world failures.
notes: Report as presence-only ('N of M images missing alt'), not as a pass/fail of alt quality, to keep FP honest. input-image-alt, area-alt, object-alt are sibling Level-A static checks for the rarer <input type=image>/<area>/<object> cases.

claim: label / form input association: for each form control that requires a name (input [excluding hidden/button/submit/image], select, textarea), assert it has an accessible name via one of: a wrapping or for-associated <label>, aria-label, or aria-labelledby pointing at an existing id. Detection: DOM parser over static HTML to resolve label[for], implicit label nesting, and id references; no browser/JS needed. Maps to WCAG 1.3.1 / 4.1.2 (axe rule label, Level A). Expected false-positive rate: moderate (~10-20%): placeholder-only or title-only inputs pass a naive check but are real failures, and aria-labelledby resolution across DOM is the main bug-source for false negatives in axe itself. Treat as 'every input has a programmatic label' rather than a full name-quality check. WebAIM Million 2026: 51% of home pages have unlabeled form inputs - directly relevant since every shop ships a contact form (the contactForm inventory item).
notes: Highest-value vertical-agnostic check given every site has a contact form. FP risk concentrated in placeholder-as-label and cross-node aria-labelledby; exclude type=hidden and require id-target existence to cut FPs.

claim: button-name / link-name: assert every
notes: Directly reinforces the existing clickToCall/bookingLink conversion items - a tel:/booking link with no accessible name is both an a11y fail and a conversion leak. Use a DOM accessible-name approximation, not bare regex, to handle icon buttons.

claim: meta-viewport (zoom not disabled): assert the viewport <meta> does NOT contain user-scalable=no and does NOT set maximum-scale below 2 (i.e. zoom is not suppressed). Detection: parse the content attribute of <meta name="viewport"> from static HTML with a regex/string parse; static, no browser, no JS. Maps to WCAG 1.4.4 Resize Text (Level AA; axe rule meta-viewport, tagged wcag2aa). This is a strict superset refinement of the existing mobileViewport inventory item, which today only checks that a viewport meta exists - it would currently PASS a site that disables zoom. Expected false-positive rate: near 0% (deterministic string inspection of a single attribute). High value because it is the rare wcag2aa criterion that is fully static.
notes: Cheapest AA-level upgrade: extend the existing mobileViewport regex to also reject user-scalable=no and maximum-scale<2. Our own Astro templates won't fail it, but prospect-audit targets often do.

claim: document-title: assert the document contains a non-empty <title> element. Detection: regex/DOM parse of static HTML head (/<title[^>]*>\s*\S/i); static, no browser. Maps to WCAG 2.4.2 Page Titled (Level A; axe rule document-title). Expected false-positive rate: near 0% (present-and-non-empty is unambiguous). Lower real-world prevalence as a failure than alt/labels/contrast, but free to add and partially overlaps the SEO grade already computed.
notes: Overlaps SEO checks; include mainly for completeness of the a11y sub-grade. Trivial, zero-FP regex.

claim: heading-order / single-h1: assert headings (h1-h6 and role=heading[aria-level]) appear without skipping levels on the way down (no h1->h3 jump) and that exactly one h1 exists. Detection: DOM parser collecting heading elements in document order from static HTML; static, no browser, no JS. NOTE: in axe-core heading-order and page-has-heading-one are tagged best-practice, NOT a WCAG success criterion - so this is an industry-accepted a11y heuristic, not strictly a WCAG 2.x AA conformance check. Expected false-positive rate: moderate (~15-25%): legitimate designs sometimes skip levels intentionally, and visually-styled non-heading text vs real headings can't always be distinguished statically; flag as advisory, not a hard fail. Useful because our shared section components emit a predictable heading structure, so deviations are usually genuine authoring mistakes.
notes: Be explicit in the rubric that this is best-practice, not WCAG-required, to avoid mislabeling the a11y grade. Higher FP than the attribute checks; ship as advisory.

claim: color-contrast: assert text foreground/background pairs meet WCAG AA contrast (4.5:1 normal, 3:1 for large text >=18.66px bold or >=24px). Detection: REQUIRES computed styles - i.e. a real DOM with CSS applied. This is NOT achievable from raw static HTML+regex; it needs a headless browser (the repo already runs Playwright for screenshot verification) to read getComputedStyle color/background-color/font-size/font-weight per text node, then compute the WCAG contrast ratio. Maps to WCAG 1.4.3 Contrast (Minimum) (Level AA; axe rule color-contrast, the single highest-volume real failure: 83.9% of WebAIM Million home pages). Expected false-positive AND false-negative rate: moderate (~10-20%): axe/Deque document that contrast cannot be reliably evaluated for text over background-image, CSS gradients, pseudo-element or border backgrounds, opacity on the foreground, or overlapping/positioned elements - these yield 'incomplete'/cannot-tell rather than pass/fail. Treat ambiguous cases as 'needs manual review', not as failures, to keep the check trustworthy.
notes: Highest-impact a11y issue by real-world volume but the ONLY one in this set that breaks the 'pure static HTML/CSS, no browser' constraint - it needs computed styles. Cleanest path: inject axe-core via the existing Playwright harness on the built preview, or compute contrast on text nodes via getComputedStyle. Must surface 'incomplete' separately from 'fail'.

claim: Detection-method split for packages/audit: the testable AA/A checks fall into two implementation tiers. Tier 1 (string/DOM over the fetched static HTML, no browser - fits the existing inventoryFromHtml/fetchPage.ts pattern exactly): html-has-lang, html-lang-valid/valid-lang, document-title, meta-viewport zoom, image-alt presence, input-image-alt/area-alt/object-alt, frame-title, plus the DOM-parser checks label, button-name, link-name, heading-order (these benefit from a lightweight HTML parser like cheerio/linkedom rather than regex). Tier 2 (needs computed styles via the already-present Playwright browser on the built preview): color-contrast only. Recommendation: add Tier-1 as deterministic inventory items (mirroring the 12-item conversion inventory), and run axe-core or a contrast pass through Playwright for color-contrast, clearly separating 'fail' from 'needs-review/incomplete'. Overall, ~29.5% of WCAG 2.2 criteria are fully automatable but a handful (contrast, name/role/value, language) account for ~58.8% of real defects, so this small set captures most actual a11y wins.
notes: Architectural guidance for where each check lands in packages/audit. Tier-1 = pure-static, extend inventory.ts. Tier-2 (contrast) = reuse existing Playwright harness. Prefer cheerio/linkedom over regex for element-enumeration checks (img/label/button/link) to cut false positives vs the existing regex approach.

claim: Every tel: phone link (in ContactNAP and CTA) must have an accessible name that conveys it is a phone number / call action; when the visible link text is the phone number itself, that text alone satisfies the name, but an icon-only call link (phone glyph with no text) MUST carry an aria-label like aria-label="Call (403) 555-0123". Pass = link has a non-empty programmatic name describing purpose; Fail = an <a href="tel:..."> whose only content is an icon/SVG with no aria-label, alt, or visible text (F89).
notes: SC 4.1.2 (A) + 1.1.1 (A). In the current shared components ContactNAP.astro line 8 and CTA.astro line 8 use visible phone text, which passes. Risk arises only if a vertical adds an icon-only call button (e.g., a phone glyph in SiteNav) — that must get aria-label by default.

claim: Generic CTA labels like "Call" and "Book online" pass Link Purpose (In Context) only when the surrounding section heading/context makes the destination unambiguous; the bare visible text plus its programmatically-associated context (heading, list item, the tel:/booking href) must let a user know where the link goes. Pass = purpose determinable from link text + context; Fail = link text is ambiguous AND no programmatic context disambiguates it.
notes: SC 2.4.4 is Level A and explicitly allows context (same section/heading). CTA.astro's "Call" button under the #book heading passes in-context; however, more descriptive defaults ("Call us", "Book an appointment") are lower-risk and recommended since screen-reader users navigating a links list hear text out of context.

claim: Every interactive target on the page — tel: links, the booking/order CTA buttons, the nav Book button, the address/map link, hours-table links if any, and all contact-form controls — must be at least 24x24 CSS px, OR be spaced so a 24px-diameter circle centered on each target does not intersect a neighbor (24px of clear space between edges), OR fall under the inline/essential exception. Pass = computed clickable box >=24x24px or adequate spacing; Fail = a target smaller than 24x24px with neighbors closer than 24px and no exception.
notes: SC 2.5.8 Level AA, 24x24 CSS px. The shared button pattern (px-6 py-3) and nav Book (px-4 py-2) clear 24px height; the at-risk targets are inline text links — ContactNAP tel: and map links are inline so they qualify for the inline exception, but the two CTA buttons sit in a flex gap-4 row (16px gap) which is fine because each button already exceeds 24x24. Setting min-height/min-width 24px (or min 44px for AAA-friendly mobile) on shared link/button base is the robust default.

claim: If the shop ships a contact form, every field that collects user input (name, email, phone, message) must have a visible label or instruction presented to all users (3.3.2, Level A) AND that label must be programmatically associated with its input via <label for>/id or aria-labelledby (1.3.1, Level A). Placeholder-only fields FAIL because placeholders disappear on input and are not reliable accessible names. Pass = each input has a persistent visible label correctly associated; Fail = placeholder-as-label, orphaned label, or unlabeled input.
notes: 3.3.2 (label presented to all) + 1.3.1 (programmatic association) are distinct and both required. The repo's audit tracks contactForm presence but not its a11y; a shared ContactForm.astro should ship labelled fields by default. Required fields must be marked in text (not color/asterisk alone) and ideally with the required attribute + aria-required.

claim: Contact-form fields collecting user information must use the correct HTML autocomplete token so the input purpose is programmatically determinable: autocomplete="name" (or given-name/family-name), autocomplete="email", autocomplete="tel". Pass = each personal-data field has the correct token; Fail = missing or wrong autocomplete value (F107). This also enables browser autofill, raising conversion.
notes: SC 1.3.5 Level AA. Cheap to ship correctly in a shared ContactForm.astro and doubles as a conversion win (faster autofill on mobile). Use type="email"/type="tel" inputs alongside the tokens to surface the right mobile keyboard.

claim: When the contact form auto-detects an input error (empty required field, malformed email), the field in error must be identified AND the error described in text near the field — not by color/border-only. Pass = on validation failure, a text error message identifies which field and what is wrong, programmatically tied via aria-describedby and announced (aria-live/role=alert); Fail = silent re-submit, color-only indication, or generic "error" with no field identity.
notes: SC 3.3.1 Level A. With Web3Forms/static handlers, native HTML5 constraint validation (required, type=email) satisfies the baseline because the browser identifies the field and shows text; relying on it is acceptable. If JS validation is added, it must inject text errors with aria-describedby + role=alert. The repo's current forms (landing) should be checked against this.

claim: The hours block must expose the day -> hours relationship programmatically: either a real <table> with a row/column header scope, or a semantically paired list. The current Hours.astro uses a <ul> of flex rows with two <span>s, which conveys the relationship visually but provides no programmatic day/value association. Pass = relationship is programmatically determinable (table th scope, or dl/dt/dd pairing); Borderline/Fail-risk = generic spans whose day-to-hours pairing exists only by visual layout.
notes: SC 1.3.1 Level A. Low real-world severity (each list item keeps day and hours adjacent in DOM order, so a screen reader reads 'Monday 9-5' in sequence), but the cleanest fix is a <dl> with <dt>day</dt><dd>hours</dd> or a table with scope='row'. Worth fixing in shared Hours.astro so every shop passes by default. Also ensure 'Closed' is text, not styling.

claim: The address/map link must have descriptive link text in context — the visible address as the link text passes Link Purpose, but the link should clearly read as 'directions/map'. An icon-only map pin link MUST have an accessible name (aria-label="Get directions to <name>, <address>"). Pass = map link has descriptive accessible name; Fail = map pin icon link with no aria-label, or an opaque 'here'/'map' with no programmatic context.
notes: SC 2.4.4 (A) + 4.1.2 (A). ContactNAP.astro line 9 uses the address string as link text, which passes; the failure mode is a future icon-only directions button. Opening maps in a new tab should be signalled (rel/target plus text or aria) so users aren't surprised.

claim: Conversion link/button text and their backgrounds must meet WCAG AA contrast (4.5:1 for normal text, 3:1 for large >=24px/18.66px-bold and for the button's non-text boundary/focus indicator). The token system must be validated so primary/primary-foreground and accent/foreground pairs used on CTA buttons hit 4.5:1. Pass = measured contrast >=4.5:1 (or 3:1 for large); Fail = any CTA label below threshold against its token background.
notes: SC 1.4.3 Contrast (Minimum) is Level AA (CLAUDE.md already states AA contrast). Because every shop swaps the OKLCH token palette, the CTA pairings must be contrast-checked per theme — this is the highest-frequency a11y regression in a token-themed engine. Add a build-time/theme-review contrast check on --primary/--primary-foreground and --accent/--foreground.

claim: All conversion controls must be keyboard reachable and show a visible focus indicator, and the sticky nav must not trap or obscure focused targets. Native <a> elements (as used throughout the shared components) are focusable by default, so the bar is: do not remove the default focus outline without replacing it, and ensure the sticky header's blur/overlay does not cover a focused in-page anchor target. Pass = every CTA/link is tabbable with a perceivable focus style; Fail = outline:none with no replacement, or non-anchor click handlers with no role/tabindex.
notes: Covers SC 2.1.1 Keyboard (A) and 2.4.7 Focus Visible (AA). The shared components correctly use semantic <a>, so the main risk is a global CSS reset that kills :focus-visible. Ship a default :focus-visible outline in base.css using a token (e.g. outline ring on --primary/--accent) so every shop passes.

claim: WCAG 2.x SC 1.4.3 (Contrast Minimum, Level AA) requires text and images of text to have a contrast ratio of at least 4.5:1 against their background for normal-size text. In the token system this is the binding threshold for every text-on-surface pairing: --foreground on --background, --primary-foreground on --primary, --accent-foreground on --accent, and any body/link/caption text rendered on --muted or --card.
notes: 4.5:1 is the floor for normal text; the repo's per-shop re-theming of --foreground/--primary means this pair must be re-validated for every new theme.css, not assumed inherited.

claim: Under SC 1.4.3 a relaxed 3:1 ratio applies to 'large text', defined precisely as >=18pt (>=24px) regular weight OR >=14pt bold (>=18.66px). This means hero headlines, large display type set in --font-display, and section headings can pass at 3:1, but body copy, prices in Services, FAQ answers, hours-table cells, and nav links must still hit 4.5:1.
notes: W3C: 1pt = 1.333px, so 14pt~18.5px and 18pt~24px. WebAIM cites 18.67px bold / 24px. The px boundary matters because Tailwind text-lg (18px) regular is NOT large text and needs 4.5:1, while text-2xl+ headings qualify for 3:1.

claim: WCAG defines contrast strictly via sRGB relative luminance L = 0.2126*R + 0.7152*G + 0.0722*B (after sRGB gamma-linearization), then (L1+0.05)/(L2+0.05). It is explicitly NOT computed from perceptual lightness. Therefore OKLCH's L channel is not a substitute for the WCAG ratio: tokens authored in OKLCH must be converted to sRGB and run through the luminance formula to certify AA.
notes: Hue-independent by design so it holds under color-blindness. This is the core reason a token gate cannot just diff OKLCH L values.

claim: OKLCH lightness difference is only a rough heuristic for WCAG contrast, not a guarantee: at equal L, higher chroma can change the sRGB luminance and shift the ratio, so two saturated brand colors with a 'safe-looking' L gap can still fail 4.5:1. A token-level gate must compute the real sRGB ratio rather than gate on delta-L. As a directional rule, only when OKLCH L >= ~0.72 does black reliably out-contrast white on that surface, and below ~0.65 white reliably out-contrasts black.
notes: Practical implication for theme.css: pick --primary-foreground (white vs near-black) by the --primary L crossing ~0.65-0.72, then VERIFY. The barber theme's --primary L=0.45 correctly pairs a near-white --primary-foreground, consistent with this rule.

claim: SC 1.4.11 (Non-text Contrast, Level AA) requires UI components and meaningful graphical objects to have >=3:1 against adjacent colors. For the token system this binds: --border where it is the only thing defining an input/button/card boundary, the boundary of form fields in the contact form, focus indicators, and informational icons rendered via Icon.astro. A purely decorative hairline border is exempt, but a functional control boundary is not.
notes: Repo risk: themes set --border very light (barber --border L=0.88 vs --background L=0.98 ~ well under 3:1). That fails 1.4.11 wherever the border is the sole affordance for a form field — a real, automatable defect.

claim: A button (or control) with sufficiently contrasting visible text/icon does NOT additionally require its background to reach 3:1 against the page under 1.4.11 — the button background-vs-page is not a 1.4.11 obligation when text carries the affordance. So --primary buttons on --background need not pass 3:1 surface contrast; only the button label (--primary-foreground on --primary at 4.5:1 via 1.4.3) and any state-only indicators must pass.
notes: Prevents over-flagging: a token gate should NOT fail a low-contrast --primary-on---background pairing for solid buttons with labels. It SHOULD flag bordered/outline controls where --border is the only boundary.

claim: Inactive/disabled components, text that is part of a logo or brand name, and decorative/non-readable text are exempt from both 1.4.3 and 1.4.11. A token contrast gate must therefore allow-list these cases (e.g. disabled-state tokens, wordmark text) to avoid false failures, while still validating all active text and functional controls.
notes: Relevant because the engine's nav wordmark (shop name) is logo-exempt, and any future disabled CTA state need not meet 4.5:1.

claim: The token contrast gate CAN be fully automated at build time. culori (a maintained JS color library) parses oklch() strings directly (parse / parseOklch) and exposes wcagLuminance(color) and wcagContrast(colorA, colorB), so a Node script can read theme.css, resolve the documented token pairings, compute true sRGB WCAG ratios, and fail the build below threshold — no browser or rendered DOM required.
notes: Concrete recipe: regex-extract the --token: oklch(...) declarations, build the pairing matrix (foreground/background, primary-foreground/primary, accent-foreground/accent, border/background, muted-foreground/muted+background), assert wcagContrast >= 4.5 (text) or 3 (border/large), exit non-zero on fail. Fits the repo's 'skill + runnable script' pattern.

claim: The specific token pairings that MUST be contrast-validated for a Studio0rbit shop theme are: (1) --foreground / --background >=4.5; (2) --foreground / --muted and --muted-foreground / --muted >=4.5 (muted sections render body text); (3) --muted-foreground / --background >=4.5 (used for subdued copy on page bg); (4) --primary-foreground / --primary >=4.5; (5) --accent-foreground / --accent >=4.5; (6) --border / --background >=3 (only where border is a functional boundary). Display headings on these surfaces may use 3:1.
notes: Derived from the documented token set in CLAUDE.md + base.css mapping; --muted-foreground is the highest-risk token because it is intentionally low-contrast yet carries readable secondary text (e.g. barber --muted-foreground L=0.45 on --muted L=0.94 should be checked but likely passes ~5-6:1).

claim: Non-underlined links must satisfy a compound rule: 4.5:1 against the background (1.4.3) AND >=3:1 against surrounding body text (1.4.1 Use of Color), plus a non-color cue on hover/focus. If a theme styles links with --primary or --accent inline in body copy, the link color must be checked against BOTH --background and --foreground, not just the background.
notes: Implication: an automated gate that only checks link-vs-background misses the link-vs-bodytext leg. Underlining links sidesteps the 3:1-vs-text requirement entirely and is the simplest engine default.

claim: WCAG contrast is hue-independent and identical for all color-vision types, so a single sRGB-luminance gate per token pairing is sufficient for AA color-contrast — there is no need for separate per-deficiency contrast checks. (Color-as-sole-meaning is a different criterion, 1.4.1, handled by adding text/icons, not by contrast math.)
notes: Simplifies the gate: one numeric check per pairing covers 1.4.3/1.4.11 for all users; do not over-engineer with simulated-color-blindness contrast passes.

claim: APCA (the WCAG 3 perceptual contrast model used by some OKLCH tooling) is informational only and not yet a conformance standard; for a 2026 Calgary local-shop AA deliverable the build gate must assert against the WCAG 2.x ratio (4.5/3), optionally reporting APCA alongside. Gating on APCA instead of WCAG 2 would not satisfy a contractual AA claim.
notes: OKLCH-native tools often surface APCA because it tracks OKLCH lightness better, but the legally/contractually meaningful target remains WCAG 2.x AA. Report APCA as advisory, gate on WCAG 2.

claim: Hero/masthead text overlaid on photographic imagery must meet WCAG 1.4.3 contrast against the actual pixels behind it (4.5:1 normal text, 3:1 for large text ≥18pt/14pt-bold); a fixed scrim/gradient overlay does not guarantee this when the per-shop photo is bright or light-toned.
notes: Directly relevant to the engine: packages/shared/src/components/Hero.astro renders text-white over a FIXED gradient overlay (from-black/45 via-black/55 to-black/75). That overlay is constant regardless of the client-supplied heroImage, so a bright/snow/light photo can drop the effective contrast below 4.5:1. The 'always reused' guarantee to bake in: a strong-enough default scrim (e.g. a solid/near-solid dark layer or a min ~60% black overlay) plus a text-shadow, OR a contained text panel, so contrast holds for ANY photo a client uploads via Storyblok. This is the highest-leverage a11y fix in the engine.

claim: Avoid text-as-image for headings/menus/prices; use real selectable text styled via tokens. WCAG 1.4.4 and 1.4.5 disfavor images of text because they pixelate on zoom and cannot have contrast adjusted; exceptions exist only for logos/brand names.
notes: The engine already favors real text (Services.astro, Faq.astro render text from content, not baked images), so this is largely a guarantee the architecture already provides. Risk surface is client-uploaded imagery containing menu/price text as a flat graphic. Logo/brand-name text in the SiteNav wordmark is exempt from the contrast requirement.

claim: Every keyboard-operable control (sticky nav links, booking/CTA buttons, FAQ summaries, mobile-menu toggle) must show a visible keyboard focus indicator (WCAG 2.4.7 Focus Visible, AA); removing the default outline without a replacement is a documented failure.
notes: Engine status: SiteNav.astro and Hero.astro CTAs rely on the browser default outline (none is explicitly removed, which is GOOD), and Faq.astro applies list-none to <summary> but does not remove its focus ring. The risk is per-shop theme.css or a global reset stripping outlines. Bake-in guarantee: a shared :focus-visible style in base.css using a token (e.g. outline via --primary/--ring) so every component inherits a visible, on-brand focus ring and no shop can accidentally ship invisible focus. This is an 'always reused' guarantee the engine should own centrally rather than per shop.

claim: The collapsed mobile navigation must keep all nav destinations operable; hiding nav links on small screens without an accessible menu/toggle removes content and fails keyboard operability and equivalent-access expectations.
notes: Concrete engine defect: SiteNav.astro renders section links with class 'hidden ... sm:inline' — on mobile the links are simply NOT rendered/visible and there is NO hamburger menu, so mobile users lose in-page navigation entirely. Because these are anchor links to on-page sections (#services/#hours/#visit) the content is still reachable by scrolling, lowering severity, but it is a real gap. If a mobile menu/toggle is added it must be keyboard-operable, have a visible focus state, and a ≥24x24px (ideally ≈44px) tap target per 2.5.8.

claim: Subtle UI motion the design bar calls for (hover scale, transitions) should be gated behind @media (prefers-reduced-motion: reduce); the engine currently ships transform/scale animations with no reduced-motion guard.
notes: WCAG 2.3.3 (Animation from Interactions) is Level AAA, so honoring prefers-reduced-motion is best-practice rather than strict AA — hence should-have, not must-have. Engine evidence: Hero.astro CTA uses 'transition hover:scale-[1.03]' and SiteNav/CTA use hover transitions, none guarded. Bake-in guarantee: one global block in base.css — @media (prefers-reduced-motion: reduce){*{animation-duration:.01ms!important;transition-duration:.01ms!important;scroll-behavior:auto!important}} — written once, inherited by every shop. Cheap, universal, and matches the 'reused guarantee' the question asks for.

claim: Pages must reflow without two-dimensional scrolling at a 320 CSS-px viewport (i.e. 1280px zoomed to 400%) per WCAG 1.4.10 Reflow (AA); single-column responsive stacking satisfies this for these content-light shop sites.
notes: Largely satisfied for free by the engine's Tailwind responsive, single-column-on-mobile layout (max-w-* + px-6, sm: breakpoints). Watch-outs that break reflow: fixed-width hero text, wide service/price rows that don't wrap, embedded maps, and the sticky nav consuming vertical space at high zoom. Verify in the build pipeline by testing at 320px width / 400% zoom with no horizontal scrollbar. This is mostly an 'already guaranteed' item to confirm, not build.

claim: Text must be resizable to 200% without loss of content or functionality per WCAG 1.4.4 Resize Text (AA); avoid viewport-unit or fixed-px font sizing and clipping containers that prevent enlargement.
notes: 1.4.4 (200% text zoom, AA) is distinct from and complementary to 1.4.10 reflow (400% page zoom). The engine's rem/Tailwind type scale generally satisfies this; the specific risk is the Hero's large clamped/viewport-sized display type and any fixed-height bands that could clip text at 200%. Verify alongside reflow in the screenshot stage.

claim: Interactive tap targets on mobile (click-to-call, booking/CTA buttons, nav toggle, FAQ summaries) should be at least 24x24 CSS px (WCAG 2.2 2.5.8 Target Size Minimum, AA), with ~44px recommended for primary actions.
notes: 2.5.8 is new in WCAG 2.2 and Level AA. Engine status is mostly good: Hero CTA (px-9 py-4) and nav Book button (px-4 py-2) clear 24px comfortably. Tighter spots to check: the FAQ '+' toggle (it's the whole summary row, so fine), inline text links in the sticky nav, and the click-to-call link in ContactNAP — ensure the call target itself is a sized button/row, not a bare inline phone number. Spacing exception applies if targets are <24px but well separated.

claim: A skip-to-content link is a low-cost keyboard-accessibility win (WCAG 2.4.1 Bypass Blocks, Level A) that the sticky nav makes worthwhile; the engine currently ships no skip link.
notes: 2.4.1 is Level A (technically below the AA scope of this dimension but bundled here as a cheap related guarantee). Engine evidence: SiteNav.astro has no skip link, and the page top anchor is '#top'. For single-page shop sites with a short sticky header the practical benefit is modest, hence nice-to-have, but it is trivial to bake once into the shared layout (a visually-hidden, focusable anchor to #main) and applies to every shop for free.

claim: Decorative hero/background images must carry empty alt (alt="") so screen readers skip them, while meaningful content images need descriptive alt text (WCAG 1.1.1 Non-text Content, Level A).
notes: Missing/incorrect alt text is the #2 most common web a11y failure (53.1% of home pages, WebAIM Million 2026). 1.1.1 is Level A but foundational to the non-text concern in this question. Engine evidence: Hero.astro correctly sets alt="" on the decorative background photo (good). The reused guarantee to enforce: any client-uploaded CONTENT image surfaced through Storyblok (e.g. gallery/feature photos) must carry an editable alt field, while purely decorative backgrounds stay alt="". This is a CMS-content-model guarantee, not just a component one.

claim: Dental practice sites convert primarily on an online new-patient appointment request (book/request a visit) rather than a booking-for-purchase or order CTA; the appointment request is the dominant above-the-fold conversion action, supported by click-to-call.
notes: Differs from barber/cafe/spa 'booking/order': the action is a request-an-appointment (often a request, not an instant-confirm slot), because new-patient onboarding precedes scheduling.

claim: A dental site's distinguishing pre-visit feature is new-patient + insurance intake: digital forms that collect demographics, health history, consents, and insurance card uploads (front/back photo) so the front desk can verify coverage and flag medical alerts before the appointment. This is a second conversion layer beyond the appointment request.
notes: Insurance verification 'tomorrow's appointments this afternoon' is the operational payoff. Intake forms must be mobile-responsive (most patients complete on phone); aim for completion 24h before the visit.

claim: Required dental page/section anatomy: hero with appointment-request CTA + click-to-call, services/treatments (general/cosmetic/emergency), new-patient info + insurance/financing/accepted-plans, online appointment request form, new-patient intake form (health history + insurance upload), team/doctor bios (trust), reviews, hours, location/map, FAQ, and LocalBusiness/Dentist schema.
notes: No e-commerce. Trust signals (doctor bios, credentials) weigh heavily in healthcare. Schema type Dentist (subtype of MedicalBusiness/LocalBusiness).

claim: Law firm sites have NO e-commerce; the single primary conversion action is a consultation/case-evaluation request (intake), expressed as a specific CTA like 'Schedule a Free 15-Minute Case Evaluation' rather than generic 'Contact Us', supported by click-to-call.
notes: Differs sharply from barber/cafe/spa booking/order: high-consideration, no transaction online; the CTA captures a lead for a screened consultation, not a purchase.

claim: Law firm anatomy is organized around case-type practice-area pages (e.g. personal injury, immigration, estate planning, family) each with its own targeted, empathetic messaging and a practice-area-specific intake form; a single broad 'we handle everything' page underperforms.
notes: Practice-area pages are the law-firm analog of a 'services' list but each is a dedicated landing/conversion page. Practice-specific intake questions improve screening/case evaluation.

claim: Law firm intake forms should be short (only essential fields: contact, type of legal issue, brief description, preferred contact) because shorter initial forms have higher completion rates; deeper intake happens after the consultation is booked.
notes: Place the same primary CTA / short form on home, practice-area, and About pages.

claim: Required law firm page/section anatomy: home with single primary consultation CTA, per-case-type practice-area pages, attorney/team bios with credentials + bar memberships, results/testimonials (trust), short consultation/intake form, contact + click-to-call, and LegalService/Attorney LocalBusiness schema. No menu, no ordering, no e-commerce.
notes: Credentials and case results are the trust currency. Mobile-optimized (over half of legal searches start on phone).

claim: Auto repair sites carry a DUAL primary conversion: a dominant above-the-fold 'Schedule Service' appointment CTA AND a quote/estimate request, both backed by sticky click-to-call. The appointment booking is the dominant action; the estimate request is the auto-specific second path absent from barber/cafe/spa.
notes: Differs from barber/cafe/spa: customers want a price-anchored estimate before committing, so a quote/estimate-request slot (or instant-estimator embed) supplements the booking CTA. Online booking aims for <5 clicks.

claim: Auto repair homepage must instantly answer three questions — 'Can you fix my problem?' (service list + makes serviced), 'Can I trust you?' (real shop photos, reviews, ASE/AAA/NAPA certifications, warranties), and 'How do I book?' (dominant schedule/call CTA).
notes: Certification badges and real (non-stock) shop/team photos are auto-specific trust drivers.

claim: Auto repair sites should publish a transparent service menu with pricing (brakes, diagnostics, AC, tires, etc.), each service ideally linked to a dedicated service page; price transparency is a stated consumer expectation in this vertical.
notes: Service-with-price list maps cleanly to the existing Services component; estimate request supplements where exact pricing varies by vehicle.

claim: Required auto repair page/section anatomy: hero with Schedule/Call CTA + credibility subheading, services-with-pricing, makes/vehicles serviced, quote/estimate-request form, appointment-booking form, certifications/warranties, real shop photos, reviews, NAP + map/directions, service-area strip, FAQ, coupon/offer near CTA, and LocalBusiness/AutoRepair schema.
notes: AutoRepair is a schema.org subtype of AutomotiveBusiness/LocalBusiness.

claim: Fitness/gym studio sites center on a class schedule/timetable as the core content and an embedded, bookable, live-updating class calendar (book + pay in-page via Stripe-backed embeds like ClassFit/GymMaster); the timetable booking is the operational conversion engine.
notes: Calendar embeds drop into any page in ~2 minutes; support class packs, memberships, waitlists, check-in. This is the fitness analog of the cafe ordering / barber booking embed.

claim: The single PRIMARY headline CTA for a fitness studio is a free trial / intro-offer signup (e.g. '30-day free trial', 'free first class', 'no credit card required'), which acts as the top-of-funnel entry point; membership/package signup and per-class booking are secondary conversions.
notes: Differs from barber/cafe/spa single booking/order CTA: fitness has a trial-led funnel (trial -> membership) layered on top of per-class booking, because the sale is a recurring membership, not a one-off transaction.

claim: Required fitness page/section anatomy: hero with free-trial/intro-offer CTA, embedded class schedule/timetable (bookable), membership/pricing packages, trainer/instructor profiles, results/testimonials + reviews, facility gallery, hours, location/map, FAQ, recurring-billing membership signup, and LocalBusiness/HealthClub/ExerciseGym schema.
notes: HealthClub / ExerciseGym are schema.org subtypes of LocalBusiness. Trainer bios + pricing packages are fitness-specific sections.

claim: Across all four new verticals the primary CTA is a LEAD/REQUEST or RECURRING-RELATIONSHIP action, not a one-off transactional booking/order: law = consultation request (no e-commerce), dental = new-patient appointment request + intake, auto = appointment + estimate request, fitness = free-trial signup leading to membership. This is the key divergence from the barber/cafe/spa booking-or-order assumption.
notes: Implication for the audit rubric: the 'bookingLink' conversion item is too narrow for these verticals; it should generalize to 'primary conversion action' covering consultation-request / appointment-request / estimate-request / trial-signup, plus an intake-form item for dental/law. None of the four require menuSchema; dental/law/fitness/auto all need a contactForm/intake item.

claim: Mobile-first, fast static delivery applies to all four verticals: dental intake/booking is mostly completed on phones; over half of legal searches start on mobile; over 60% of auto searches are mobile and a 1s delay can cut conversions ~7%; fitness booking is phone-led. The repo's Core Web Vitals targets (LCP<2.5s/INP<200ms/CLS<0.1) and Astro static output fit all four.
notes: Treat the auto '1s delay = 7% fewer conversions' and '60% mobile' figures as directional vendor stats; the mobile-first + sub-2s principle is mainstream and matches existing repo targets.

claim: Google explicitly recommends emitting the MOST SPECIFIC schema.org LocalBusiness subtype available rather than the generic LocalBusiness, because the specific subtype carries category-appropriate properties and helps Search categorize the business. This is a per-vertical guardrail, not a per-shop optimization.
notes: Google's doc names examples: Restaurant, DaySpa, HealthClub, Pharmacy, Store, Electrician.

claim: The verified schema.org subtype each vertical should emit: barber -> HairSalon (there is NO BarberShop type; barbers use HairSalon, parent HealthAndBeautyBusiness); cafe -> CafeOrCoffeeShop (FoodEstablishment); spa -> DaySpa or BeautySalon (HealthAndBeautyBusiness); fitness -> ExerciseGym or HealthClub (HealthClub has dual parents SportsActivityLocation + HealthAndBeautyBusiness); dental -> Dentist (dual parent LocalBusiness/MedicalBusiness AND MedicalOrganization); law -> Attorney or LegalService; auto -> AutoRepair (parent AutomotiveBusiness); retail -> Store; trades -> a HomeAndConstructionBusiness subtype (Electrician/Plumber/HVACBusiness/GeneralContractor/RoofingContractor/HousePainter/Locksmith).
notes: HealthAndBeautyBusiness subtypes confirmed: BeautySalon, DaySpa, HairSalon, HealthClub, NailSalon, TattooParlor. SportsActivityLocation subtypes: BowlingAlley, ExerciseGym, GolfCourse, HealthClub, PublicSwimmingPool, SkiResort, SportsClub, StadiumOrArena, TennisComplex. AutomotiveBusiness subtypes include AutoRepair, AutoBodyShop, AutoDealer, AutoWash. LegalService subtypes: Attorney, Notary.

claim: Emitting ANY valid LocalBusiness-subtype structured data unlocks only ONE concrete Google rich result: the knowledge-panel-style business details (hours, ratings, directions, action links). The minimum required properties for this are just name + address (PostalAddress). Everything beyond name+address is recommended, not required, for the knowledge panel.
notes: The audit rubric's localBusinessJsonLd item maps directly to this; the panel itself is driven largely by Google Business Profile, but matching on-page JSON-LD reinforces entity matching.

claim: priceRange is a recommended LocalBusiness property (Google: a Text string under 100 chars, e.g. '$$' or '$10-$50') applicable to EVERY vertical's subtype. It is inherited down the whole LocalBusiness tree (confirmed on Dentist and LegalService). It is machine/AI-readability plus a knowledge-panel signal, not its own rich-result unlock.
notes: Cheap to add in the LocalBusinessJsonLd component; one token-like field per shop.

claim: For SERVICE businesses (law, trades, auto, spa, fitness, dental), the AI-readable 'menu of services' should be modeled as Service + OfferCatalog / makesOffer, NOT as Product. Product schema is for retail goods; Service/OfferCatalog is the correct construct that AI systems parse as the firm's offerings. None of these produce a Google rich result by themselves (machine/AI-readability only).
notes: makesOffer/Offer and areaServed are real schema.org properties on LocalBusiness subtypes but are absent from Google's LocalBusiness rich-result doc -> they aid AI Overview/entity parsing, not a snippet. Our existing Services.astro content can be mirrored into OfferCatalog JSON-LD.

claim: areaServed (AdministrativeArea/GeoShape/Place/Text — the geographic area a service covers) is the high-value property for verticals that travel to the customer or serve a region rather than a storefront: trades, auto (mobile/mobile-mechanic), and law. It is machine/AI-readability (helps 'near me' / 'in Calgary' AI local recommendations), not a Google rich-result unlock.
notes: Pairs with the existing ContactNAP serviceArea content field — wire that field into JSON-LD areaServed. Calgary trades especially benefit ('serving Calgary and surrounding areas').

claim: medicalSpecialty is a Dentist/MedicalOrganization-specific property (inherited from MedicalOrganization, value type MedicalSpecialty) that only the dental (and any future medical) vertical should emit. It is machine/AI-readability — there is no dedicated medical rich result for a local dental practice from this property; the dental practice still surfaces via the generic LocalBusiness knowledge panel.
notes: Dentist's dual inheritance (LocalBusiness/MedicalBusiness + MedicalOrganization) means it can carry medicalSpecialty AND priceRange/openingHours. Niche but cheap.

claim: Google's FAQPage rich result is effectively DEAD: restricted to authoritative government/health sites in Aug 2023, then fully deprecated — FAQ rich results stopped appearing in Search on May 7 2026, with Search Console reporting and Rich Results Test support removed by June/Aug 2026. A local shop will NOT get an FAQ rich result. FAQ markup now has value only for AI/LLM parsing, not SERP real estate.
notes: CONTRADICTS the implicit assumption that the Faq.astro section earns a rich result. Keep the FAQ section for UX/AI-readability, but do not market it as a SERP-snippet feature. Search gallery no longer lists FAQ/HowTo.

claim: Event structured data is the only candidate for a fitness studio's class schedule, but Google's Event rich result supports ONLY single-event pages (one Event per URL); recurring classes require a separate Event element per occurrence on its own page. schema.org's eventSchedule/Schedule (for recurrence) exists but is NOT consumed by Google's Event rich result. So a recurring-class-schedule rich result is not practically achievable for a small studio.
notes: Required Event props: name, startDate, location (venue + postal address). For a small studio, booking-platform deep links (Mindbody/etc.) are a better path than per-class Event pages. Treat Event as out-of-scope for the standard fitness template.

claim: Restaurant/cafe is the one vertical where a content-specific schema (Menu via hasMenu -> Menu -> hasMenuSection -> hasMenuItem, plus servesCuisine) is the right subtype-specific markup. servesCuisine and menu are explicitly recommended by Google for food establishments. The Menu graph itself is machine-readability (not a guaranteed snippet); the Restaurant carousel rich result exists but is limited-access. (Already settled in restaurant/retail research — restated for completeness.)
notes: Covered by existing 2026-06-15-vertical-anatomy-restaurant-retail.md; cafe uses CafeOrCoffeeShop subtype + servesCuisine + Menu schema.

claim: Review/aggregateRating snippet markup on a local shop's OWN site does NOT earn a Google review-snippet rich result for the business itself — Google's LocalBusiness doc states review/aggregateRating are only honored when a site captures reviews ABOUT OTHER businesses (i.e., self-serving reviews are ignored). The star rating in a shop's knowledge panel comes from Google Business Profile, not on-page JSON-LD.
notes: Important guardrail: do not inject self-authored aggregateRating into a client's LocalBusiness JSON-LD expecting stars in SERP — it can be ignored or flagged. The Reviews.astro component should pull/display real GBP-style proof but not fabricate review schema.

claim: Structured data does NOT directly influence Google rankings (confirmed by Google's John Mueller, 2025); its SEO value is indirect via richer display, higher CTR, and entity understanding. For AI Overviews / answer engines, valid LocalBusiness + Service/Organization schema improves the odds of being cited in 'best [service] near me' style AI answers — so per-vertical schema is an AI-visibility (AEO/GEO) play, not a ranking lever.
notes: Sets correct expectations for one-time-fee clients: schema = display + AI-citation eligibility, not a guaranteed rank boost.

claim: Practical net for the template engine: ONE per-vertical knob (the LocalBusiness @type string) plus a small map of recommended properties is sufficient. The LocalBusinessJsonLd component should accept a vertical-driven @type and conditionally emit: priceRange (all), servesCuisine+hasMenu (cafe), areaServed (trades/auto/law), makesOffer/OfferCatalog (service verticals), medicalSpecialty (dental). All other 'rich result' wins for local shops come from GBP + name/address/hours/geo, which the generic component already covers.
notes: Keeps the 'one engine, themed per shop' rule: the only schema variance per vertical is @type + a handful of optional fields, driven from shop.ts content, not per-shop component edits. Highest leverage because it converts the current single generic JSON-LD into correct per-vertical typing with minimal code.

claim: Dental: displaying regulatory registration with the Alberta Dental Association and College / College of Dental Surgeons of Alberta (e.g. dentist name + 'registered with' the regulator) is a must-have trust signal, and the regulator requires marketing be truthful, not misleading, and bars claiming a specialty/superiority unless certified.
notes: Governed by Health Professions Act + College of Dental Surgeons of Alberta advertising rules. 'Specialist' claims need certification. Build default: registration line in footer/about, avoid 'best dentist'/specialty language unless verified.

claim: Dental: before/after photos and patient testimonials with full names require documented written patient consent before publishing on the website; sites should ship a consent-gated before/after gallery slot rather than scraping patient images.
notes: Before/after is a strong conversion element for cosmetic/ortho work but is a compliance liability without consent. Treat the gallery as a slot the client populates with consented images.

claim: Dental: listing accepted insurance providers / 'direct billing' is a high-impact, near-table-stakes conversion signal because cost/coverage is the dominant patient concern; it is frequently underused on existing sites.
notes: Not regulator-mandated, but materially affects conversion. Build default: 'direct billing / insurance accepted' band.

claim: Law: membership in the Law Society of Alberta is the foundational trust credential, and the regulator's Code of Conduct (Chapter 4) requires all marketing to be demonstrably true, accurate and verifiable.
notes: Chapter 4 of the Code governs marketing of legal services and applies to websites, ads, social, directory listings.

claim: Law: the site must avoid prohibited language — 'specialist'/'expert' (no Alberta specialist certification program), superlatives ('best','top','leading'), and advertised settlement/verdict amounts — and use permitted alternatives like 'focuses on' / 'extensive experience in'.
notes: Copy-level constraint baked into template content, not a component. Affects how practice-area headings are worded.

claim: Law: where past results or case outcomes are referenced, a 'past results do not guarantee future outcomes' disclaimer is required, with the qualification that outcomes vary by individual facts.
notes: Conditional must-have: only triggered if the site shows results/testimonials about outcomes. Ship a disclaimer slot near results/testimonials sections.

claim: Auto: an AMVIC (Alberta Motor Vehicle Industry Council) business licence is mandatory to legally operate an automotive repair business in Alberta, and the AMVIC licence number is a must-have trust signal to display.
notes: AMVIC licence is required by law; displaying the number signals legitimacy. Note exception: pure service stations doing only minor work (oil/tire) may not need a technician, and bench work is not AMVIC-licensed.

claim: Auto: technician trade certification (Automotive Service Technician, with optional Red Seal endorsement) is the credibility signal customers look for; AMVIC requires a qualifying trade certificate on file for most repair classes.
notes: Red Seal is the Canadian equivalent of the US ASE badge for marketing purposes; ASE is US-centric and rarely the signal in Alberta. Display 'Red Seal certified technicians' rather than ASE.

claim: Auto: warranty (e.g. nationwide parts-and-labour warranty) and financing options are high-impact conversion signals — table-stakes-adjacent differentiators that reduce perceived risk on a high-cost service.
notes: Not regulatory; pure conversion. Build default: warranty badge + financing mention near CTA.

claim: Trades: proof of WCB Alberta coverage (clearance letter / WCB number) is a must-have trust signal because homeowners are advised to verify it before hiring, and any construction business with workers must register with WCB.
notes: Alberta.ca's official 'Hiring a contractor' guidance tells homeowners to ask for the WCB number and a clearance letter. 'Licensed, insured & WCB-covered' is the canonical trades trust line.

claim: Trades: a 'licensed & insured' statement (public liability + property damage coverage) is a near-universal expected trust signal; Alberta's official guidance tells homeowners to ask for an insurance certificate.
notes: Note: Alberta has no single 'general contractor licence'; trade-specific certs + municipal business licence + insurance + WCB are the real signals. A prepaid contracting licence applies to prepaid contractors specifically.

claim: Fitness: certified-trainer credentials (NCCA-accredited certs such as NASM-CPT) are the primary expertise trust signal, but trainer certification is not provincially licensed/regulated in Alberta — so it is a should-have conversion signal, not a legal requirement.
notes: NCCA accreditation is the recognized credibility marker. Build default: trainer bios with certification logos/letters.

claim: Fitness: a liability waiver / informed-consent process is the operative legal protection (not a website trust badge); the website's role is to surface waiver/intake as part of booking, confirming the client understood exercise risks and participated voluntarily.
notes: Waiver is an operational form, not a public trust signal; affects booking flow (intake step) more than on-page conversion. Niche as a website element.

claim: Spa: estheticians, nail techs and lash artists are NOT provincially licensed in Alberta (only hairstyling is a designated trade), so 'licensed esthetician' is largely a niche/marketing claim rather than a verifiable credential — but AHS personal-services facility approval and (for massage) a Calgary massage practitioner licence ARE real, displayable signals.
notes: Key correction: esthetician licensing is a common assumption that is false in Alberta. Lead with AHS sanitation/infection-control approval + training (e.g. accredited program) as the trust signal instead.

claim: Spa/massage: in Calgary, paid massage requires a massage practitioner's licence (membership in good standing with a recognized association such as MTAA, NHPC, CRMTA) OR a body rub practitioner licence (min. 250 training hours), and the centre/facility is licensed separately — so for massage-offering spas, the practitioner/RMT credential is a must-have, materially conversion-relevant signal.
notes: RMT designation also drives insurance direct-billing for clients (extended health), a real conversion lever. Distinguish 'massage therapy' (RMT, insurable) from 'body rub' clearly to avoid mis-positioning.

claim: Cross-vertical: customer reviews / star ratings are the single highest-impact trust signal across all local-shop verticals and outweigh credential badges for conversion — 86% of consumers cite homepage 5-star ratings/positive reviews as the top signal driving a first purchase.
notes: Confirms the existing audit rubric's 'reviews' item is correctly weighted. Treat as the universal must-have above credential badges.

claim: Cross-vertical: 3-5 relevant, recognizable credential/trust badges is the optimal count; cramming many or irrelevant badges decreases trust — so per-vertical templates should surface only the few governing credentials, not a wall of logos.
notes: Design guardrail: a single 'credentials' band per vertical (regulator + insurance/warranty + cert), not an everything badge strip. Trust-badge studies cite ~10-30% conversion lift but warn against overuse.

claim: Cross-vertical pattern: regulatory/licence display is the 'table-stakes legitimacy' layer (auto=AMVIC, law=Law Society, dental=ADA&C/CDSA, trades=WCB+insurance, massage=Calgary practitioner licence) while reviews, insurance-accepted/direct-billing, warranty, and consented before/after are the 'conversion-moving' layer; esthetics/fitness have NO governing licence so their signals are voluntary certifications.
notes: Synthesis claim defining the two-layer model: a 'governing credential' slot (regulated verticals) + a 'conversion proof' slot (reviews/billing/warranty/before-after) per template. Regulated verticals additionally carry advertising-content constraints (law strongest, dental next).

claim: mobileViewport is a truly universal must-have across all nine verticals: 59-60% of restaurant/dental/local sessions originate on smartphones, and mobile-optimized sites convert materially better (restaurants with mobile optimization capture ~42% more online orders; mobile 'Order Now' buttons lift conversion ~34%). It should carry full weight in every vertical's conversion score.
notes: Current rubric weights this equally with all 12; keep it as a universal full-weight item. Repo already targets static Astro + CWV (LCP<2.5s) which reinforces mobile performance.

claim: https is a universal must-have and functions as a hard trust gate, not just a nice-to-have: a large share of users abandon sites showing a browser 'Not Secure' warning (reported figures range ~64% to ~85%), and HTTP carts can see up to ~40% higher abandonment. Because failure is catastrophic rather than incremental, https should be treated as a near-pass/fail gate in scoring rather than 1/12 of an averaged inventory.
notes: Recommendation: missing https should impose a grade penalty/cap (like the existing cwvPass and structuralFlags caps in rubric.ts) rather than only docking ~8 points. Abandonment stats are vendor blogs; treat exact percentages as directional, but the trust-gate direction is robust.

claim: reviews / social proof is a universal high-impact conversion element: 93% of consumers read reviews before purchasing, 92% require at least a 4-star rating before considering a local business, and services with 5+ reviews show ~270% higher conversion versus none. It deserves full weight in every vertical, with extra weight for trust-sensitive verticals (dental, law, spa, auto).
notes: Northwestern Spiegel finding: 4.2-4.5 stars converts better than a perfect 5.0 (perfection reads as fake to 46% of shoppers). Reviews component (the Reviews.astro rating) is already in the component catalogue; weighting should be highest for dental/law/spa/auto.

claim: clickToCall is a universal must-have but is the PRIMARY conversion action specifically for high-intent, urgent-need verticals (trades, auto, dental). ~70% of calls to local businesses now originate from click-to-call, phone leads convert ~10-12x better than web-form leads, and ~37% of phone leads convert during the call. For trades/auto/dental it should be weighted above generic items.
notes: Higher per-vertical weight for trades, auto, dental, law (urgent/high-ticket, phone-driven). Slightly lower relative weight for retail/cafe where call is not the main action. The repo's refuted '63% of local searches' framing is avoided; this evidence is independent.

claim: hours and addressOrMap (map/directions/NAP) are universal must-haves for any business with a physical location, which is all nine verticals. They are common-but-frequently-incomplete on prospect sites and underpin the 'visit us' decision; keep both as full-weight universal items.
notes: addressOrMap doubles as NAP consistency signal (settled in prior repo research). Both already in component catalogue (Hours.astro, ContactNAP.astro). Keep universal; no vertical needs them dropped.

claim: localBusinessJsonLd is a universal should-have: it is cheap, verified-correct (only name+address strictly required), and supports local-pack/map discovery, but it is an SEO/discovery signal rather than an on-page conversion lever. It belongs in the inventory at modest weight across all verticals; its conversion impact is indirect.
notes: Already settled in repo (seo-day.de). Recommend it counts toward SEO sub-score more than conversion; currently it sits inside the conversion inventory which slightly over-credits conversion. Low risk to leave as-is.

claim: menuSchema (schema.org/Menu) is NOT a universal item: it is meaningful only for cafe and restaurant verticals and is dead weight (always-false, depressing the score for no reason) for barber, spa, trades, fitness, dental, law, auto, and retail. The conversion inventory should be vertical-masked so menuSchema is only scored for cafe/restaurant.
notes: CRITICAL RUBRIC BUG: inventory.ts always evaluates menuSchema, and rubric.ts counts every non-error key, so a perfect barber site is capped because menuSchema=false. The denominator should exclude items not applicable to the vertical. Restaurant template already ships Menu schema per prior repo research.

claim: bookingLink is a universal item but its weight should be vertical-specific: it is a PRIMARY conversion action for appointment-driven verticals (barber, spa, dental, fitness) and near-irrelevant as a distinct signal for retail. ~73% of dental patients book after hours and 70-77% of patients value/prefer online booking, and online reservations lift restaurant website conversion ~19%.
notes: The current inventory detects a booking LINK by keyword. See separate claim recommending an embedded online-booking-WIDGET as a distinct, higher-value missing item for barber/spa/dental/fitness. Weight bookingLink highest for these four; low for retail/law/trades/auto.

claim: MISSING from the inventory: an embedded online-booking WIDGET (Fresha/Booksy/Square Appointments/Calendly iframe or script), distinct from a mere booking LINK, is the single highest-value addition for barber, spa, dental, and fitness. Evidence: 30-35% of salon/spa calls go unanswered; ~73% of dental bookings happen after hours when phones are unstaffed; reminders cut no-shows up to ~50%; studios report ~30% lift in lead conversion with integrated booking.
notes: Detect by iframe/script src to known booking hosts (fresha, booksy, squareup/square.site, calendly, setmore, simplybook, mindbody, acuityscheduling). The repo's settled tooling list (Fresha/Booksy/Square Appointments) maps directly. This is the strongest per-vertical scoring upgrade.

claim: MISSING from the inventory: a quote/estimate request mechanism (estimate form or instant-quote tool) is a high-value item specifically for trades and auto. Contractor websites convert ~10-15% of visitors who provide contact info; online estimates/quotes are framed as the primary trust-and-conversion lever for trades and auto repair, letting customers get a price before committing 24/7.
notes: Today the rubric only sees a generic contactForm (any <form>). A trades/auto-specific quote/estimate form (or instant estimator) should be a separate, higher-weighted item for these two verticals; for them it ranks alongside clickToCall as the primary CTA.

claim: MISSING from the inventory: a visible insurance-accepted / financing / payment-options block is a high-value, vertical-specific item for dental (and useful for some auto/collision). When affordability/insurance acceptance is vague, patient conversion drops and prospects keep searching; clearly listing accepted insurance is described as a critical conversion factor for new-patient sites.
notes: Calgary/Alberta nuance: Canadian dental is direct-billing to private insurers (e.g. Sun Life, Blue Cross) plus the new Canadian Dental Care Plan; 'direct billing accepted' is the local phrasing. Detect by keywords (insurance, direct billing, financing, payment plans). Dental-only weight.

claim: MISSING from the inventory: a class/schedule timetable is a high-value, vertical-specific item for fitness studios/gyms. A live, bookable timetable is the core conversion surface for class-based fitness (members decide by what classes run when), and integrated scheduling/booking platforms report measurable conversion and retention lift.
notes: Overlaps with the booking-widget item but is specific: a schedule grid plus per-class booking. Detect timetable markup or known fitness booking hosts (mindbody, vibefam, gymmaster, classfit, wodify). Fitness-only weight.

claim: MISSING from the inventory: dedicated practice-area pages (and a matching per-area contact/intake CTA) are the highest-value structural element for law-firm sites. Practice-area pages 'matter more than almost any other element' on a legal site, must mirror the client's own problem language, and clear well-placed CTAs on them can lift conversions ~25% (some sources up to ~200%).
notes: Detect via multiple practice-area URLs/nav items plus a consultation CTA/form. For law, contactForm (consultation/intake) and reviews/testimonials carry the most weight; bookingLink and menuSchema are near-irrelevant.

claim: contactForm weight should be vertical-specific, not flat: it is a primary lead-capture action for law (consultation/intake) and a meaningful secondary for trades/auto (quote requests), but it is low-value for cafe/barber/retail where the primary action is call, book, order, or visit. The current flat 1/12 weighting under- and over-credits it depending on vertical.
notes: Current inventory matches any <form> (could be a newsletter signup) — it over-credits. A higher-fidelity check (lead/intake/quote form vs newsletter) plus per-vertical weight would improve scoring. Weight high for law; low for cafe/barber/retail.

claim: ogTags and favicon are universal nice-to-have polish signals (brand/share hygiene), not conversion drivers. They are common-but-missing on prospect sites and useful as cheap audit talking points, but should carry the LOWEST weight in every vertical's conversion score and never gate a grade.
notes: No vertical-specific evidence ties favicon/ogTags to conversion lift; keep them as minor inventory items. They are good 'easy win' items for the outreach audit narrative even though impact is low.

claim: Recommended scoring change: replace the flat 12-item average with (a) a vertical applicability mask (exclude items like menuSchema for non-food verticals so they do not depress the denominator) and (b) per-vertical weights with two universal hard gates (https, mobileViewport). This corrects a current bug where non-applicable items penalize otherwise-strong sites and where catastrophic gates count as only ~8%.
notes: Concrete weighting per vertical (primary CTA items 2x; trust/social-proof full; polish 0.25x): barber/spa -> bookingWidget+reviews+clickToCall primary; dental -> bookingWidget+clickToCall+insurance+reviews; fitness -> classSchedule/bookingWidget+reviews; trades/auto -> clickToCall+quoteForm+reviews; law -> contactForm/intake+practiceAreas+reviews; cafe -> menu/menuSchema+bookingLink(ordering/reservation)+clickToCall; retail -> reviews+addressOrMap+clickToCall (booking/menu masked out). Implement in rubric.ts alongside existing cwvPass/structuralFlags caps.

claim: For dental practices, the dominant 'native to the website' booking pattern is a patient-engagement / practice-management-synced platform (NexHealth being the most cited), which syncs real-time availability with the practice's PMS/EHR and is added to a static site via either a copyable button/embed code or a hosted 'Book Now' direct link — both of which drop cleanly into an Astro site without a backend.
notes: NexHealth offers exactly two website-integration methods per its help center: (1) a Direct Link (copy URL → use as a hosted booking link / button href), and (2) an embedded 'Conversion analytics button and link' code snippet with selectable button color + UTM tracking. Both are static-site-friendly. It also supports Reserve with Google.

claim: NexHealth-class dental booking is a premium, sales-quoted subscription — roughly USD $299–$350+/month, modular per-feature, month-to-month with no long-term contract — so it is justified mainly for established practices, not a default we bundle into a low-cost one-time shop site.
notes: Pricing reported $299–$350/mo starting, tiered Starter/Standard/Pro, quoted after a discovery call. For our one-time-fee model the realistic default is to embed whatever booking link the client's existing PMS provides, or fall back to a simple appointment-request form.

claim: For dental (and clinic-style health/wellness verticals generally), the strongest CANADIAN-specific booking option is Jane App — a Canadian-built practice-management platform whose branded online-booking page can be embedded on the practice website, priced transparently from ~CAD $54/mo (most clinics on the ~$79–$99 tiers).
notes: Jane is dominant among Calgary physio/massage/chiro/multidisciplinary clinics and spas. Booking page is brandable (logo, colours) and embeds on the site. Better CAD pricing fit than NexHealth for our market; relevant to spa and boutique-fitness verticals too.

claim: For law firms, the dominant lead-capture pattern is NOT a third-party booking widget but a short contact/intake form plus prominent click-to-call and a 'Free Consultation / Free Case Review' CTA repeated in the sticky header and mid-page — i.e. capture-first, with scheduling optional.
notes: Best practice is short forms (name, contact, brief description), CTA in top-right header + sticky + mid-page, and outreach speed (5-min follow-up converts far better). Many firms deliberately use no online scheduler because intake/conflict-checking happens by phone. This maps directly to our existing contactForm + clickToCall conversion-inventory items — no third-party embed needed for the baseline build.

claim: When a law firm does want self-serve consult booking, Calendly is the de-facto standard, and it embeds on a static Astro site in three documented ways — inline embed, pop-up widget (floating button), and pop-up text/link — all via copyable embed code available on ALL plans including the free tier (paid Standard ~USD $10/seat/mo adds intake-question fields, reminders, and Clio/MyCase integrations).
notes: Embedding is free-tier-available, so a consult-booking slot costs $0 to add for a single attorney. Calendly's event-type intake questions double as a lightweight intake form. This is the cheap, no-backend scheduling add-on for the law template's optional 'book a consultation' slot.

claim: For auto repair shops, the dominant booking pattern is a capacity-aware shop-scheduling tool (AutoOps is the most cited; Shopmonkey for full shop-management) that embeds a 'Book Online' widget into any website and pushes a 'Book Online' button to the Google Business Profile, with AutoOps around USD $100/month — versus the simpler/default of a quote-request form.
notes: AutoOps advertises 'universal site compatibility' (works with all web hosts), capacity-based routing, upsell of previously-declined work, and Reserve-with-Google. Their bundled website is $100/mo but the scheduler can sit on top of an external site. For our one-time-fee model, the realistic default ships a quote-request form + click-to-call, with an AutoOps/Shopmonkey embed slot if the client already pays for shop-management software.

claim: For fitness studios/gyms, the dominant pattern is an embeddable class/membership booking widget from a fitness-management platform — Mindbody (Starter from USD $99/mo) and Glofox for boutique studios, Wodify (from USD $79/mo) for CrossFit/functional — added to the site by pasting a copyable code snippet that renders the class calendar, appointment booking, and account sign-up in-page.
notes: Mindbody 'branded web tools' explicitly install via 'copying a snippet of text and pasting it' onto Squarespace/Wix/WordPress 'and more' — i.e. a script-snippet widget that works on a static Astro page; transactions occur in-page. Embeddable components: class calendar, appointment booking, staff profiles, account sign-up, contact form, membership/gift-card purchase. Wodify CORE handles class booking/payments (from $79/mo + onboarding fee); Glofox is sales-quoted. The class schedule + membership CTA is effectively mandatory for the fitness vertical.

claim: Across all four verticals, the lowest-cost universal fallback is a Reserve-with-Google / Google Business Profile booking link, which is free from Google (you only pay for the underlying booking provider, if any) and is just a hosted URL — ideal for low-volume shops that don't want a paid widget on the page.
notes: A manual booking URL or a Reserve-with-Google partner button can be added to the GBP for free; Reserve with Google is available in 80+ countries (some integrations/business-type gating applies). On the site itself this collapses to a single hosted link/button — no embed, no backend — matching our static-Astro + one-time-fee constraint.

claim: The embed mechanism for all four verticals collapses to three static-site-compatible primitives — (a) a JS/code-snippet widget rendered in-page (Mindbody/Wodify/NexHealth conversion button, Calendly inline/popup), (b) a hosted booking link/button (NexHealth Direct Link, AutoOps/Reserve-with-Google), or (c) a plain HTML form (law intake, auto quote request) — none of which require server-side code, so every pattern drops into an Astro static build via a content-configurable embed slot.
notes: Architectural takeaway for the shop-site engine: add a per-vertical 'BookingEmbed' content slot accepting either {embedCode html-string} or {bookingUrl hosted-link}, plus the existing form component. Vertical defaults: dental → PMS/Jane embed or link (fallback request form); law → intake form + click-to-call (optional Calendly free embed); auto → quote form + click-to-call (optional AutoOps embed/GBP link); fitness → Mindbody/Wodify class-calendar snippet (mandatory).

claim: A before/after photo gallery is conversion-critical for visual-transformation verticals (med-spa/cosmetic, dental cosmetic, auto body, barber): on aesthetic-practice sites the before/after gallery is the second-most-visited page after the home page, making it a primary decision-making and CRO surface — so the template needs a dedicated paired before/after media slot for these verticals.
notes: ADvance Gallery: before/after gallery is 'the second most visited section, right after the home page' and 'a critical component for Conversion Rate Optimization.' Applies to med-spa, cosmetic dermatology, dental cosmetic, and auto body. For barber/fitness it is portfolio-style rather than clinical before/after.

claim: Authentic, real (non-stock) photography of the actual business, team, and work measurably out-converts stock imagery and is the baseline expectation across every local vertical — driving the template to default all media slots to client-supplied photos, not stock.
notes: DesignWebLocal aggregates: 35% higher landing-page conversion with authentic vs stock imagery (HubSpot), 29% more form completions when real team photos replace stock (Unbounce), 83% greater trust with real business/team/product photos (PhotoShelter), 4.5x higher credibility with actual location+team (Stanford Web Credibility). 'Common-but-missing' because many local sites still ship stock.

claim: A 'Meet the Team' section with professional headshots plus short bios is conversion-critical for high-trust, person-delivered services (dental, law) and strong for fitness, barber, spa: 92% of healthcare seekers read a clinician's bio before booking and 77% rely on visual cues including the headshot to build confidence — so the template needs a team/staff-bio component with a headshot slot for these verticals.
notes: Capturely cites Physicians Weekly (2024): 92% read clinician bio before booking, 77% rely on visual cues. For law, 97% of legal consumers start online and scan attorney bios/photos as a trust signal before calling. Lower priority for trades/auto/retail where the individual practitioner is less central.

claim: Menu/food photography is conversion-critical for cafe/restaurant: items with photos sell measurably more (commonly cited ~+30% on digital menus, up to higher with full photo+description), and ~84% of diners want to see food photos before choosing — so the cafe template's menu/Services component needs per-item image slots, not text-only.
notes: Treat exact percentages as illustrative vendor stats (Square, Restolabs, Grubhub-sourced); direction is well-supported. The repo's restaurant-anatomy research already flags food gallery + online menu; this adds the per-menu-item photo slot as the high-value detail.

claim: A project/job gallery (work organized by service type, ideally before/after with location context) is conversion-critical for trades and auto repair: prospects convert much higher with visual proof of completed work, and a filterable gallery of 40+ real categorized project photos is cited as a conversion lift — so the trades/auto template needs a categorizable project-gallery slot.
notes: Contractor guides: dedicated gallery/portfolio organized by project type, before/after with location context, is a core trust/conversion element. For auto, overlaps with the before/after claim; for trades it is broader job-portfolio evidence of capability.

claim: Facility/interior/ambiance photography is conversion-relevant for experience-and-atmosphere verticals (cafe, spa, fitness, dental): for fitness, high-quality facility/virtual-tour photos raise interest ~19% and clean locker-room/space photos reduce a major pre-commit anxiety; for spa, 72% of clients want before/after and ambiance imagery before booking — so the template should expose an interior/space gallery slot for these.
notes: Travvir: facility photos/virtual tour increase interest ~19%; locker-room/space photos address a top membership anxiety. claim: A portfolio/results gallery of recent cuts, fades and beard work is conversion-important for barbers (lets visitors picture the result and feel confident booking), and pairs naturally with an Instagram feed of real work — so the barber template should ship a portfolio gallery slot rather than relying on a single hero image.

claim: Menu/product photography is conversion-relevant for retail product showcase (real photos of actual products vs stock build trust and drive the buy/visit decision), so the retail template's product showcase should default to real per-product image slots — but it is should-have rather than must-have because catalog-as-foot-traffic sites can convert on a curated subset.
notes: Reuses the authentic-photo trust evidence applied to products. claim: License numbers, certifications/insurance badges, and OEM/affiliation logos are conversion-critical visual trust proof for trades and auto (often more decisive than gallery polish) — distinct from photography, this is a 'credential/badge' media slot the template should support for these verticals.

claim: For law firms, photography emphasis is on professional team/attorney headshots and (secondarily) office/credibility imagery rather than work galleries — there is no before/after or product/menu media need, so the law template should prioritize the team-bio+headshot slot and de-prioritize gallery slots.
notes: Law is the clearest 'no gallery, yes headshots' case: consistent professional attorney headshots across the roster signal stability and cohesion; case results are text/proof, not visual galleries. Confirms media-slot allocation should be vertical-specific, not one-size-fits-all.
